Case Name: League of United Latin American Citizens (LULAC) v. Executive Office of the President(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-00946
Executive Action: Election Law
Case Summary: On March 25, 2025, President Trump signed an Executive Order (EO) titled “Preserving and Protecting the Integrity of American Elections,” which directs the Election Assistance Commission (EAC) to require documentary proof of U.S. citizenship on voter registration forms and conditions federal funding to states based on compliance with the EO. Plaintiffs are three nonprofits, the League of United Latin American Citizens (LULAC), the Secure Families Initiative (SFI), and the Arizona Students’ Association (ASA). They allege that the EO violates the separation of powers, the Electors and Election Clauses of the Constitution, the Uniformed and Overseas Citizens Absentee Voting Act (UOCAVA), and the Administrative Procedure Act (including allegedly making “arbitrary and capricious” changes to the Federal Form and Federal Post Card Application). Plaintiffs request that the Court declare that the EO is unlawful and unconstitutional, enjoin the implementation and enforcement of the EO, and block the Department of Justice (DOJ) and Attorney General Pam Bondi from taking action that would prohibit the counting of valid mail-in and absentee ballots.
Case Updates: April 3, 2025:This case was consolidated withDemocratic National Committee v. TrumpandLeague of Women Voters Education Fund v. Trump. These cases will proceed underLeague of United Latin American Citizens v. Executive Office of the President.April 4, 2025:The nonpartisan plaintiffs filed amotionto clarify the consolidation order to allow them to brief the issues in the case separately from the Democratic Party plaintiffs, and to expedite the hearing and briefing on their motion for a preliminary injunction.April 5, 2025:Judge Colleen Kollar-Kotelly issued anordermodifying the consolidation order to allow the plaintiffs to rule separately.April 7, 2025:Nonpartisan plaintiffs filed amotionfor a preliminary injunction blocking enforcement of the EO. In an accompanyingmemorandum, plaintiffs reassert that the EO violates Constitutional protections for federalism and the separation of powers.April 7, 2025:Democratic Party plaintiffs filed amotionfor a preliminary injunction blocking enforcement of the EO. In an accompanyingmemorandum, plaintiffs reassert that the EO violates Constitutional protections for federalism and the separation of powers.April 24, 2025:Judge Kollar-Kotelly granted apreliminary injunction, with the exception of the Democracy Party Plaintiffs’ motions as to Sections 2(b), 7(a), and 7(b) of the EO.May 31, 2025:DefendantsansweredLULAC’s complaint. On June 1, Defendantsansweredthe Democratic National Committee’s (DNC) complaint. On June 7, Defendantsansweredthe League of Women Voters’ complaint. Additionally, RNC’s answer to League of Women Voters alleged that the League of Women Voters lacked standing to bring forth their claims.Aug. 28, 2025:Judge Kollar-KotellydeniedDefendants’ motion to strike, deny, or defer consideration of Plaintiff’s motion for partial summary judgment.Sept. 17, 2025:Democratic Party Plaintiffs and Defendants filed ajoint stipulation of partial dismissalof claims challenging Sections 4(b), 4(c), and 4(d) of EO 14248. This includes ultra vires claims as to Sections 4(b) and 4(c) in Count 1, Plaintiffs’ separation of powers/unlawful intrusion upon congressional authority claims as to Sections 4(b) and 4(c) in Count II, Plaintiffs’ separation of powers/usurpation of Congress’s spending powers claim as to Section 4(d) in Count IV and Plaintiffs’ APA claims as to Section 4(b) and 4(c) in count XI.Oct. 31, 2025:District Judge Kollar-KotellygrantedPlaintiffs’ motion for partial summary judgment on their constitutional separation of powers claims and issued a permanent injunction against the administration blocking Section 2(a) ofExecutive Order 14248, which directs the Election Assistance Commission to require documentary proof of U.S. citizenship to apply for mail-in-voting. The court noted that Congress has never assigned nor intended to assign any responsibility with respect to mail-in-voting to the President or Executive Branch with the power to act unilaterally.Dec. 23, 2025:Defendants filednotice of appeal.Jan. 30, 2026:Judge Kollar-Kotelly issued amemorandum opiniongranting in part, denying in part, and deferring in part cross-motions for summary judgment by both Plaintiffs and the government, and dismissing certain claims without prejudice. The order declared that Sections 2(d) and 3(d) of the EO, which impose new requirements for verifying U.S. citizenship of people registering to vote or applying for absentee ballots, are unconstitutional and permanently enjoined their implementation. The court found Plaintiffs’ claims regarding Sections 4(a), 7(a), and 7(b) are not ripe. As to Sections 2(b) and 3(a), the court dismissed as moot Plaintiffs’ request for injunctive relief against SAVE program changes, but granted declaratory relief requiring federal agencies to comply with the Privacy Act when implementing data-sharing provisions. Remaining claims proceed to further proceedings.

Case Name: Democratic National Committee v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-00952
Executive Action: Election Law
Case Summary: On March 25, 2025, President Trump signed an Executive Order (EO) titled “Preserving and Protecting the Integrity of American Elections.” Among its requirements, the EO directs the Election Assistance Commission (EAC) to require documentary proof of U.S. citizenship on voter registration forms and provides that the Department of Homeland Security and DOGE shall review each state’s voter registration lists. The EO also directs the Attorney General to take action against states that count absentee or mail-in ballots received after Election Day, and it conditions various federal funding on compliance with the Executive Order. The Democratic National Committee (DNC), Democratic Governors Association, Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee (DSCC), Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee (DCCC), U.S. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer, and U.S. House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries challenged the EO, alleging that the President does not have legal authority to impose these policy changes, and that the Constitution reserves such authority to the states and to Congress. They further allege that the EO will make it harder to register to vote, cast a ballot, and to administer fair elections, and that it violates individual privacy rights. Plaintiffs brought 11 counts, alleging (1) Ultra Vires presidential action; (2) Violation of the Separation of Powers with respect to various congressional and state authorities; (3) Administrative Procedure Act (APA) violations with respect to various statutes; and (4) APA violations with respect to various constitutional provisions. Plaintiffs requested that the court declare the relevant sections of the EO unlawful and preliminarily and permanently enjoin the various government departments from taking any action to implement the EO.
Case Updates: April 3, 2025:This case was consolidated withLeague of Latin American Citizens v. Executive Office of the PresidentandLeague of Women Voters Education Fund v. Trump. These cases will proceed underLeague of United Latin American Citizens v. Executive Office of the President.

Case Name: League of Women Voters Education Fund v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-00955
Executive Action: Election Law
Case Summary: On March 25, 2025, President Donald Trump signed an Executive Order (EO) titled “Preserving and Protecting the Integrity of American Elections,” which directs the Election Assistance Commission (EAC) to require documentary proof of U.S. citizenship on the federal voter registration form (“Federal Form”) and conditions federal funding to states based on compliance with the EO. Plaintiffs are seven voting rights organizations, including multiple branches of the League of Women Voters (LMV), the Hispanic Federation, and the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP). They allege that the EO violates the separation of powers and the National Voter Registration Act. Plaintiffs request that the Court declare that the EO is unlawful and enjoin the implementation of the EO to the extent that it directs the EAC to change the Federal Form.

Case Name: State of California v. Trump(D. Mass.)1:25-cv-10810
Executive Action: Election Law
Case Summary: On March 25, 2025, President Donald Trump signed an Executive Order (EO) titled “Preserving and Protecting the Integrity of American Elections.” Among its requirements, the EO directs the Election Assistance Commission (EAC) to require documentary proof of U.S. citizenship on voter registration forms and provides that the Department of Homeland Security and Department of Government Efficiency shall review each state’s voter registration lists. The EO also directs the Attorney General to take action against states that count absentee or mail-in ballots received after Election Day, and it conditions various federal funding on compliance with the EO. Plaintiffs are 19 States that allege the President lacks authority to make the proposed changes to election law, that the EO infringes on the authority of States and Congress to determine election law, and that the EO would impose significant burden on the States to begin immediately conforming to the proposed new policy changes. Plaintiffs challenged six provisions of the EO: (1) Section 2(a), which requires the EAC to require documentary proof of citizenship in its national mail voter registration form; (2) Section 2(d), which requires Federal voter registration departments or agencies to assess citizenship prior to providing a Federal voter registration form to enrollees of public assistance programs; (3) Section 3(d), which requires the Secretary of Defense to require documentary proof of citizenship in the Federal Post Card Application pursuant to the Uniformed and Overseas Citizen Voting Absentee Voting Act; (4) Section 4(a), which requires the EAC to freeze funds to States that do not implement these documentary requirements; (5) Section 7(a), which directs the Attorney General to enforce a new statutory interpretation to disallow counting of ballots received after Election Day; and (6) Section 7(b), which directs the EAC to condition funding to States based on adherence to this interpretation of Election Day. Plaintiffs allege that the President does not have authority to implement these changes to election law. Specifically, they argue that these provisions of the EO are unconstitutional under the Bicameralism, Presentment, Elections, and Electors Clauses (depending on the provisions), ultra vires, in violation of the National Voter Registration Act, violate Separation of Powers, and violate State sovereignty. Plaintiffs have requested the court declare the challenged provisions unconstitutional and void, and to preliminarily and permanently enjoin all Defendants except Trump from implementing or enforcing the challenged provisions.

Trump; and (3)League of Women Voters Education Fund v. Trump. The motion seeks relief for five of the six challenged provisions of the EO, including sections 2(a), 2(d), 3(d), 7(a), and 7(b).Plaintiffs argue that these provisions violate constitutional separation of powers, unconstitutionally impose extra-statutory conditions on congressionally appropriated funding, and unlawfully invade State sovereignty.May 7, 2025:Plaintiffs filed amotion in oppositionto Defendants’ motion to transfer and consolidate or in the alternative to stay, arguing that there is a strong presumption in favor of Plaintiffs’ choice of forum, that the Plaintiff States have distinct interests and harms from the private plaintiffs in the DDC cases, and that they therefore face severe prejudice if not allowed to litigate on their own timeline.May 9, 2025:The courtdeniedthe transfer, stating that “the differences between the matters counsel against consolidation”, that the interests of justice do not favor transfer, and that a stay is not warranted.June 13, 2025:Judge Casper issued apreliminary injunctionblocking the administration from enforcing key parts of the administration’s electionExecutive Order, which requires states to demand proof of citizenship for voter registration, imposes new requirements on public assistance agencies, and penalizes states for ballot receipt deadlines and ballot curing practices. The court found that the coalition of states suing the government showed a “substantial risk” that their citizens would be disenfranchised.July 18, 2025:Judge Casper issued anamended preliminary injunctionfollowing the Supreme Court’s decision inTrump v. CASAlimiting the use of nationwide injunctions. The amended order continues to block federal agents from enforcing key aspects of the electionExecutive Orderin Plaintiff States, but no longer applies to all States. The amended order also continues to block federal officials from engaging in national enforcement actions that cannot be limited to non-Plaintiff States, such as updating the federal postcard application for all uniformed and overseas voters.July 31, 2025:Defendantsappealedthe amended preliminary injunction order to the First Circuit.Sept. 17, 2025:District Court Judge Casperdeniedthe administration's motion to dismiss 19 states’ challengeE.O. 14248, which requires documentary proof of citizenship for federal voter registration programs and conditions Help America Vote Act funding on states adopting Election Day ballot receipt deadlines. The court held the states have standing, allowing a priorpreliminary injunctionto remain in effect and allowing plaintiff states to proceed with their other causes of action.Oct. 15, 2025:The district courtgrantedDefendants’ motion to stay all deadlines due to lapse in appropriations, but limited the stay to expire on Oct. 31. On Oct. 20, in response to defendants’ motion for clarification, the district courtconfirmedthat the stay ends on Oct. 31, and no other relief was granted.Nov. 24, 2025:The First Circuitliftedits Oct. 14stayof proceedings entered during the government shutdown.

Case Name: State of Washington v. Trump(W.D. Wash.)2:25-cv-00602
Executive Action: Election Law
Case Summary: On March 25, 2025, President Trump signed an Executive Order (EO) titled “Preserving and Protecting the Integrity of American Elections.” Among its requirements, the EO directs the Election Assistance Commission (EAC) to require documentary proof of U.S. citizenship on voter registration forms and provides that the Department of Homeland Security and Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) shall review each state’s voter registration lists. The EO also directs the Attorney General to take action against states that count absentee or mail-in ballots received after Election Day, and it conditions various federal funding on compliance with the EO.Plaintiffs are the State of Washington and the State of Oregon. They are challenging ten provisions of the EO: (1) Section 2(a), which directs the EAC to require documentary proof of citizenship in its national mail voter registration form; (2) Section 2(b)(iii), which directs the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and DOGE to review each state’s public voter registration list against federal immigration databases; (3) Section 2(d), which requires Federal voter registration departments or agencies to assess citizenship prior to providing a voter registration form to enrollees of public assistance programs; (4) Section 2(e)(ii), which directs the U.S. Attorney General to enforce federal election laws by using state-issued identification records and driver’s license databases; (5) Section 3(d), which requires the Secretary of Defense to require documentary proof of citizenship for overseas voting; (6) Section 4(a), which directs the EAC to freeze funds to States that do not implement these documentary requirements; (7) Section 4(b), which sets guidelines for voting systems and directs the EAC to review and re-certify voting systems; (8) Section 4(d), which directs DHS and FEMA to consider withholding federal funding based on states’ compliance with the voting system guidelines; (9) Section 5(b), which directs the the U.S. Attorney General to enforce Federal election integrity laws in the states and potentially withhold federal funding to states based on compliance; and (10) Section 7, which directs the Attorney General to take enforcement actions against any states found not in compliance with the EO and the EAC to condition federal funding to states based on their compliance. Plaintiffs allege that the challenged provisions violate the Elections Clause of the Constitution, the Tenth Amendment (powers reserved to the states), the constitutional right to vote, ultra vires and separation of powers principles, the Take Care Clause of the Constitution, and the National Voter Registration Act. Plaintiffs are requesting that the Court declare that these provisions of the EO are unlawful and that the President does not have authority to implement these changes to election law. Plaintiffs are also requesting both a preliminary and a permanent injunction to prevent Defendants from implementing or enforcing these provisions of the EO.

Case Name: League of Women Voters of the United States v. U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services(D. Md.)8:25-cv-03777
Executive Action: Election Law
Case Summary: On Aug. 29, 2025, the U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) issued apolicy alertrevising its longstanding rules for voter registration at administrative naturalization ceremonies, barring nongovernmental organizations from distributing or collecting voter registration applications or providing any voter registration or education services at such ceremonies. USCIS stated the change was consistent withExecutive Order 14148andSection 9 of Executive Order 14248, citing an administrative burden in verifying the nonpartisanship of participating organizations, though the policy alert identifies no specific compliance failures under the prior rules. The League of Women Voters — a nonpartisan nonprofit that, by its account, had registered more than 37,000 new citizens at naturalization ceremonies in 2022 alone — filed acomplaintalleging the policy change is a legislative rule issued without the notice-and-comment procedures required by the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), 5 U.S.C. § 553, and is arbitrary and capricious under 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A), for relying on unsupported factual assertions and failing to consider reliance interests and reasonable alternatives. Plaintiffs further allege the ban violates the First Amendment as a content-, viewpoint-, and speaker-based restriction on core political speech and association, and constitutes agency action contrary to constitutional right under 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(B) and (D). Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief vacating the policy and enjoining Defendants from refusing to disclose the schedule and location of administrative naturalization ceremonies.

Case Name: DSCC v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01114
Executive Action: Election Law
Case Summary: On March 31, 2026, President Donald Trump issued an executive order (“EO”) titled “Ensuring Citizenship Verification and Integrity in Federal Elections,” which directs federal agencies to compile state-specific lists of U.S. citizens over age 18 — drawn from Social Security Administration records, DHS’s SAVE database, and other federal databases — and transmit those lists to state election officials no fewer than 60 days before each federal election. The order also directs the Postmaster General to create a "Mail-In and Absentee Participation List" and prohibit USPS from transmitting any mail-in or absentee ballot from a voter not enrolled on that list.On April 1, 2026, the Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee, the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee, Democratic National Committee, Democratic Governors Association, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer, and House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffrieschallengedthe EO in D.C. federal court. Plaintiffs claim that the EO will disenfranchise eligible voters, particularly Democratic-leaning voters who rely disproportionately on mail voting, and force Plaintiffs to divert resources to counteract its effects. Plaintiffs allege that, while Congress and states have explicit authority to regulate elections, the President has no such role, rendering the EO anultra virespresidential action, and a violation of separation of powers under the Elections Clause and the Tenth Amendment. Plaintiffs claim that the EO directs the USPS to impose obligations on state election procedures, compile a list of mail-in voters, and refuse to mail ballots from voters not on that list, none of which Plaintiffs claim the agency is authorized to do by law. Plaintiffs also argue that the EO violates the Privacy Act and the Administrative Procedure Act by using a “computerized matching program” without legal authority, disclosing federal data outside the scope of permissible “uses” as defined by the Privacy Act, and by collecting data from a patchwork of sources that "guarantee inaccuracies” and outdated information in violation of the Act’s substantive provisions. Plaintiffs allege that the EO burdens the right to vote in violation of the First and Fifth Amendments; and conflicts with the Voting Rights Act’s provisions on absentee voting. Plaintiffs seek declaratory judgments that key sections of the order are unlawful and preliminary and permanent injunctive relief against their implementation.

The order also directs the Postmaster General to create a “Mail-In and Absentee Participation List” and prohibit USPS from transmitting any mail-in or absentee ballot from a voter not enrolled on that list.On April 2, 2026, the League of United Latin American Citizens (LULAC), Secure Families Initiative, and Arizona Students’ Association filed acomplaintalleging that their members — including military and overseas voters, recently naturalized citizens, elderly voters, and students — will be wrongly excluded from the citizenship lists or the USPS participation list, preventing them from casting mail ballots. Case Name: NAACP v. Donald J. Trump(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01151
Executive Action: Election Law
Case Summary: On March 31, 2026, President Donald Trump issued an executive order (EO) entitled “Ensuring Citizenship Verification and Integrity in Federal Elections,” directing the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) to compile and transmit to each state a list of residents confirmed to be U.S. citizens eligible to vote (the “State Citizenship List”), derived from federal citizenship and naturalization records, Social Security Administration records, andSAVE databasedata. The order further directs the United States Postal Service (USPS) to initiate rulemaking within 60 days establishing requirements for mail-in and absentee ballots, including prohibiting delivery of mail-in ballots to any individual not enrolled on a federally compiled “Mail-In and Absentee Participation List,” and instructs the Attorney General to prioritize investigation and prosecution of state and local officials and others who provide ballots to individuals the administration deems ineligible.On April 3, 2026, a coalition of civil rights and voting rights organizations, including The National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP), Common Cause, and the Black Voters Matter Fund, filed acomplaintalleging that the order is ultra vires and unconstitutional and that the underlying federal databases are inaccurate and will erroneously exclude eligible voters. Plaintiffs allege that the order exceeds the authority the Constitution assigns to the President over federal election administration, violating the separation of powers under the Elections Clause and Qualifications Clause; violates the Tenth Amendment by conditioning ballot transmission on compliance with federally prescribed procedures in areas the Constitution reserves to states; burdens the right to vote in violation of the First and Fourteenth Amendments by interposing a federal eligibility-verification layer built on allegedly error-prone databases; exceeds the Postal Service’s statutory authority by directing USPS rulemaking outside the governance structure Congress established; and directs disclosure of SSA and DHS records without a published system-of-records notice or applicable routine use, in violation of the Privacy Act as enforced through the APA, 5 U.S.C. § 706(2). Plaintiffs seek declaratory judgments that the challenged provisions are unlawful and a preliminary and permanent injunction against implementation.

Case Name: State of California v. Trump(D. Mass.)1:26-cv-11581
Executive Action: Election Law
Case Summary: On March 31, 2026, President Donald Trump issued an executive order (EO) entitled “Ensuring Citizenship Verification and Integrity in Federal Elections,” directing federal agencies to create what Plaintiffs describe as “shadow voter eligibility lists” that state and local entities and officials must use or face federal investigation, prosecution, or funding cuts. The EO requires the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) to compile "State Citizenship Lists" of eligible voters from federal databases and transmit them to states before federal elections. It also directs the U.S. Postal Service (USPS) to maintain “Mail-In and Absentee Participation Lists” and to refuse delivery of completed mail ballots from voters not appearing on those lists. Furthermore, it instructs states to preserve election records for five years.On April 3, 2026, 23 state attorneys general and the Governor of Pennsylvania filed acomplaintalleging that the EO violates the separation of powers and the Elections and Electors Clauses by usurping authority reserved to the states and Congress over federal election administration. The complaint also alleged the EO unconstitutionally commandeers state elections officials and conflicts with the National Voter Registration Act, violates Congress's plenary authority over the postal system, and the Postal Reorganization Act’s insulation of USPS from presidential direction; and that all three challenged provisions exceed the President's constitutional authority underYoungstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer. Plaintiffs allege imminent harm from voter disenfranchisement, disruption to ongoing 2026 election preparations, and the threat of criminal prosecution of state elections officials, among others. Plaintiffs seek declaratory judgments that the EO’s challenged provisions are unconstitutional and void, and preliminary and permanent injunctive relief to block enforcement of the EO, and attorneys’ fees.

Case Name: State of Louisiana v. U.S. Election Assistance Commission(W.D. La.)3:26-cv-01191
Executive Action: Election Law
Case Summary: In 2024, Louisiana enacted Act 500, which took effect on Jan. 1, 2025, requiring voter registration applicants to provide information sufficient to allow state election officials to verify United States citizenship. Louisiana submitted a corresponding request to the United States Election Assistance Commission (EAC), seeking modification of the Louisiana-specific instructions on the National Mail Voter Registration Form, which states are required to accept under the National Voter Registration Act (NVRA). The EAC deadlocked 2–2 on the request and issued a final denial on Jan. 16, 2026, concluding that Louisiana had not shown the additional information was “necessary” under 52 U.S.C. § 20508(b)(1).The State of Louisiana and Secretary of State Nancy Landry filed acomplaintalleging that the EAC’s denial constitutes agency action unlawfully withheld under APA § 706(1); that the denial was arbitrary, capricious, and contrary to law under APA § 706(2)(A), including because the EAC had previously approved similar Louisiana-specific instructions without controversy; and that the EAC acted in excess of its statutory authority under APA § 706(2)(C). Plaintiffs further allege that 52 U.S.C. § 20508 is unconstitutional as applied, arguing that Congress may not delegate to a federal agency the power to veto a state’s determination of what voter eligibility information it requires, a power reserved to the states under the House Qualifications Clause, U.S. Const. art. I, § 2, cl. 1. Plaintiffs seek vacatur of the EAC’s decision, a declaratory judgment that § 20508 (NVRA) is unconstitutional as applied by the EAC or as applied to Louisiana to the extent it vests the EAC with discretionary authority to refuse to modify the State-specific instructions of the Federal Form, and injunctive relief prohibiting the EAC from blocking implementation of Louisiana’s voter registration law.

Case Name: Common Cause v. U.S. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01352
Executive Action: Election Law
Case Summary: On March 25, 2025, President Donald Trump issuedExecutive Order 14248directing the Attorney General to prioritize enforcement of federal election-integrity laws and to consider withholding federal funds from states who refuse to cooperate in election crime investigations. Pursuant to that order and broader administration policy, the Justice Department has sought to federalize voter list maintenance by demanding that nearly every state and the District of Columbia provide their unredacted statewide voter registration lists — including Social Security numbers, driver’s license numbers, dates of birth, home addresses, and party affiliation — for compilation in a national voter registration system of records. DOJ has allegedly sued 30 states and the District of Columbia to compel compliance; at least 12 states have reportedly acquiesced. DOJ allegedly plans to run the collected data through theDepartment of Homeland Security’s SAVE system– a database that was designed to help states verify the citizenship and immigration status of people applying for government benefits but that allegedly produces false non-citizen identifications at a significant rate – and to require states to remove from their rolls any voters DOJ identifies as “ineligible.”On April 21, 2026, Common Cause, the Common Cause Education Fund, and four individual registered voters in Texas and Nebraska whose data was allegedly disclosed to DOJ without their consent filed acomplaintalleging that DOJ’s policy exceeds its statutory authority and violates the Constitution’s allocation of election administration to the states, the Elections Clause, the Voter Qualifications Clause, and the Seventeenth Amendment. Plaintiffs further allege that the policy violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) and is arbitrary and capricious and contrary to law by failing to comply with the Privacy Act and Paperwork Reduction Act. Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief that the Policy and associated actions are unlawful, vacatur and injunctive relief halting DOJ’s compilation and disclosure of voter data, and an order requiring DOJ to delete data already collected and to instruct recipients to do the same.

Case Name: Burnley v. United States(D. Mass.)1:26-cv-10364
Executive Action: Extrajudicial Killings
Case Summary: Coming soon.On Jan. 27, 2026, the family of two Trinidadian men killed in a Oct. 2025 U.S. missilestrikeon a small boat off the coast of Venezuelafileda wrongful death suit against the administration. Plaintiffs argue that the strike violated international and federal law against extrajudicial killings and seek compensation for the resulting deaths.

Case Name: National Council of Jewish Women v. Citizenship and Immigration Services(D. Md.)8:25-cv-3675
Executive Action: Changes to Voter Registration Guidance for Naturalized Citizens
Case Summary: On Aug. 29, 2025, U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (“USCIS”) issued a guidance document announcing that non-governmental entities are not permitted to provide voter registration services. The plaintiff, National Council of Jewish Women, is an advocacy organization that regularly provided voter registration assistance to newly eligible voters: those who had recently turned 18 and naturalized citizens. On Nov. 7, 2025, the plaintiff sued USCIS and the Department of Homeland Security over the August USCIS decision banning private voter assistance services. The plaintiff argued that the ban was motivated by discriminatory intent, violating the Fifth Amendment. The plaintiff further argued the ban violated the First Amendment in limiting the right to free expression and association by discouraging voter participation. Finally, the plaintiff alleged the ban was arbitrary and capricious and violated the Administrative Procedure Act (APA). The plaintiff requested that the court find that the voter assistance ban violated the First and Fifth Amendments and the APA, and to temporarily block and permanently prevent Defendants from enforcing the ban.

Case Name: National Association of Immigration Judges v. Sirce E. Owen(E.D. Va.)1:20-cv-00731
Executive Action: Government Employees' Speech
Case Summary: In July 2020, the National Association of Immigration Judges (NAIJ) filed suit in the Eastern District of Virginia to challenge an employee policy that requires immigration judges to obtain permission before speaking publicly on issues relating to immigration. The NAIJ argued that the policy violates the First and Fifth Amendment rights of its members. The District Court dismissed the case for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, concluding that the policy could only be challenged through the administrative procedures established by the Civil Service Reform Act (CSRA).The Plaintiffs appealed the dismissal, and on June 3, the Fourth Circuitvacated the dismissal and remandedthe case to the district court. The Court held that although the CSRA’s administrative scheme would normally be the appropriate venue for this case, the CSRA “requires a strong and independent Merit Systems Protections Board (MSPB) and Special Counsel” and stated that the principle that “functioning and independent bodies would receive, review, and decide in the first instance challenges to adverse personnel actions affecting covered federal employees has recently been called into question.” In particular, the panel noted that “the Special Counsel and several members of the MSPB have been terminated by the President and the Government has questioned the constitutionality of the removal protections enshrined in the CSRA.”The Court vacated the case and remanded so that the District Court can evaluate whether the CSRA continues to function as Congress intended in order to determine whether it would be appropriate to strip jurisdiction from the district court.
Case Updates: Dec. 5, 2025:Chief Justice John Robertsplaced a holdon the Fourth Circuit’s ruling in the case. The Circuit had overturned a District Court’s dismissal of a challenge to the administration’s policy requiring that immigration judges obtain permission before speaking publicly on issues relating to immigration.Dec. 19, 2025:The Supreme Courtdeclinedto stay the Fourth Circuit’s ruling, finding that the government had not demonstrated that it would otherwise suffer irreparable harm.

Case Name: American Federation of Government Employees, AFL-CIO v. Kupor(D. Mass.)1:25-cv-13305
Executive Action: Government Employees' Speech
Case Summary: In May 2025, the White House and Office of Personnel Management (“OPM”) implemented the “Merit Hiring Plan,” which includes open-ended essay questions in federal civil service job applications, one of which asks prospective employees how they would help advance the President’s Executive Orders and policy priorities. In June 2025, OPM issued updated guidance that states that the essay questions are not mandatory for applicants to answer but are encouraged. On Nov. 6, the American Federation of Government Employees (“AFGE”), the American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees (“AFSCME”), and the National Association of Government Employees (“NAGE”) sued OPM and its Director Scott Kupor and the United States of America to challenge the inclusion of this question. Plaintiffs argue that the question violates the First Amendment because it functions as viewpoint discrimination that compels speech pertaining to President Trump’s policy priorities and chills speech critical of those policy priorities, as applicants would feel pressured to self-censor and instead give an answer that aligned with the current president’s views in order to remain competitive. Plaintiffs also argue that the question and guidance violate the Privacy Act and are arbitrary and capricious, in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (“APA”). They further argue that the question’s infringement of First Amendment rights also violates the APA. Plaintiffs request the court find the use of the question to be unlawful, and to both temporarily block and permanently prevent Defendants from using the question in civil service employment, from relying on answers to the question, and from retaining applicants’ answers to the question.

Case Name: Does v. Patel(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04258
Executive Action: Government Employees' Speech
Case Summary: On Dec. 8, 2025, 12 anonymous former Federal Bureau of Investigations (FBI) agents sued several federal agencies and offices, including the FBI and the Department of Justice (DOJ), alleging they were demoted or fired in retaliation for kneeling during a 2020 protest as a de-escalation tactic, conduct that the administration has characterized as participating in the demonstration. Plaintiffs allege their firing was politically motivated, and violated their First and Fifth Amendment rights. The lawsuit alleges that Defendant Kashyap Patel, Director of the FBI, circumvented established FBI procedures in terminating the Plaintiffs. Plaintiffs allege Defendants’ actions violate their First Amendment right since they constitute retaliation for perceived political affiliation and their Fifth Amendment substantive and procedural due process rights. They also allege that Defendants violated the Separation of Powers and acted ultra vires. Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief, backpay, and reinstatement to their positions, and ask the court to enjoin Defendants from taking any further adverse personnel action against each of them.
Case Updates: Feb. 27, 2026:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaintalleging further facts to support their claims, including allegations regarding FBI internal policies limiting summary dismissal to compelling or exigent circumstances and protecting employees’ rights to political non-association.

Case Name: Joslin v. U.S. Department of the Interior(E.D. Cal.)1:26-cv-00576
Executive Action: Government Employees' Speech
Case Summary: Coming soon.On Feb. 23, 2026, Dr. Shannon Joslin, a former park ranger with the National Park Service (NPS), filed acomplaintagainst NPS, the Department of the Interior (DOI), the Department of Justice (DOJ), and the U.S. Attorney’s Office for the Eastern District of California, alleging violations of the First Amendment and the Privacy Act arising from her termination and subsequent criminal investigation for displaying a trans pride flag from the El Capitan rock formation in Yosemite National Park while off duty. Plaintiff seeks declaratory and injunctive relief and damages.
Case Updates: March 11, 2026:Judge Sooknanantransferredthe case to the Eastern District of California.

Case Name: Aaron v. Bondi(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04250
Executive Action: Retaliation Against Protected Speech
Case Summary: The app ICEBlock, on which users can report sightings of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents, was created in early 2025 in response to the Trump Administration’s campaign to arrest, detain, and deport immigrants. In October, Attorney General Pam Bondi remarked on Fox News that the Trump Administration had reached out to Apple requesting the removal of ICEBlock from their App Store, which Apple subsequently did. On December 8, the creators of ICEBlock sued the administration to challenge the removal of the app. Plaintiffs allege that the administration illegally threatened to prosecute the app’s creators and pressured Apple to remove the app from its App Store, which the creators claim had more than one million users. Plaintiffs allege that government officials have unlawfully threatened to criminally investigate and prosecute the app’s creator, Joshua Aaron, for his role in developing the app. Plaintiffs claim these threats, along with the administration’s pressuring of Apple into removing the app, constitute violations of their First Amendment rights. Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief and ask that the court permanently enjoin Defendants from demanding Apple to stop distribution of the App, and from investigating or prosecuting Aaron.

Case Name: Jeanne M. Marrazzo v. Robert F. Kennedy Jr.(D. Md.)1:25-cv-04144
Executive Action: Retaliation Against Protected Speech
Case Summary: On Dec. 16, 2025, Jeanne Marrazzo, former National Institutes of Health (NIH) Director of the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases, filed acomplaintagainst Robert F. Kennedy and several other Department of Health and Human Services officials for removing Marrazzo from her position following her objections to new NIH policies. After being placed on administrative leave, Dr. Marrazzo filed a whistleblower retaliation complaint and a disclosure of government wrongdoing with the U.S. Office of Special Counsel (OSC) in September 2025, and was terminated three weeks later. Plaintiff alleges that her termination is a violation of the Civil Service Reform Act of 1978 (CSRA), the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment, the First Amendment as retaliation for protected speech, the Whistleblower Protection Act (WPA), 5 U.S.C. § 2302(b) as illegal retaliation for engaging in protected activity, and the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as arbitrary and capricious and contrary to constitutional rights. She seeks declaratory relief, backpay, and reinstatement to her position and asks the court to enjoin Defendants from taking any further adverse personnel action against her.

Case Name: Tincher v. Noem(D. Minn.)0:25-cv-04669
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Executive Action: Retaliation Against Protected Speech
Case Summary: During the summer of 2025, the administration undertook a nationwide campaign of increased federal immigration enforcement. Federal law enforcement officers, including Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents, were dispatched to various cities as part of the administration’s increased efforts to deport individuals from the United States. On Dec. 4, 2025, ICE agents began “Operation Metro Surge,” a targeted enforcement effort in Minnesota. Minnesota residents protested the agents’ actions. ICE agents allegedly responded with unlawful seizures and arrests, threats and intimidation tactics, and physical assault of observers and protestors. On Dec. 17, 2025, a group of Minnesota residents filed a class actioncomplaintagainst DHS Secretary Kristi Noem and several other DHS officials for ICE officers’ actions during the protests. Plaintiffs allege that Defendants violated their First Amendment by retaliating against protesters and using threats and excessive force to chill protected speech, assembly, and newsgathering rights. Plaintiffs also allege that Defendants violated the Fourth Amendment by unlawfully detaining Plaintiffs and using excessive force without probable cause. Plaintiffs further argue that Defendants engaged in a civil conspiracy to deprive Plaintiffs of their constitutional rights. Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief, a permanent injunction barring Defendants from engaging in unconstitutional conduct and retaliation against class members, and expungement of any records pertaining to Plaintiffs created by Defendants during the course of Operation Metro Surge.
Case Updates: Dec. 19, 2025:At a status conference, the courtconvertedPlaintiffs' Motion for a Temporary Restraining Order into a Motion for a Preliminary Injunction.Jan. 16, 2026:Judge MenendezgrantedPlaintiffs’ motion in part,prohibitingfederal agents from arresting, threatening, or using force against individuals solely for observing, recording, or verbally criticizing immigration enforcement activity, absent probable cause or a lawful order to disperse. The court also restricted the use of chemical irritants and other force against non-violent observers and imposed limits on vehicle stops.Jan. 19, 2026:Defendantsappealedto the Eight Circuit.Jan 21, 2026:The Eighth Circuit issued anadministrative stayof Judge Menendez’s preliminary injunction, temporarily lifting the restrictions on ICE's use of force against protesters.Jan. 24, 2026:Plaintiffs filed a renewedemergency motionasking the Eighth Circuit to immediately lift its Jan. 21 administrative stay of the district court’s injunction governing “Operation Metro Surge,” citing events including the killing of Alex Pretti by federal agents and “escalating, imminent risks” of irreparable harm to protesters and observers.Jan. 26, 2026:The Eighth Circuitstayedthe lower court’s preliminary injunction restricting ICE officers’ actions during protests and denied Plaintiffs’ emergency motion to lift the administrative stay, finding that the injunction was overbroad and vague. The panel granted Plaintiffs’ request for expedited merits briefing.Feb. 13, 2026:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaintexpanding their challenge to DHS’s “Operation Metro Surge,” now supported by over 100 sworn declarations that “tell a story of widespread cruel, immoral, and unconstitutional conduct by DHS agents.”Feb. 19, 2026:The Eight Circuitstayedthe appeal proceedings pending an indicative ruling by the district court on a motion to dissolve the preliminary injunction, following the reported end of Operation Metro Surge in Minneapolis.March 5, 2026:Judge Katherine Menendez issuedan indicative rulingstating that, if given the jurisdiction to do so, the court would dissolve the Jan. 16, 2026, preliminary injunction as moot if the Eight Circuit remands for that purpose.March 12, 2026:The Eighth Circuitremandedthe case to the district court for the limited purpose of ruling on Plaintiffs’ motion to dissolve the preliminary injunction, directing the parties to seek appropriate relief from the appellate court within fourteen days of the district court's ruling.April 8, 2026:The courtdissolvedthe Jan. 16, 2026 preliminary injunction as moot following Operation Metro Surge’s official conclusion, rejecting Defendants’ arguments that the injunction remained operative as to “related operations” or ongoing protests and declining to dissolve the injunction with prejudice or otherwise restrict Plaintiffs’ ability to seek future injunctive relief.April 23, 2026:The Eighth Circuitgrantedthe government’s motion to dismiss its appeal after the district court on remanddissolvedits preliminary injunction as moot. The panel simultaneously denied Plaintiffs’ motion to vacate the Eighth Circuit’s prior order staying the injunction pending appeal.

Case Name: Kelly v. Hegseth(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00081
Executive Action: Retaliation Against Protected Speech
Case Summary: On Nov. 18, 2025, Senator Mark Kelly and five other members of Congress posted a video titled “Don’t Give Up The Ship” which reminded service members that they can refuse illegal orders. On Jan. 5, 2026, Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth issued acensure letterto Senator Kelly based on determinations that statements in the video undermined the chain of command, counselled disobedience, created confusion about duty, brought discredit upon the armed forces, and was conduct unbecoming an officer. Kelly was subsequently referred to retirement grade determination proceedings. On Jan. 12, 2026, Kelly filed acomplaintchallenging the censure and related efforts to reopen and reduce his retired military grade. The complaint alleges that the censure and reopening of Plaintiff’s grade determination violates the First Amendment as retaliation for protected speech, the Speech or Debate Clause of the U.S. Constitution as his statements were legislative acts and thus not subject to any adversarial proceedings, Separation of Powers as Defendants’ action usurps Congress’s authority to punish its own members, the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment as the decision to censure was preordained, 10 U.S.C. § 1370 which the complaint contends requires that only the Secretary of the Navy, not the Secretary of Defense, can reopen a retirement grade determination and then only for good cause, and the Administrative Procedure Act as contrary to law and arbitrary and capricious. Plaintiff seeks a declaratory judgment that the defendants’ actions were unlawful and an order vacating and enjoining the Defendants’ actions.
Case Updates: Jan. 12, 2026:Plaintiffmovedfor a temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction.Feb. 12, 2026:Judge LeongrantedPlaintiff’s motion for a preliminary injunction, enjoining the administration from censuring Senator Mark Kelly or initiating proceedings to reduce his retirement rank and pay, finding that such actions likely violated the Senator’s First Amendment rights by retaliating against him in response to a video message in which he warned U.S troops not to follow illegal orders.Feb. 24, 2026:Defendantsappealedto the D.C. Circuit, Case No.26-5070.

Case Name: Wright v. Noem(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00144
Executive Action: Retaliation Against Protected Speech
Case Summary: On January 19, 2026 Brandon Wright, a former civil servant and employee of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS)filed suitagainst the DHS and an unknown woman, defendant Doe, alleging that his employment was unlawfully terminated after he was filmed criticizing the agency’s head, Kristi Noem, during a date with Doe. The complaint states that defendant Doe was working for compensation and/or in conjunction with a conservative media group  and during their date, without his knowledge or consent, recorded him making negative comments about the DHS and Noem. After the recordings spread online, Wright was placed on administrative leave in January 2025 and then fired on Jan 8, 2026 due to the stated reason that he had made statements that were “disrespectful and unprofessional, and unbecoming of a federal employee.” The complaint alleges that the termination is a violation of the First Amendment as retaliation for protected speech and the Fifth Amendment due process clause as he was unable to obtain an objective review of his termination due to the non-functioning of the Merit Systems Protection Board (MSPB) as an independent agency. He seeks a declaration that the termination was unconstitutional and that the MSPB does not have the ability to adjudicate his claims. Mr. Wright separately seeks relief against Defendant Doe for fraudulent misrepresentation, conspiracy to commit fraudulent misrepresentation, tortious interference with employment relationship, and violation of state and federal wire tap laws.

Case Name: Dickinson v. Trump(D. Or.)3:25-cv-02170
Case Status: Government Action Not Blocked Pending Appeal
Executive Action: Retaliation Against Protected Speech
Case Summary: Plaintiffs are journalists and protestors, including Jack Dickinson, Laurie Eckman, Richard Eckman, Mason Lake, and Hugo Rios who bring a class action lawsuit against President Donald Trump, Secretary of the United States Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Kristi Noem, and DHS (Defendants). Plaintiffs allege that, beginning in June 2025, Defendants have engaged in a pattern and practice of excessive, indiscriminate, and retaliatory force against peaceful protesters and members of the press near the Portland ICE building. According to the complaint, Defendants deployed chemical and impact munitions, performed physical assaults, hindered journalistic coverage, and imposed unconstitutional restrictions on protected First Amendment activities. Plaintiffs further assert that Defendants have targeted those perceived as critical of the administration while permitting or encouraging pro-administration demonstrators, and have adopted policies chilling protected speech and newsgathering. The complaint seeks injunctive and declaratory relief and alleges violations of the First and Fourth Amendments (including First Amendment retaliation, viewpoint discrimination, and excessive force), the Fifth Amendment’s Due Process Clause, and the Administrative Procedure Act, for actions of the Defendants they allege to be arbitrary, capricious, contrary to law, and in excess of statutory authority. Plaintiffs ask the court for injunctive and declaratory relief and to vacate Defendants’ policies and agency actions as unlawful.
Case Updates: Dec. 10, 2025:Plaintiffs filed aFirst Amended Class Action Complaint.Feb. 3, 2026:The courtgranteda temporary restraining order to the putative class, enjoined DHS and other federal officers from using chemical or projectile munitions unless the specific target poses an imminent threat, and set an evidentiary hearing for March 2, 2026.Feb. 17, 2026:Judge Simonextendedthe temporary restraining order by 14 days until March 3, 2026.March 9, 2026:Judge Simonprovisionally certifieda class consisting of "[a]ll people who, since the beginning of Operation Skip Jack, have, desire to, or will nonviolently protest against or report on DHS activities at the PortlandICE Building." Judge SimongrantedPlaintiffs’motionfor a preliminary injunction and denied Defendants’ request for a stay. Judge Simonorderedall DHS agents to cease using a range of chemical or projectile munitions unless there is an imminent threat of physical harm.March 17, 2026:The governmentappealedJudge Simon’s March 9, 2026 order to the Ninth Circuit. The appeal is proceeding as Case No.26-1609.March 25, 2026:The Ninth Circuitgrantedthe administration’s request for an administrative stay, temporarily pausing the district court’s preliminary injunction while the court considers whether to issue a full stay pending appeal. Judge de Alba dissented from the panel’s decision, writing that there was not sufficient exigency to justify the stay. The panel consolidated oral argument for the appeal withReach Community Development v. U.S. Department of Homeland Security,grantingstays in both cases. Oral argument will be held virtually via Zoom on April 7, 2026.

Case Summary: On Feb. 6, 2026, NewsGuard Technologies, a nonpartisan media organization that rates the reliability of online news sources, filed acomplaintagainst the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and its chairman challenging a Civil Investigative Demand (CID) that the FTC issued to NewsGuard and an FTC consent order in the Omnicom-IPG merger that prohibits the merged company from using NewsGuard’s rating system. NewsGuard alleges that the CID is retaliatory, overly broad and burdensome and the order is politically motivated and driven by Chairman Ferguson’s ideological hostility to its ratings, and subject it to an unprecedented speech-based backlist. NewsGuard specifically alleges that Defendants violated, and continue to violate, its First Amendment and Fourth Amendment rights. It seeks declaratory and injunctive relief to block enforcement of the CID or merger condition.

Case Summary: Coming soon.On Feb. 11, 2026, Kassandra Rosado, creator of the Facebook group “ICE Sightings - Chicagoland” and Kreisau Group LLC, creator of Eyes Up, an app that allowed users to track and share videos of ICE activity,suedAttorney General Pam Bondi and DHS Secretary Kristi Noem for violating their First Amendment rights to record law enforcement activity. The court found that the Endocrine Society was likely to succeed on its First Amendment retaliation claim, concluding that “the FTC’s purported reason for its investigation bears little relationship to the extensive demand it issued” and that the CID “was likely issued for a retaliatory purpose.” The court denied the Society’s request for broader relief against further FTC investigative steps and denied the government’s request to stay the injunction pending appeal.

Case Name: World Professional Association for Transgender Health v. Federal Trade Commission(D.D.C)1:26-cv-00532
Executive Action: Retaliation Against Protected Speech
Case Summary: Coming soon.On Feb. 18, 2026, the World Professional Association for Transgender Health (WPATH)suedthe FTC and its Chairman and Commissioners over a civil investigative demand (CID) the agency served on WPATH requesting records and information on its work and operations. WPATH alleges the CID unconstitutionally retaliates against the organization in violation of its First Amendment rights to engage in legal advocacy for transgender rights and constitutes viewpoint discrimination. WPATH also argues that CID is overbroad and seeks irrelevant information, in violation of the Fourth Amendment. The organization seeks a declaration that the CID is unlawful and an injunction barring the FTC from enforcing the CID.

Case Name: Coalition for Independent Technology Research v. Rubio(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00815
Executive Action: Retaliation Against Protected Speech
Case Summary: On March 9, 2026, the Coalition for Independent Technology Research (CITR), a technology ethics nonprofit, filed acomplaintagainst the Secretary of State, the Secretary of Homeland Security, and the Attorney General challenging the administration’s purported policy of excluding and deporting noncitizens whose work involves combatting misinformation and disinformation, fact-checking, content moderation, trust and safety, or compliance (the “Censorship Policy”). Plaintiff alleges that the administration is using the Censorship Policy to punish independent researchers, advocates, and others who research and report on the major internet platforms through visa denials, visa revocations, and detention and deportation. They claim that this is having a chilling effect on CITR’s noncitizen members as well as harming CITR ‘s ability to advance its programmatic goals. Plaintiff alleges that the Censorship Policy violates the First Amendment as retaliation for protected speech and association based on viewpoint and content, the Fifth Amendment as vague and inviting arbitrary and discriminatory enforcement, and the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as arbitrary and capricious and contrary to statutory authority and constitutional right. Plaintiff seeks declaratory and injunctive relief.

Case Name: Accountability NOW USA v. Griess(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01385
Executive Action: Retaliation Against Protected Speech
Case Summary: Accountability NOW USA, an unincorporated association conducting an ongoing anti-Trump protest in Washington, D.C.suedKevin Griess, Superintendent of the National Mall and Memorial Parks and Doug Burgum, Secretary of the Interior, over alleged threats by the National Park Service (NPS) to revoke the group’s demonstration permit. Plaintiff alleges that the NPS classified two signs the group displayed at its demonstration site as obscenities unprotected by the First Amendment and ordered the signs removed. Plaintiff alleges that the threatened permit revocation constitutes an imminent violation of the First Amendment, which protects political speech — including references to alleged sexual misconduct by a public official — displayed in a traditional public forum under a valid permit, and that no colorable argument supports the signs' classification as legally obscene. Plaintiffs seek declaratory judgments that the signs are not legally obscene and are First Amendment-protected, a preliminary and permanent injunction prohibiting revocation of the permit or seizure of property based on the signs' content, and an order requiring a minimum of two full business days’ advance notice of any permit revocation.

Case Name: Doe v. Mullin(N.D. Cal.)3:26-cv-03988
Executive Action: Retaliation Against Protected Speech
Case Summary: On Feb. 4, 2026, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) issued an administrative summons to Google LLC demanding comprehensive records associated with a Gmail account belonging to the plaintiff — a Canadian citizen residing in Canada who has not entered the United States since 2015. The summons, issued by DHS under 19 U.S.C. § 1509, a customs-enforcement statute, sought all account records, location data, and activity and transaction logs for a five-month period. The plaintiff, who posts political commentary critical of the Trump administration pseudonymously on X and other platforms, alleges DHS issued the summons to unmask his identity and chill protected speech, not to investigate any customs violation.On May 4, 2026, the plaintiff — a pseudonymous social media user proceeding as John Doe — filed acomplaint, alleging that the summons exceeds DHS’s authority under 19 U.S.C. § 1509, which authorizes summonses only for customs-related investigations and limits compellable records to those required under 19 U.S.C. § 1508 or pertaining to unlawfully imported merchandise; and that the summons violates the Stored Communications Act, 18 U.S.C. §§ 2702–2703, which bars government acquisition of the categories of records sought through administrative subpoena alone. Plaintiff seeks a declaration that the summons exceeds Defendant’s statutory authority and an injunction barring enforcement of the summons or similar compulsory process targeting Plaintiff.

Case Name: Demster v. Blanche(W.D. Tenn.)2:26-cv-02546
Executive Action: Retaliation Against Protected Speech
Case Summary: On Sept. 15, 2025, President Donald Trump signed amemorandumestablishing the Memphis Safe Task Force, composed of agents from more than 13 federal agencies, to help conduct immigration enforcement and crime-reduction operations in Memphis, Tennessee. On May 13, 2026, four Memphis residents who regularly document and record Task Force activity in public filed acomplaintalleging that Task Force agents have systematically retaliated against them. They allege that this retaliation — including verbal threats, physical intimidation, traffic stops, surveillance and photographing, and at least one arrest and 27-hour detention — violates the First Amendment. Plaintiffs also challenge the as-applied constitutionality of Tennessee’s “Halo Law,” which criminalizes approaching law enforcement within 25 feet after a warning, alleging it unconstitutionally burdens their First Amendment right to observe and record government activity. Plaintiffs bring their claims against Federal Defendants directly under the First Amendment and against state defendants under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. Plaintiffs seek declaratory judgments that both the retaliation policy and the Halo Law’s application violate the First Amendment, a permanent injunction against further retaliation, and expungement of surveillance records collected as part of the retaliatory scheme.

The plaintiff, Maria Moe, is a transgender female federal inmate who was placed in a Special Housing Unit to await transfer to a men’s facility. The suit seeks to enjoin the Executive Order on the basis that it violates the 5th Amendment by discriminating against transgender individuals on the basis of sex and gender identity; the 8th Amendment by subjecting Moe to risk to life and dignity; the Rehabilitation Act by failing to accommodate Moe’s gender dysphoria; and the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) by doing so in an arbitrary and capricious manner.

Case Name: Poe v. U.S. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01552
Executive Action: Housing of Transgender Incarcerated Individuals (Executive Order 14168)
Case Summary: On Jan. 20, 2025, President Donald Trump issuedExecutive Order 14168directing federal agencies to recognize only two biological sexes and ordering the Department of Justice (DOJ) to amend regulations that conflicted with that policy. On Dec. 2, 2025, DOJ’s Bureau of Justice Assistance issued amemorandumsuspending all regulations under the Prison Rape Elimination Act (PREA) that address protections for transgender, intersex, and gender non-conforming incarcerated people as conflicting with Executive Order 14168.On May 6, 2026, a transgender woman incarcerated in a men’s facility filed acomplaintalleging she has been sexually assaulted, repeatedly subjected to unwanted sexual contact, and strip-searched by male officers, and has suffered psychological harm due to the removal of PREA protections. Case Summary: On Jan. 20, 2025 the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) issued a directive rescinding the Biden Administration’sguidelinesfor ICE and CBP enforcement actions that restricted agents from conducting immigration enforcement in or near “sensitive” areas, such as places of worship, schools, and hospitals. The suit asks the court to enjoin and vacate the new policy and require the 2025 policy to be made public.

Case Name: New England Synod, Evangelical Lutheran Church in America v. Department of Homeland Security(D. Mass.)4:25-cv-40102

Thechange in policy, which reverses two decades of guidance, allows the administration to conduct enforcement at “sensitive locations,” such as schools, shelters, food banks, hospitals, and churches. Case Name: Fridley Public School District, Independent School District 14 v. Mullin(D. Minn.)0:26-cv-01023
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Executive Action: Immigration Enforcement Against Places of Worship and Schools (Policy Memo)
Case Summary: On January 20, 2025, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) issued a memorandum rescinding a decades-old policy that restricted immigration enforcement actions in or near "sensitive locations," such as schools and school bus stops. The new policy, often referred to as the "2025 Policy," replaced previous guardrails with "enforcement discretion" and "common sense," effectively allowing federal agents to conduct operations at these locations without prior written approval or the existence of exigent circumstances. Following this policy change, the government launched "Operation Metro Surge" in the Minneapolis-St. Paul area, resulting in a significant federal presence and numerous enforcement actions on or near school grounds.Plaintiffs—including the Fridley and Duluth Public School Districts and the Education Minnesota union—filed acomplaintagainst the administration challenging the 2025 Policy. They allege the 2025 Policy disrupted education and its operations, as well as chilled school attendance by nearly one third. Plaintiffs assert that the 2025 Policy violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) because it is arbitrary and capricious. They argue the government failed to provide a reasoned explanation for abandoning a 30-year policy, ignored the reliance interests of the impacted communities, and failed to consider the harms the change would cause. Additionally, they contend the policy is unlawful because it was issued without the required notice-and-comment rulemaking. Plaintiffs have requested that the court declare the 2025 Policy unlawful, preliminarily and permanently enjoin the government from implementing the policy, and specifically enjoin enforcement actions within 1,000 feet of school property or bus stops absent a warrant or exigent circumstances.
Case Updates: May 6, 2026:Judge ProvinzinodeniedPlaintiffs’ motion to stay implementation of thenew guidanceon ICE enforcement operations at certain sensitive or protected locations, including schools. The court found that Plaintiffs likely did not have standing for their claims, principally on traceability and redressability grounds. Judge Provinzino found that the new guidance did not actually change DHS’ ability or authority to engage in enforcement activity at or near protected locations, as such enforcement “has always been subject to DHS’ judgment and discretion.” This lack of traceability between the new guidance and the alleged harms the school districts asserted – trespass by agents, decreased attendance, and resource expenditure – meant they had no standing to bring their claims. Education Minnesota, the court wrote, also had no standing to bring its claim in either an individual or a representative capacity. The lack of standing meant that Plaintiffs were unlikely to succeed on the merits and therefore not entitled to a preliminary injunction or stay.

Case Name: American Center for International Labor Solidarity v. Chavez-Deremer(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01128

Case Name: Orr v. Trump(D. Mass.)1:25-cv-10313
Case Status: Government Action Not Blocked Pending Appeal
Executive Action: Passport Policy Targeting Transgender Individuals (Executive Order 14168)
Case Summary: On Jan. 20, 2025, the Trump administration issued an executive order stating that there are only two sexes, male and female, and that they are determined by immutable biological factors at conception. The order directed the Secretary of State to change policies related to documents like passports to align with the order’s definition of sex. The State Department subsequently stopped processing passport applications of individuals seeking to change their sex designation, or who selected an “X” designation. Plaintiffs, represented by the ACLU, sued, arguing the policy is unconstitutional and violates the 5th Amendment’s equal protection guarantee by discriminating on the basis of sex and transgender status; violates the Fifth Amendment by restricting the right of free movement and travel; violates the Fifth Amendment by forcing disclosure of private and intimate information; and violates the First Amendment by compelling the speech of transgender applicants through their passports. Plaintiffs also argue the policy is unlawful under the Administrative Procedure Act, as contrary to constitutional rights, powers, and immunities; as an arbitrary and capricious abuse of discretion; and by failing to observe procedures as required by law in instituting the policy without a comment period. They seek a declaratory judgment that the policy is unconstitutional and unlawful; preliminary and permanent injunctions stopping the policy from being implemented; and an order vacating agency actions already taken under the policy.

Case Name: Schlacter v. U.S. Department of State(D. Md.)1:25-cv-01344
Case Status: Government Action Not Blocked Pending Appeal
Executive Action: Passport Policy Targeting Transgender Individuals (Executive Order 14168)
Case Summary: On Jan. 20, 2025, the Trump administration issued an executive order stating that there are only two sexes, male and female, and that they are determined by immutable biological factors at conception. The order directed the Secretary of State to change policies related to documents like passports to align with the order’s definition of sex. The State Department subsequently stopped processing passport applications of individuals seeking to change their sex designation, or who selected an “X” designation. Plaintiffs sued, arguing the policy is unconstitutional and violates the Fifth Amendment’s equal protection guarantee by discriminating on the basis of sex and of transgender status; violates the Fifth Amendment by restricting the right of free movement and travel; violates the Fifth Amendment by forcing disclosure of private and intimate information; and violates the First Amendment by compelling the speech of transgender and nonbinary applicants through their passports. Plaintiffs also argue the policy is unlawful under the Administrative Procedure Act, as contrary to constitutional rights, powers, and immunities and as an arbitrary and capricious abuse of discretion. They seek a declaratory judgment that the policy is unconstitutional and unlawful; and preliminary and permanent national injunctions that stop agencies from implementing the policy as it relates to passports.

Russellgranteda preliminary injunction to six transgender individuals denied passports matching their gender identity under anew government policy. The court held the policy violated the Fifth Amendment equal protection guarantee, finding the policy did not satisfy the heightened scrutiny required for sex-based classifications because the rationale for the policy given by the government did “not serve an important governmental interest that is exceedingly persuasive” and because “the discriminatory means employed are not substantially related to the achievement of those objectives.” Judge Russell declined to reach the other constitutional and statutory claims, finding that the equal protection claim was sufficient to grant the injunction. The court dismissed a claim by a seventh plaintiff for lack of standing, and did not extend the preliminary injunction beyond the six successful plaintiffs.Nov. 6, 2025:In the case of Orr v. Case Summary: On Feb. 11, 2025, White House officials banned AP journalists from entering areas like the Oval Office and Air Force One as members of the press pool unless the AP began referring to the Gulf of Mexico as the Gulf of America. The Plaintiff seeks a declaratory judgment that denying AP access is unconstitutional, and a court order that the White House rescind the policy. On Feb. 21, 2025, the AP also filed amotion for a TROand amotion for a preliminary injunction.
