Case Name: State of Illinois v. EPA(D.C. Cir.)26-1072
Executive Action: Rescission of Previous Executive Orders and Actions, Including on Climate and Environment (Executive Order 14148)
Case Summary: On February 24, 2026, the Administration issued the following notice that repeals amendments to one of the EPA’s Rules: the National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants: Coal- and Oil-Fired Electric Utility Steam Generating Units: Final Repeal. The States of Illinois, Minnesota, Arizona, Connecticut, Delaware, Maine, Maryland, Michigan, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York Oregon, Rhode Island, Vermont, Washington, and Wisconsin, as well as several municipalities, petitioned to have this notice decreed unlawful pursuant to Sec. 307(d)(9) of the Clean Air Act, 42 U.S.C. § 7607(d)(9). The original Rule sought to minimize harmful pollutants from certain energy-generating products; the repeal revises standards to permit higher quantities of emissions of hazardous air pollutant materials, such as mercury and other metals.

Case Name: Environmental Defense Fund, Inc. v. Wright(D. Mass.)1:25-cv-12249
Case Status: Case Closed in Favor of Plaintiff
Executive Action: Noncompliance with the Federal Advisory Committee Act
Case Summary: In March 2025, the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) and the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) established and utilized a Climate Working Group composed of individuals skeptical of mainstream climate science to produce a report that informed EPA’s proposed rescission of the 2009 determination that greenhouse gases threaten the public health and welfare of the American people (the “Endangerment Finding”). The group was formed and operated without public disclosure, and its report was published concurrently with EPA’s proposed rulemaking, which cited the report extensively. On August 12, the Environmental Defense Fund and the Union of Concerned Scientists filed acomplaintchallenging the administration’s conduct regarding the Climate Working Group. Plaintiffs allege that the formation and use of the Climate Working Group violated procedural and transparency requirements under federal law, depriving them and the public of access to meetings, records, and balanced representation. They claim these violations hinder their ability to participate in public comment processes and to advocate for science-based climate policy. Plaintiffs allege that the administration’s action violates the Administrative Procedure Act as arbitrary, capricious, not in accordance with law, in excess of statutory jurisdiction, authority, or limitations, and without observance of procedure required by law, including the Federal Advisory Committee Act, and constitutional and statutory provisions. They seek declaratory and injunctive relief to vacate the establishment and actions of the Climate Working Group, prohibit reliance on its report, compel disclosure of related records and extend public comment periods on EPA’s proposed rulemaking.
Case Updates: Sept. 17, 2025:Judge YoungdeniedPlaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction, finding they had not demonstrated irreparable harm, while at the same time granting partial summary judgment that the Climate Working Group is subject to FACA because the 3.40(e) “exchange of facts” exception does not apply.Jan. 30, 2026:Judge YounggrantedPlaintiffs’ motion for summary judgment, ruling that the Department of Energy’s Climate Working Group was not exempt from the Federal Advisory Committee Act and the agency failed to satisfy that statute’s transparency, fair balance, and reporting requirements. The court separately dismissed the EPA as a defendant, finding no persuasive evidence of conduct violating FACA outside of the Department of Energy.

Case Name: Northeast Organic Farming Association of New York v. U.S. Department of Agriculture(S.D.N.Y.)1:25-cv-01529
Issue: Removal of Information from Government Websites
Executive Action: Removal of Climate Change and Environmental Information
Case Summary: On Jan. 30, 2025, the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) removed climate change-related data from many of its websites. Plaintiffs, including environmental organizations and a nonprofit representing farmers, gardeners, and consumers, allege that the removal of data violates the Paperwork Reduction Act, the Administrative Procedure Act, and the Freedom of Information Act. They seek declaratory judgment that the removal of information is unlawful; injunctions on further removal of information from agency websites; notice of any further modifications to webpages; and restoration of previously publicly-available datasets.

USDA noted that the “vast majority of webpages–including all webpages that plaintiffs attached as exhibits to their complaint–have been restored.”Jan. 13, 2026:Plaintiffs filed astatus reportindicating that the parties were continuing to assess whether an amicable resolution is possible and requested that the court grant the parties additional time to contemplate settlement.

Case Name: State of New York v. Trump(D. Mass.)1:25-cv-11221
Executive Action: Halting Wind Energy Approvals
Case Summary: On January 20, 2025, President Trump issued a Presidential Memorandum that categorically and indefinitely halted all federal approvals necessary for the development of offshore- and onshore-wind energy, pending an inter-agency review. The Memorandum orders that federal agencies “not issue new or rendered approvals, rights of way, permits, leases, or loans for onshore or offshore wind projects pending the completion of a comprehensive assessment and review of Federal wind leasing and permitting practices”. Plaintiffs, 17 States and the District of Columbia, seek a declaration that the adoption and implementation of this directive is a violation of the APA, a violation of federal environmental laws, and ultra vires. They also seek a preliminary injunction to prevent the federal government from implementing or otherwise giving effect to any action that halts or impedes wind energy development based on the directive.
Case Updates: May 12, 2025:Plaintiffs filed amotionfor preliminary injunction, requesting that the Court enjoin the defendant federal agencies from implementing or giving effect to the directive.May 29, 2025:The defendant federal agencies filed theiroppositionto Plaintiff’s motion for a preliminary injunction.July 3, 2025:The Courtaffirmedthe lawsuit can proceed in part, dismissing the plaintiffs’ constitutional claims but allowing the claims related to the Administrative Procedure Act to proceed.Dec. 8, 2025:Judge Patti SarisgrantedPlaintiffs’ motion for summary judgment, holding that the administration’s order halting federal approvals for development of wind energy was arbitrary and capricious and therefore unlawful under the Administrative Procedure Act.Feb. 17, 2026:The administrationappealedJudge Saris’rulingthat the administration unlawfully halted federal approvals for offshore wind leases.

Case Name: Protect Our Coast NJ v. USA(D.N.J.)3:25-cv-06890
Executive Action: Halting Wind Energy Approvals
Case Summary: On Jan. 20, 2025, President Donald Trump issued aPresidential Memorandumthat indefinitely halted all federal approvals necessary for the development of wind energy and required that the Secretary of the Interior investigate all offshore wind projects to determine if they will cause ecological, environmental or economic harm. Plaintiffs are fisheries and fishing industry participants, trade groups, coastal environment nonprofits, and the mayor of Seaside Park, New Jersey, who are all claiming harm from the offshore wind Empire Wind project. Defendants are the United States, Secretary of the Interior Doug Burgum, the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management (BOEM) and its Acting Director Walter Cruickshank, Empire Wind, Equinor (owner of Empire Wind), and the Kingdom of Norway (the controlling owner of Equinor). Pursuant to the Memorandum, a stop work order was issued on April 16, 2025, that stopped all work on Empire Wind until an investigation was completed. On May 19, 2025, the stop work order was reversed by BOEM and a reinstatement order allowing work to resume on Empire Wind was issued. Plaintiffs are arguing that the May 19 reinstatement order violated the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) because it is arbitrary, capricious, or unreasonable. Plaintiffs also claim that the reinstatement order violates the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act (OCSLA), which prohibits Norway, as a foreign government, from acquiring a lease on the outer continental shelf of the U.S. Plaintiffs are requesting that the court declare that the May 19 reinstatement order was unlawful and an injunction directing that the reinstatement order be vacated and BOEM to resume investigating Empire Wind.
Case Updates: June 12, 2025:Plaintiffsfiledan ex parte motion for order to show cause, seeking a temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction to prevent Defendants from proceeding with installation and construction activities for Empire Wind.July 4, 2025:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaintin which it added additional plaintiffs.

Case Name: Revolution Wind, LLC v. Burgum(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-02999
Executive Action: Halting Wind Energy Approvals
Case Summary: Following the Jan. 20, 2025 issuance of aPresidential Memorandumrestricting offshore wind development and a subsequentorderby the Secretary of the Interior, on August 22, 2025 the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management (BOEM) issued aStop Work Orderrequiring Revolution Wind, a company constructing an offshore wind project intended to provide energy to the New England region, to halt construction of the Revolution Wind Project (the “Project”). The Project commenced in 2023 following a multi-year process of review that resulted in a consensus decision of 15 federal and state agencies that the Project was both safe and consistent with federal and state law. Construction is now 80% complete at a cost of billions of dollars.Revolution Road filed a complaint on Sept. 4, 2025, alleging that the Stop Work Order significantly harms its financial obligations and commercial commitments and endangers the energy, environmental and public health benefits of the Project. (An additionalcomplaintwas also filed by the States of Rhode Island and Connecticut, alleging that the Stop Work Order harms the states and their residents).The Plaintiff claims that the Stop Work Order is a violation of the Administrative Procedure Act as arbitrary and capricious, contrary to law as a violation of the provisions of the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act (OCSLA) and a violation of due process under the Fifth Amendment, and seeks declaratory and injunctive relief staying and vacating the Stop Work Order and preventing any further interference with the Project.
Case Updates: Sept. 22, 2025:District Court Judge Lamberthenjoinedthe administration’sStop Work Orderwhich halted construction on Revolution Wind’s two-year offshore wind project intended to provide energy to the New England region. In a bench ruling, Judge Lamberthreportedlycalled the administration’s decision the “height of arbitrary and capricious action” and said it failed to offer any factual findings justifying the reversal.Jan. 12, 2026:The court granted theRevolution Wind’s motion for a preliminary injunction, pausing the implementation of the government’s Stop Work Orders.

Case Name: State of Rhode Island v. United States Department of the Interior(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04328
Executive Action: Halting Wind Energy Approvals
Case Summary: Following the Jan. 20, 2025 issuance of aPresidential Memorandumrestricting offshore wind development and a subsequentorderby the Secretary of the Interior, on August 22, 2025 the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management (BOEM) issued aStop Work Orderrequiring Revolution Wind, a company constructing an offshore wind project intended to provide energy to the New England region, to halt construction of the Revolution Wind Project (the “Project”). The Project commenced in 2023 following a multi-year process of review that resulted in a consensus decision of 15 federal and state agencies that the Project was both safe and consistent with federal and state law. Construction is now 80% complete at a cost of billions of dollars.The States of Connecticut and Rhode Islandfiled suiton Sept. 4, 2025, alleging that the Stop Work Order harms the energy, economic and contractual interests of their states and undermines their ability to meet state energy and environmental requirements. (An additionalcomplaintwas filed the same day by Revolution Wind.)The Plaintiff States claim that the Stop Work Order is a violation of the Administrative Procedure Act as arbitrary and capricious, as well as contrary to law and ultra vires as not in accordance with BOEM’s authority. The complaint also includes a citizen suit under the provisions of the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act (OCSLA), 43 U.S.C. § 1349, which provides that “any person having a valid legal interest which is or may be adversely affected” may commence a civil action to compel compliance with the Act and alleges that the Stop Work Order violates the procedural requirements of the OCSLA. The Plaintiff States seek declaratory and injunctive relief preventing any further interference with the Project.

Case Name: State of New York v. Burgum(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00071
Executive Action: Halting Wind Energy Approvals
Case Summary: Since June 2024, Empire Offshore Wind LLC and Empire Leaseholder LLC (together, Empire Wind) has been constructing a commercial wind installation (the Empire Wind Project) in federal waters on the Outer Continental Shelf, around 14 miles southeast of Long Island, under contract with the State of New York and the New York State Energy Research and Development Authority (NYSERDA) and with the approval of the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management (BOEM), a division of the U.S. Department of the Interior. On Dec. 22, 2025, BOEM issued an order directing Empire Wind to “suspend all ongoing activities related to [the Empire Wind Project] on the Outer Continental Shelf for the next 90 days for reasons of national security.” On Jan. 9, 2026, the State of New York and NYSERDA filed acomplaintseeking to overturn the suspension, alleging the order violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as arbitrary and capricious due to the breadth of the suspension and the failure to provide a reasoned explanation or justification for the change in position, which they contend is due to the administration’s opposition to wind energy rather than national security concerns. Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief vacating the suspension order and enjoining its enforcement.
Case Updates: Jan. 30, 2026:The courtconsolidatedState of New York v. Burgum(D.D.C.), 1:26-cv-00071 withEmpire Leaseholder LLC v. Burgum(D.D.C.), 1:26-cv-00004. These cases will proceed underEmpire Leaseholder LLC v. Burgum.

Case Name: Sunrise Wind LLC v. Burgum(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00028
Executive Action: Halting Wind Energy Approvals
Case Summary: Since June 2024, Sunrise Wind LLC (Sunrise Wind) has been constructing a commercial wind installation (the Sunrise Wind Project) in federal waters on the Outer Continental Shelf, around 30 miles east of Long Island, under contract with the State of New York and the New York State Energy Research and Development Authority (NYSERDA) and with the approval of the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management (BOEM), a division of the U.S. Department of the Interior. On Dec. 22, 2025, BOEM issued an order directing Sunrise Wind to “suspend all ongoing activities related to [the Sunrise Wind Project] on the Outer Continental Shelf for the next 90 days for reasons of national security.” On Jan. 6, 2026, Sunrise Wind, an offshore wind energy company,filed suitagainst the Department of the Interior, the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management (BOEM), and the Bureau of Safety and Environmental Enforcement (BSEE), alleging the order violates the Fifth Amendment Due Process Clause, the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act (OCSLA), and the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), specifically), specifically that the Stop Work Order is arbitrary and capricious, issued without observance of procedure required by law, and issued in excess of statutory authority. Plaintiff alleges that the Stop Work Order will cost Sunrise Wind over $1 million per day and risks cancellation of the project, leading to losses of more than $8 billion in investments and expected future profits. Plaintiff seeks declaratory and injunctive relief, and a stay of the Stop Work Order, and an order vacating and setting it aside.

Case Name: Vineyard Wind 1 LLC v. United States Department of the Interior(D. Mass.)1:26-cv-10156
Executive Action: Halting Wind Energy Approvals
Case Summary: Since 2021, Vineyard Wind has been constructing a commercial wind installation (the Vineyard Wind Project) in federal waters on the Outer Continental Shelf, around 14 miles from Massachusetts, with the approval of and a lease from the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management (BOEM), a division of the U.S. Department of the Interior. The construction was approved by the Department of Defense and subject to litigation, which was successfully defended by BOEM and is now approximately 95% completed. On Dec. 22, 2025, BOEM issued an order directing Vineyard Wind to “suspend all ongoing activities related to [the Vineyard Wind Project] on the Outer Continental Shelf for the next 90 days for reasons of national security.” On Jan. 15, 2026, Plaintiff filed acomplaintseeking to overturn the suspension, alleging the order violates BOEM’s regulations as well as procedures set out in the lease and the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act (OCSLA). Plaintiff also claims that the suspension violates their due process rights under the Fifth Amendment and violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as arbitrary and capricious due to the failure to follow procedures and the failure to provide a reasoned explanation or justification for the change in position, which they allege is due to the administration’s opposition to wind energy rather than national security concerns. Plaintiff seeks declaratory and injunctive relief vacating the suspension order and enjoining its enforcement.
Case Updates: Jan. 23, 2026:The State Attorney General of Massachusetts submitted anamicus briefin support of Plaintiffs.Jan. 27, 2026:Judge Murphygrantedin part Plaintiff’s motion for a temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction, staying the administration’s Dec. 22, 2025 stop work order as to the Vineyard Winds 1 Project until further order of the court.

Case Name: Virginia Electric and Power Company v. United States Department of the Interior(E.D. Va.)2:25-cv-00830
Executive Action: Halting Wind Energy Approvals
Case Summary: On December 22, 2025, the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management (BOEM) issued a one-page Director’sorderdirecting Dominion Energy Virginia (DEV) to immediately stop work on the Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind Commercial Project (CVOW). The project had had all necessary permits and government approval since 2024 and was set to begin generating electricity in early 2026. The order came with no prior warning and did not allege specific violations or deficiencies with CVOW. The order only cited ceunspecified national security threats, but apress releaseannounced the same day referenced purported radar interference. The same press release revealed that all large-scale offshore wind projects had been halted. On Dec. 23, 2025, DEV and OSW Project (the lessee and developer of CVOW) filed acomplaintagainst the Department of the Interior (DOI) and the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management (BOEM). Plaintiffs assert that implementation of the order would result in significant financial loss and harm public interest by delaying access to renewable energy. The halt would also jeopardize compliance with Virginia’s legislatively mandated clean energy targets and DEV’s commitments to net-zero emissions. Plaintiffs allege that Defendants’ actions violate the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as the order is arbitrary and inconsistent with BOEM’s policies. Additionally, Plaintiffs argue that the order deprives them of a property interest without due process in violation of the Fifth Amendment and violates the Property Clause of the Constitution by unlawfully overriding legislation enacted by Congress (an ultra vires action). Plaintiffs also brought suit pursuant to the citizen suit provision of the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act. Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief and temporary, preliminary, and permanent injunctions.
Case Updates: Dec. 28, 2025:Plaintiffs’ motion for a temporary injunction wasconvertedinto a motion for a preliminary injunction. In a bench order on January 16, 2026, Judge WalkergrantedPlaintiffs’ motion for preliminary injunction.

Case Name: Renew Northeast v. United States Department of the Interior(D. Mass.)1:25-cv-13961
Executive Action: Halting Wind Energy Approvals
Case Summary: Eight renewable energy trade associations filed acomplainton Dec. 23, 2025, challenging six federal agency actions that Plaintiffs allege operate collectively to delay and prevent the permitting and development of wind and solar energy facilities nationwide. The challenged actions alleged are: (1) aJuly 15, 2025, Department of the Interior (DOI) memorandumrequiring multiple layers of review of every wind and solar permitting decision across DOI bureaus; (2)Secretarial Order 3438(Aug. 1, 2025), directing DOI to apply a “capacity density” metric that allegedly disfavors wind and solar in permitting decisions on federal lands and waters; (3) a Sept. 18, 2025, Army Corps of Engineers memorandum embedding the same capacity density metric into Clean Water Act Section 404 and Rivers and Harbors Act Section 10 permit reviews; (4) aU.S. Fish & Wildlife Service (USFWS) ban, effective Jan. 20, 2025, barring issuance of required permits to avoid Bald and Golden Eagle Protection Act (BGEPA) liability; (5) aUSFWS decision (around July 15, 2025)blocking wind and solar developers from accessing the agency’s Information for Planning and Consultation (IPaC) database, which developers use to streamline the permit process; and, (6) aMay 1, 2025, DOI Solicitor’s opinion(the “Zerzan/Jorjani Opinion”) reinstating a narrower interpretation of Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act (OCSLA) subsection 8(p)(4) that allegedly creates a de facto moratorium on the approval of new offshore wind facilities. Plaintiffs allege that each action has halted or delayed wind and solar permitting, causing billions of dollars in project costs, lost investment and revenue, and breached contracts.Plaintiffs allege all six actions violate the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as arbitrary and capricious and as in excess of statutory authority under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act, the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act, the Clean Water Act, the Rivers and Harbors Act, the Bald and Golden Eagle Protection Act, and the Endangered Species Act; and for failure to conduct required notice-and-comment rulemaking. Plaintiffs further allege the Bottleneck Memorandum and the Corps Memorandum violate APA §§ 555(b) and 558(c) by failing to process permit applications within a reasonable time, and that the Zerzan/Jorjani Opinion is impermissibly retroactive and conflicts with First Circuit precedent. Plaintiffs seek declaratory judgments that all six actions are unlawful, vacatur, and preliminary and permanent injunctive relief against their implementation.
Case Updates: April 21, 2026:In anopinionandorder, Judge Casper granted Plaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction as to five of the six challenged agency actions (all but the Eagle Take Permit Ban, which the government had already reversed), holding that Plaintiffs were likely to succeed on their APA claims. FollowingTrump v. CASA, Inc., the court limited the injunction to Plaintiffs’ members rather than entering nationwide relief.

Case Name: Oregon Environmental Council v. Internal Revenue Service(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04400
Executive Action: Rescission of Federal Tax Credits for Solar and Wind Facilities
Case Summary: Plaintiffs are not-for-profit organizations interested in the promotion of renewable energy. Defendants are the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), Department of the Treasury, and the Secretary of the Treasury. Plaintiffs allege that the IRS recently decided—allegedly at the direction of President Trump—to eliminate a longstanding method for most solar and wind facilities to demonstrate eligibility for federal tax credits (the “Five Percent Safe Harbor Test,” which requires “paying or incurring five percent or more of the total cost of the facility.”) Plaintiffs argue that the decision is arbitrary and capricious agency action in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) because it provides no statutory or evidentiary justification for eliminating the Five Percent Safe Harbor Test for wind and solar facilities while retaining it for all other energy technologies (5 U.S.C. § 702). The decision allegedly provides no reason for ignoring the longstanding and ongoing industry reliance interests on the Five Percent Safe Harbor. The decision also allegedly does not discuss any alternatives to the elimination of the Five Percent Safe Harbor that might better accommodate these reliance interests. Plaintiffs primarily ask the court, therefore, to declare that defendants’ decision to end use of the Safe Harbor Test is contrary to law and arbitrary and capricious; and hold the decision unlawful.

Case Name: Appalachian Voices v. United States Environmental Protection Agency(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01982
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Executive Action: Unleashing American Energy (Executive Order No. 14154); Ending Radical and Wasteful Government DEI Programs and Preferencing (Executive Order 14151); Implementing the President’s “Department of Government Efficiency” Cost Efficiency Initiative (Executive Order 14222)
Case Summary: In February 2025, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) under the direction of President Trump, began eliminating theEnvironmental and Climate Justice Block Grant program, which was created by Congress and funded by specific appropriations through the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA). The IRA directs the EPA to award grant program funding to disadvantaged communities.  On Jun. 25, 2025, Appalachian Voices and various organizations filed aclass action complaintagainst the EPA alleging that the termination violates the separation of powers, the Presentment Clauses, and the Administrative Procedure Act.
Case Updates: June 27, 2025:Plaintiffs filed a motion forpreliminary injunction, seeking reinstatement of the grant program, and amotion to certify the class.July 7, 2025:State Attorneys General submitted anamicus briefin support of Plaintiffs.Aug. 29, 2025:Judge LeongrantedDefendants’ motion to dismiss and denied Plaintiffs’ motions for a preliminary injunction and class certification, holding that the court lacked jurisdiction. The courtconcludedthat Plaintiffs’ APA claim was, in substance, a contract claim over disbursed grant funds that must be brought in the Court of Federal Claims, and that their constitutional claim could not proceed independently where the alleged violation (failure to continue the Grant Program) was rooted in a statutory obligation under the Clean Air Act.Sept. 16, 2025:Plaintiffsnoticedan appeal to the D.C. Circuit from Judge Leon’s Aug. 29 dismissal order and denial of preliminary relief and class certification. On the same day, Plaintiffs filed an emergency motion in district courtaskingJudge Leon for an injunction pending appeal.Sept. 25, 2025:Judge Leon issued anorderandopiniondenying Plaintiffs’ motion for an injunction pending appeal.

Case Name: Friends of the Ruidosa Church v. Mullin et al(W.D. Tex.)3:26-cv-01099
Executive Action: Border Wall Construction
Case Summary: On Feb. 17, 2026, then-DHS Secretary Kristi Noem issued adetermination, invoking Section 102(c) of the Illegal Immigration Reform and Immigrant Responsibility Act of 1996 (IIRIRA), to waive 29 federal laws — including NEPA, the Endangered Species Act, the National Historic Preservation Act, the Clean Water Act, and the Administrative Procedure Act — to expedite construction of new 30-foot-tall bollard walls and associated infrastructure spanning more than 250 miles along the Rio Grande in the Big Bend region of Texas, citing the sector as an area of high illegal entry.A coalition of historic and environmental nonprofits and a professional river guide filed acomplaintalleging that the waiver violates four constitutional doctrines, the major questions doctrine, the nondelegation doctrine, the Take Care Clause, and the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment. Plaintiffs allege that the wall would block river access and destroy critical Chihuahuan Desert habitat. Plaintiffs seek declaratory judgments that IIRIRA does not grant the government authority to implement border-wide waivers and that § 102(c) is an unconstitutional delegation if construed otherwise, vacatur of the Big Bend Waiver, and a preliminary and permanent injunction against implementation of the Big Bend Border Wall Project.

Case Name: Lopez v. United States of America(D. Ariz.)2:25-cv-02758
Executive Action: Construction on Public Lands
Case Summary: Since the mid-1900s, the United States government has recognized and protected the religious significance of Oak Flat in Arizona as a site of religious significance to Western Apaches, which was taken by force from the Western Apache over 150 years ago. In 1995, a large copper deposit was discovered beneath Oak Flat and in 2014, the Secretary of Agriculture transferred the title of Forest Service lands, including Oak Flat, to Resolution, a foreign-owned copper mining company, and revoked previous protections for Oak Flat, subject to the publication of an environmental impact statement (EIS). On March 20, 2025, President Donald Trump issuedExecutive Order (EO) 14241titled "Immediate Measures to Increase American Mineral Production", stating that the United States must “take immediate action to facilitate domestic mineral production to the maximum possible extent” and ordering agencies to “identify priority projects that can be immediately approved or for which permits can be immediately issued.” An EIS relating to Oak Flat, which confirmed that the surface mining project will destroy Oak Flat, including for use by Western Apaches for religious practice, and that other, more expensive but less destructive mining techniques are allegedly available, was published on June 20, 2025 and the government then stated its intent to transfer Oak Flat to Resolution on Aug. 19, 2025.On July 24, 2025, members of the Apache community filed acomplaintchallenging the administration’s plan to develop a mine onOak Flat. Plaintiffs allege the plan violates multiple statutes including the Religious Freedom Restoration Act, the First Amendment Free Exercise Clause, National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA), National Historic Preservation Act, and the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) for arbitrary and capricious conduct, and seek a permanent injunction to prevent the transfer of Oak Flat to Resolution and a declaration that the transfer and destruction of Oak Flats would be invalid.
Case Updates: July 25, 2025:Plaintiffs filed amotionfor a preliminary injunction requesting that the court enjoin Defendants from conveying Oak Flat to the mining company on August 19 or otherwise allowing or authorizing the company to take any action affecting Oak Flat’s physical integrity.Aug. 1, 2025:D.C. District Judge Timothy J. Kelly issued anorderandopinionthat the case be transferred to the District of Arizona, where it is now proceeding asLopez v. United States of America, No. 2:25-cv-02758 (D. Ariz.).Aug. 15, 2025:Plaintiffs filed amotionrequesting that, if the court denied their July 25 motion for a preliminary injunction, it should instead enter an expedited injunction pending appeal to the Ninth Circuit.Aug. 17, 2025:Judge Dominic W. LanzadeniedPlaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction and their emergency motion for an injunction pending appeal. Plaintiffs filed anoticeof appeal the same day,Oct. 26, 2025:The Ninth Circuitscheduledoral arguments for Jan. 7, 2026.

Case Name: Southern Utah Wilderness Alliance v. United States Department of the Interior(D. Utah)2:25-cv-00657
Executive Action: Construction on Public Lands
Case Summary: In August 2019, the former Assistant Secretary of Lands and Minerals Management approved a potash mining project on public lands in Utah’s West Desert and Sevier Lake region following an environmental impact statement (EIS). In 2025, the mining company, Peak Minerals, proposed a different plan with a longer timeline and expanded production that would allegedly significantly impair the natural habitat for migratory birds and create considerable light pollution in the area.  In the winter of 2025, the Bureau of Land Management (BLM) announced it would conduct a supplemental environmental assessment to study the expanded project plans. However, in April 2025, BLM stopped this new environmental assessment and approved the new plan through a Determination of National Environmental Policy Act Adequacy (DNA), that had been conducted to support the original, smaller 2019 plan, which allegedly did not cure NEPA violations over which the Plaintiff, Southern Utah Wilderness Alliance (SUWA), previously sued.On August 7, 2025, a Utah-based nonprofit environmental membership organisation that works to protect public land in Utah filed acomplaintchallenging the administration’s approval of the modified 2025 mining plan from Peak Minerals. The Plaintiff alleges that the administration used the 2019 DNA “to circumvent the necessary supplemental environmental analysis” for the modified plan and that the administration violated the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) and NEPA, and seeks to halt the mining plan until the administration has complied with the law.
Case Updates: Feb. 19, 2026:Judge Barlowgranted a motionallowing Peak Minerals, the owner of mineral leases at stake in Southern Utah Wilderness Alliance’s lawsuit against the government, to intervene in the case.

Case Name: Defenders of Wildlife v. Burgum(D. Alaska)3:25-cv-00319
Executive Action: Construction on Public Lands
Case Summary: On Jan. 20, 2025, President Trump issued Executive Order 14153, which directed the Secretary of the Dep’t of the Interior to take all necessary steps to develop a road between the community of King Cove and the airport in Cold Bay. The proposed road would cut through Izembek National Wildlife Refuge. On Oct. 21, 2025, the CEO of the King Cove Corp. (KCC) (an Alaska Native Village corporation) and the Secretary signed an agreement exchanging land within Izembek. On Nov. 12, 2025,threeseparatelawsuitswere filed challenging the Department of the Interior’s approval of the land exchange. In this case, plaintiff Defenders of Wildlife, brought suit against the Secretary of the U.S. Dep’t of the Interior, the Director of the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, and KCC. Plaintiff alleges that defendants violated the Refuge Act by agreeing to exchange land and they failed to carry out the conservation and subsistence principles provided for in the act. Plaintiff further alleges that defendants violated the Wilderness Act—which provides for a national wilderness preservation system—by enabling the construction of a permanent road and commercial enterprise within the Izembek Wilderness and by failing to uphold their obligations to preserve Izembek’s wilderness character. Plaintiff seeks to vacate the agreement, preliminary and permanent injunctive relief, and declaratory relief finding the agreement violated the previously stated federal statutes.

Case Name: Native Village of Hooper Bay v. Doug Burgum(D. Alaska)3:25-cv-00316
Executive Action: Construction on Public Lands
Case Summary: On Jan. 20, 2025, Trump issuedExecutive Order 14153, which directed the Secretary of the Department of the Interior to take all necessary steps to develop a road between the community of King Cove and the airport in Cold Bay. The proposed road would cut through Izembek National Wildlife Refuge. On Oct. 21, 2025, the CEO of the King Cove Corp. (KCC) (an Alaska Native Village corporation) and the Secretarysigned an agreementexchanging land within Izembek. On Nov. 12, 2025,threeseparatelawsuitswere filed challenging the Department of the Interior’s approval of a land exchange designed to facilitate construction of a road through the Izembek National Wildlife Refuge in Alaska. In this case, the plaintiffs, a group of native villages and the Center for Biological Diversity, brought suit, claiming the land exchange agreement violates several federal laws, specifically the Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act, the National Environmental Policy Act, and the Endangered Species Act, and that Defendants failed to follow proper process and procedures in entering that agreement. Plaintiffs allege the proposed land exchange may restrict subsistence resources crucial to Native Alaskan communities, adversely impact endangered species, and damage the refuge’s ecosystem. Plaintiffs a declaration that the Federal Defendants’ final agency acts violate federal law under the Administrative Procedure Act, invalidation of the agreement and the related opinion and analysis, and injunctive relief.

Case Name: Friends of Alaska National Wildlife Refuges v. Burgum(D. Alaska)3:25-cv-00318
Executive Action: Construction on Public Lands
Case Summary: On Oct. 21, 2025, Interior Secretary Doug Burgum signed an agreement to transfer protected lands within the Izembek National Wildlife Refuge in exchange for lands held by the King Cove Corporation (KCC), an Alaska native village corporation, for purposes of constructing a road connecting the communities of King Cove and Cold Bay, Alaska in the Aleutian Islands (the ‘Exchange Agreement’). Construction of a road through the Izembek Wilderness had previously been rejected on several occasions dating back to 1985 by prior Interior Secretaries, Congress, environmental assessors with the Fish and Wildlife Service or Army Corps of Engineers, and federal courts for environmental and administrative deficiencies. On Nov. 12, Plaintiffs filed a suit challenging the Exchange Agreement as procedurally and substantively deficient. Plaintiffs are several environmental nonprofits led by the Friends of Alaska National Wildlife Refuges, a volunteer organization based in Anchorage that advocates for stewardship of Alaska’ sixteen designated National Wildlife Refuges. Defendants are Interior Secretary Doug Burgum in his official capacity, the Department of the Interior itself, the U.S. Bureau of Land Management (BLM), BLM’s Alaska State Director Kevin Pendergast, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service (FWS), FWS’ Alaska Regional Director Sara Boario, and the King Cove Corporation. Plaintiffs allege the Exchange Agreement violates Title XI of the Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act (ANILCA), which requires that the creation of transportation systems through designated conservation units follow certain procedures, including Presidential recommendation and congressional approval, and that exchanges of protected land further the general purposes of ANILCA. Plaintiffs additionally assert that the Secretary’s Decision supporting the Agreement fails to provide a reasoned explanation of the agency’s departure from its prior findings that construction of a road through the Izembek Wilderness would harm wildlife and degrade the environment in violation of ANILCA. Plaintiffs also challenge the agency’s failure to conduct an environmental assessment as required by the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA). Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief and vacatur of the Exchange Agreement, the Secretary’s Decision justifying the agreement, and the related transactions of land rights between BLM and KCC.

Case Name: League v. Department of Interior(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00477
Executive Action: Construction on Public Lands
Case Summary: In October 2026, months after Interior Department leadership met with President Donald Trump to discuss redeveloping the public golf course at East Potomac Park in Washington, D.C. into a championship-level golf course, the administration began demolishing the East Wing of the White House and dumping its debris into East Potomac Park. The Administration claimed the dumping of debris was Categorically Excluded from National Environmental Protection Act (NEPA) environmental assessment requirements, and issued an assessment claiming compliance with the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA). In January 2026, Trump confirmed the Administration’s plan to redevelop East Potomac Park into “Washington National Golf Course.” On Feb. 13, 2026, DC Preservation League and local residents who are patrons of East Potomac Golf Coursesuedthe Department of the Interior and the National Park Service, seeking to block the administration from dumping debris at the Park and to halt its “Washington National” plans. Specifically, Plaintiffs allege the Washington National plans were adopted without reasoned explanation as required by the Administrative Procedure Act (APA). Plaintiffs further allege that Defendants’ Washington National plans and dumping at East Potomac Park violated Defendants’ obligations for environmental and historic site impact assessments under NEPA and NHPA, respectively, as well as their obligations for conservation and stewardship of East Potomac Park under both the National Park Service Organic Act and the Act of March 3, 1897, the statute that created East Potomac Park. Plaintiffs claim these statutory violations render the challenged actions unlawful and remediable under the APA and seek a declaration from the court of the unlawfulness of the administration’s actions.
Case Updates: May 4, 2026:Judge Reyesorderedthe Defendants “to begin compiling communications concerning Hains Point or golf courses in Washington, D.C.” including any communications between: (1) the National Mall and Memorial Parks (NAMA) and the National Garden of American Heroes Foundation; (2) NAMA and the White House; and (3) NAMA and National Links Trust, and to produce communications between NAMA Superintendent Kevin Griess and NAMA's Chief of Maintenance.

Case Name: Lemmon v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00544
Executive Action: Construction on Public Lands
Case Summary: On February 19, 2026, a group of private plaintiffs, including Vietnam War veterans, dipolmats, and a historic preservation expert, filed acomplaintto block President Donald Trump’s proposed 25-story “Independence Arch” on Memorial Circle between the Lincoln Memorial and Arlington National Cemetery in Washington, D.C. Plaintiffs allege that the administration directed construction on an accelerated schedule ahead of July 4, 2026, which harmed their personal and aesthetic interests in the historic viewshed between Arlington National Cemetery and the Lincoln Memorial.  They further allege that federal officials proceeded with plans to construct the monument in violation of statutory requirements governing federal commemorative works and historical and environmental review, namely legislative approval.  Plaintiffs allege that the administration violated the Commemorative Works Act 40 U.S.C. §§ 8901-8909, the National Historic Preservation Act 54 U.S.C. §§ 300101 et seq., and the National Environmental Policy Act 42 U.S.C. § 4321 et seq., and they assert that proceeding without the approvals and reviews required by those statutes is ultra vires.  Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief barring the government from proceeding with construction.  They have filed a preliminary injunction that is currently pending.

Case Name: Center for Biological Diversity v. Burgum(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04285
Executive Action: Modifications to National Parks Pass
Case Summary: On Dec. 10, 2025, the Center for Biological Diversity sued the Departments of the Interior (DOI) and Agriculture (DOA) and the National Park Service for placing pictures of President Donald Trump on national park passes in violation of the Federal Lands Recreation Enhancement Act (FLREA) and the Administrative Procedure Act (APA). FLREA dictates a number of public-participation requirements, including that the Secretaries hold an annual photography contest to select the winning image that will appear on the Annual Pass for that year. The official rules governing the contest require that the photo be taken on federal public lands or waters. In November, the DOI announced that the Annual Pass would feature an image of President Trump, rather than the contest-winning image of a national park. Plaintiffs allege this action disregards the public participation requirements of the statute, and betrays the expectations of the thousands of people who participate in the contest. Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief, and ask the court to remand Defendants’ decisions related to the 2026 national park passes, and enjoin Defendants’ use of any pass that unlawfully displays an image of President Trump unless and until the image is in compliance with the FLREA and the APA.

Case Name: Sovereign Inupiat for a Living Arctic v. Burgum(D. Alaska)3:25-cv-00356
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Environmental Impact Requirements
Case Summary: On Dec. 11, 2025, a coalition of community and non-profit organizations whose members live in and use the land in the National Petroleum Reserve-Alaska (Reserve)filed suitagainst the Department of the Interior (DOI) and the Bureau of Land Management (BLM), challenging BLM’s approval of ConocoPhillips Alaska, Inc.’s seismic and exploration oil drilling program (exploration program) within the Reserve. Plaintiffs allege that defendants failed to assure adequate mitigation impacts or provide maximum protection as required under the Naval Petroleum Reserves Act of 1976 (Reserves Act) and that the approval of the exploration program “without proper analysis or process” violated both the Reserves Act and the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as arbitrary and capricious and contrary to law. Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief and ask the court to declare the approval illegal, vacate approval of the exploration program, and compel defendants to comply with the APA and Reserves Act. On the same day plaintiffs filed amotion for a preliminary injunctionto enjoin implementation of the approval.
Case Updates: Dec. 29, 2025:The courtgrantedthe State of Alaska's unopposedmotion to interveneas Intervenor-Defendant.Dec. 29, 2025:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaint.Jan. 27, 2026:Judge GleasondeniedPlaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction.

Case Name: Center For Biological Diversity v. National Marine Fisheries Service(N.D. Cal.)4:25-cv-09109
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Environmental Impact Requirements
Case Summary: Two environmental organizations filed suit against the U.S. Coast Guard (USCG) and the National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) alleging violations of the Endangered Species Act (ESA) which requires the USCG, which regulates vessel traffic off the California coast, to consult with the NMFS to ensure that its actions do not endanger any protected species. They argue that USCG violates the ESA by failing to ensure that its actions, such as creating revised shipping lanes, do not jeopardize ESA-listed species, and further contend that because USCG’s previous biological opinion was vacated by a court in 2017 and USCG’s reliance on that biological opinion was declared unlawful, there is currently no valid biological opinion covering USCG’s actions. Plaintiffs also claim that because USCG’s routing measures are major federal actions under the National Environmental Policy Act, they violate that statute by failing to prepare an environmental impact statement. Plaintiffs further allege that NMFS violates the Administrative Procedures Act (APA) by unlawfully withholding and unreasonably delaying completion of the consultation on USCG’s actions. Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief.

Case Name: Center For Biological Diversity et al v. U.S. Department of Agriculture et al(N.D. Cal.)3:26-cv-00866
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Environmental Impact Requirements
Case Summary: On Jan. 28, 2026, the Center for Biological Diversity and the Sierra Club filed acomplaintagainst the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), USDA Secretary, Brooke L. Rollins, the United States Forest Service (USFS), USFS Chief, Tom Schultz, the Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service (APHIS), and APHIS Administrator Michael Watson, challenging a USDAinterim final rulerescinding regulations and procedures requiring public participation in USDA’s and its sub-agencies’ environmental reviews under the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA), as well as major amendments to its NEPA regulations, pursuant toExecutive Order 14154.  Plaintiffs allege that the USDA’s interim final rule constitutes a final rule, and therefore violated the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) by failing to proper notice-and-comment as required by statute.  Plaintiffs further allege that the new rule failed to provide a reasoned explanation for “the evisceration of public participation in NEPA reviews,” in violation of the APA.  Plaintiffs ask the court to declare that the interim final rule violates the APA and NEPA, vacate the interim final rule, and enjoin the USDA and its sub-agencies from changing their operations under the NEPA sub-policies without complying with the APA.

Case Name: Eagle County v. Department of the Interior(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01039
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Environmental Impact Requirements
Case Summary: On Jan. 20, 2025, President Trump issuedExecutive Order 14156,declaring a “national energy emergency.” In response, the Department of the Interior issued guidance on “Alternative Arrangements for NEPA Compliance” creating an expedited review procedure allowing energy project proponents to opt into a 14-day environmental review process without public notice and comment. Under the new guidance, the Bureau of Land Management (BLM) approved a right-of-way amendment authorizing the Wildcat Loadout Facility in Carbon County, Utah, to increase crude oil transloading capacity from 20,000 to 100,000 barrels per day, allegedly a roughly 400% increase.On March 26, 2026, Eagle County, Colorado filed acomplaintin the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia alleging that the expanded facility will significantly increase the number of oil trains traveling on the Union Pacific Line through Eagle County, adjacent to the Colorado River and through BLM-managed public lands, raising risks of derailments, oil spills, and wildfires. The complaint alleges that the new guidance is a substantive rule requiring notice-and-comment rulemaking under the Administrative Procedure Act and Federal Land Policy and Management Act; that Interior’s invocation of emergency procedures was unlawful under the APA, FLPMA, and NEPA because no qualifying “sudden, urgent, unusually unexpected” emergency existed; and that BLM’s authorization violated NEPA and the APA by failing to assess reasonably foreseeable indirect effects and arbitrarily reversing its prior commitment to public review and comment. The complaint also alleges BLM failed to issue a timely determination on Eagle County’s requests under the Freedom of Information Act. Plaintiffs seek declaratory judgments that the guidance and the expansion approval are unlawful, injunctive relief vacating both, and an order requiring BLM to produce FOIA-responsive records.

Case Name: Greater Hells Canyon Council v. United States Forest Service(D. Or.)2:26-cv-00673
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Environmental Impact Requirements
Case Summary: In May 2025, the United States Forest Service released a finalEnvironmental Impact Statement(EIS) for the Ellis Integrated Vegetation Project, a 110,000-acre vegetation-management  management project on the Umatilla National Forest in eastern Oregon. In Jan. 2026, the Forest Service released its finalRecord of Decision(ROD) for the project, which removed nearly all planned road closures and decommissioning contemplated by the EIS.Two Oregon environmental nonprofits filed acomplaintalleging that the Forest Service violated the National Forest Management Act (NFMA) by failing to ensure consistency with habitat standards; violated the APA by failing to explain the decision to remove road closures from the ROD; and violated the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) by failing to take a “hard look” at the selected action’s impacts and failing to prepare a supplemental EIS. Plaintiffs seek vacatur and remand of the ROD and final EIS, declaratory judgment that the Forest Service’s plan is illegal, and preliminary and permanent injunctions against implementation until the Forest Service complies with the relevant laws.

Case Name: National Parks Conservation Association v. U.S. Department of the Interior(C.D. Cal.)2:26-cv-04002
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Environmental Impact Requirements
Case Summary: In April 2025, the Bureau of Land Management (BLM)announcedthat the Department of Interior had authorized renewed mining operations at the Colosseum Mine within Mojave National Preserve, a 1.6-million-acre protected desert land in southernCalifornia.In an April 3, 2025 letter, the National Park Service (NPS) allegedly stated that the mine’s Australian operator, Dateline Resources Ltd., holds “valid existing rights” at Colosseum and declared that Dateline could rely on a plan of operations the BLM had approved in 1985. NPS also allegedly rescinded a series of prior directives issued between 2022 and 2023 that had ordered Dateline to cease operations pending approval of a new plan of operations and demanded over $200,000 for allegedly unauthorized operations that damaged Preserve resources.On April 15, 2026, the National Parks Conservation Association (NPCA), a nonprofit with members who live and work near near the mine site or use the preserve for recreation, filed acomplaintalleging that the April 2025 decision violates the Mining in the Parks Act, California Desert Protection Act, and National Environmental Policy Act, which require review and approval of operations plans before any mining may proceed. NPCA also alleges the decision is arbitrary and capricious under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) because it contradicts Defendants’ prior findings without reasoned explanation. Plaintiffs seek vacatur of the April 2025 decision, declaratory judgment that the decision was illegal, and an injunction barring any further operations pending compliance with applicable law.

Case Name: Center for Biological Diversity v. U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00043
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Endangered Species Act
Case Summary: On Jan. 8, 2025, the Center for Biological Diversitysuedthe administration for refusing to protect the Rio Grande cooter (a freshwater turtle) under the Endangered Species Act, arguing that climate change and current conditions place it at risk of extinction. The defendants are the United States Fish and Wildlife Service, its Director Brian Nesvik, and Secretary of the Department of the Interior Doug Burgum. The Center challenges the agency’s decision not to list the Rio Grande cooter, a species of turtle native to New Mexico, Texas, and northern Mexico, as threatened or endangered under the Endangered Species Act (16 U.S.C. §§ 1531–1544) (ESA) and asserts violations of the Administrative Procedure Act (5 U.S.C. § 706(2)) (APA). The Center alleges the Service’s decision was arbitrary, capricious, and contrary to law because the agency relied on flawed and unsupported risk metrics, irrationally excluded direct mortality and collection impacts from consideration, failed to incorporate or rationally address the best available science regarding climate change impacts and feminization of the species, and unlawfully limited its analysis of the species’ risk in significant portions of its range.The Center seeks declaratory and injunctive relief, including an order vacating the Service’s “not warranted” finding and requiring a new 12-month determination consistent with the ESA and APA.

Case Name: Center for Biological Diversity v. EPA(9th Cir.)26-298
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Endangered Species Act
Case Summary: On Nov. 19, 2025, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)approvedthe registration of isocycloseram as a new active ingredient and registered ten products containing the ingredient. Isocycloseram is used as a broad-spectrum insecticide. On Jan. 15, 2026, non-profits Center for Biological Diversity (CBD) and Center for Food Safetypetitionedthe Ninth Circuit to review and set aside the order. They argue that the chemical falls within the broader definitions of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), which may pose significant harm to the environment and animals. Petitioners allege that the order violates the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA), as the EPA may only register a pesticide if it is shown that the pesticide “will not generally cause unreasonable adverse effects on the environment.” Petitioners also argue that the order violates the Endangered Species Act (ESA), as the chemical could harm protected species. Petitioners seek a permanent injunction.

Case Name: Center for Biological Diversity v. United States Fish and Wildlife Service(W.D. Wash.)2:25-cv-02125
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Endangered Species Act
Case Summary: Coming soon.

Case Name: Center for Biological Diversity v. Burgum(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00940
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Endangered Species Act
Case Summary: On March 18, 2026, the Center for Biological Diversity filed acomplaintseeking to block Interior Secretary Doug Burgum from convening aMarch 31 meetingof the Endangered Species Committee, a cabinet-level panel colloquially known as the "God Squad" because it holds the extraordinary authority to permanently exempt federal actions from the Endangered Species Act's (ESA) protections. The committee wasdesignedas an administrative tribunal of last resort, and in the 48 years since its creation has adjudicated only three exemption applications. Plaintiff alleges Burgum is attempting to use the committee without meeting the ESA legal requirements to strip protections from all Gulf of Mexico oil and gas operations. Plaintiff further alleges that Burgum violated the ESA’s mandatory procedural requirements by publishing a single vague one-page Federal Register notice with 15 days’ notice, identifying no applicant, no specific agency action, and no public access to meeting records, and by proposing to restrict public attendance to a YouTube link rather than an open meeting as the statute requires. The complaint brings three claims — two APA violations for the procedurally defective notice and the unlawful convening of the Committee, and a mandamus claim compelling the Secretary to comply with the ESA's disclosure obligations — and seeks an injunction barring the March 31 meeting and any future Committee meetings that fail to satisfy the ESA's substantive and procedural prerequisites.

Case Name: Montana Wildlife Federation v. Burgum(D. Mont.)4:26-cv-00133
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Endangered Species Act
Case Summary: In December 2025, the U.S. Bureau of Land Management (BLM) issuednew plansamending its greater sage-grouse resource management plans for Montana and Wyoming. The amended plan allegedly eliminates the oil and gas leasing prioritization requirement that directed development away from sage-grouse habitat, removes the Sagebrush Focal Area designation and associated recommendation for a mineral withdrawal on 10 million acres of critical habitat, and weakens compensatory mitigation standards from a “net conservation benefit” to a “no net loss” standard, and removes the science-based Targeted Annual Warning System adaptive management framework, changes BLM allegedly made in response to state governors’ objections rather than conservation science.Three conservation organizations filed acomplaintin the U.S. District Court for the District of Montana challenging the Montana and Wyoming 2025 Plans. The complaint alleges that BLM violated the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA) by failing to manage for multiple use, sustained yield, and prevention of unnecessary or undue degradation; violated the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) by failing to analyze the environmental impacts of eliminating key habitat protections, the effectiveness of replacement mitigation measures, and the effect of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act's narrowing of BLM's leasing discretion on sage-grouse habitat; and acted arbitrarily and capriciously under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) by departing from the conservation framework that the U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service relied on in its 2015 finding that the Endangered Species Act listing of the sage-grouse was not warranted. Plaintiffs seek declaratory and permanent injunctive relief, vacatur of the 2025 Plans and supporting Environmental Impact Statement, and reinstatement of the 2015 Plans.

Case Name: Healthy Gulf v. Burgum(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01118
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Endangered Species Act
Case Summary: On March 31, 2026, the Endangered Species Committee (Committee)—comprised of seven high-ranking administration officials including the Secretaries of Interior, Defense, and Agriculture—unanimously voted to grant a sweeping categorical exemption (Exemption) to the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management (BOEM) and the Bureau of Safety and Environmental Enforcement (BSEE). The Exemption purports to excuse "all oil and gas activities" in the Gulf of America from the procedural and substantive requirements of the Endangered Species Act (ESA), and is based on a National Security Finding (Finding) issued by the Secretary of Defense on March 13, 2026, asserting that ongoing or future litigation threatens national security.A coalition of conservation nonprofits filed acomplainton April 2, 2026 against the Endangered Species Committee and the Secretary of Defense. Plaintiffs assert that by granting the Exemption, the Committee unlawfully bypassed mandatory ESA procedures, including public hearings and formal reports–which serve as safeguards necessary to protect essential Gulf ecosystems. The complaint alleges this decision to grant the Exemption for offshore oil and gas activities in the Gulf of America is contrary to law and ultra vires or in excess of authority, in violation of the ESA and Administrative Procedure Act (APA). Plaintiffs also argue the Finding is legally deficient as it provides no evidence of irreconcilable conflict between species protection and oil production. The complaint asserts that the Finding is thus arbitrary and capricious and contrary to the ESA, in violation of the ESA and APA. Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief and vacatur of both the Finding and Exemption.

Case Name: Center for Biological Diversity v. Burgum(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01172
Executive Action: Noncompliance with Endangered Species Act
Case Summary: The coal darter (Percina brevicauda) is a small freshwater fish native to the Black Warrior, Cahaba, and Coosa river systems of Alabama’s Mobile River basin. According to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service’s (FWS) December 2023proposed listingof the fish as a threatened species, the species’ range has contracted from more than 355 to approximately 189 river miles, due to habitat loss from dams, degraded water quality from agricultural and mining runoff, and climate change.The Center for Biological Diversity filed acomplainton April 7, 2026 against Interior Secretary Douglas Burgum, FWS Director Brian Nesvik, and FWS. The complaint alleges that Defendants violated their nondiscretionary statutory duty under the Endangered Species Act by failing to publish a final listing determination within one year of the December 2023 proposed rule. Plaintiff seeks a declaratory judgment that Defendants violated the ESA, an injunction compelling FWS to issue a final listing determination by a date certain, and an award of attorneys’ fees and costs under 16 U.S.C. § 1540(g)(4).

Case Name: Healthy Gulf v. U.S. Department of the Interior(11th Cir.)26-11284
Executive Action: Offshore Oil and Gas Drilling
Case Summary: A coalition of environmental groups filed apetitionfor the Eleventh Circuit to review the Department of Interior and Bureau of Ocean Energy Management’s approval of BP’s plans for the Kaskida Project, an ultra-deepwater oil drilling project in the Gulf of Mexico. Per the Petitioners’press release, BP’s proposal is missing legally required information, issignificantly flawed, and underestimates “the volume of a worst-case oil spill by at least half-a-million barrels of oil.”

Case Name: Gwich'in Steering Committee v. United States Department of the Interior(D. Alaska)3:20-cv-00204
Executive Action: Permitting Oil Drilling in Protected Lands or Wildlife Refuges
Case Summary: On Jan. 27, 2026, a tribal organization and several environmental non-profits filed an amended and supplementalcomplaintto renew litigationoriginally commencedin 2020. The 2020 decision challenged the Secretary of the U.S. Department of the Interior’s (DOI Secretary) signing of the record of decision (ROD) for the Coastal Plain Oil and Gas Leasing Program, which opened up the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge (ANWAR) to future oil and gas leasing, exploration, development, and transportation. The case wasstayedin 2021 with a change in administration, but since then, the administration has taken actions to allow for oil drilling across the coastal plain of the ANWAR. These actions include the Department of Interior’s issuance of a new ROD on Oct. 23, 2025 adopting a new Leasing Program. Plaintiffs allege violations of the Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act, 16 U.S.C. §§ 3101–3233; the National Wildlife System Administration Act (Refuge Act), 16 U.S.C. §§ 668dd–668ee; Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (Tax Act), the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA), 42 U.S.C. §§ 4321–4370j; the Wilderness Act, 16 U.S.C. §§ 1131–1136; the Endangered Species Act (ESA), 16 U.S.C. §§ 1531-1544; and the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), 5 U.S.C. §§ 701–706. Plaintiffs seek vacatur and declaratory and injunctive relief against the Department of the Interior, the Bureau of Land Management, and the Fish & Wildlife Service.

Case Name: State of California v. Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration(9th Cir.)26-508
Executive Action: Assertion of Federal Jurisdiction Over Oil Pipelines
Case Summary: On Dec. 17, 2025, the Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration (PHMSA) issued the first of three orders concerning the Las Flores Pipelines (Pipelines), which originate in Santa Barbara County, California, and terminate in Kern County, California. This order purported to assume exclusive federal jurisdiction over these two pipelines that historically have been under the control of California’s Office of the State Fire Marshal (OFSM). On Dec. 22, 2025, PHMSA issued an order approving a Restart Plan for the Pipelines without the completion of required repairs and remediation to the pipelines or compliance with OSFM’s alternative conditions. On Dec. 23, 2025, PHMSA issued a permit waiving compliance with federal pipeline safety regulations.On Jan. 23, 2026, the State of California filed apetitionfor the Ninth Circuit to review these three orders issued by PHMSA. Under the Pipeline Safety Act, which permits the review of orders issued by PHMSA, California challenges the three orders, which reclassified the Pipelines as “interstate” instead of “intrastate” and approved their restart. The petition alleges that the orders are in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A) & (D), as arbitrary and capricious, and the State seeks vacatur of the orders.

Case Name: State of California v. Wright(N.D. Cal.)3:26-cv-02500
Executive Action: Assertion of Federal Jurisdiction Over Oil Pipelines
Case Summary: On March 13, 2026, Secretary Christopher Wright of the Department of Energy, issued thePipeline Capacity Prioritization and Allocation Order(PCPAO) invoking the Defense Production Act (DPA) to direct a private oil company to immediately restart oil transportation through the Santa Ynez Pipeline System, which had been closed indefinitely following an oil spill on Refugio State Beach in 2015. On March 14, the company resumed operations pursuant to the PCPAO, which allegedly violated both a federal consent decree and a preliminary injunction issued by a California State Court. On March 23, California Attorney General Robert Bonta filed acomplaintagainst Secretary Wright and the Department of Energy, challenging the PCPAO as exceeding the DPA’s statutory authority and arbitrary and capricious in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA); purporting to override binding judicial orders in violation of the Separation of Powers; and commandeering state property without the required congressional authorization in violation of the Tenth Amendment. California seeks vacatur of the Wright Order, a declaratory judgment that the Wright Order is unconstitutional and unlawful, and a permanent injunction barring Defendants from invoking it to authorize pipeline operations without state approval and compliance with applicable court orders.
Case Updates: March 24, 2026:The Courtorderedto show cause why case should not be dismissed for improper venue, finding California's challenge to the Energy Secretary's DPA pipeline restart order was likely filed in the wrong district given the pipelines at issue are located in the Central and/or Eastern Districts; California has one week to respond or refile.March 30, 2026:Plaintiffvoluntarily dismissedthis action without prejudice so they can refile in the United States District Court in a different judicial district of California.

Case Name: State of California v. United States of America(N.D. Ca.)4:25-cv-04966
Executive Action: Vehicle Standards Preemption
Case Summary: Between April 2023 and January 2025, the State of California obtained three waivers from the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) which allowed California to promulgate stricter emissions standards and requirements concerning zero-emission vehicles than those imposed under, and that would otherwise be preempted by, the Clean Air Act (CAA). The waivers, which were granted under a long-standing provision in the CAA designed by Congress to ensure that California could continue to promulgate its own strict emission standards, allowed California to develop regulations to strengthen the sales of zero-emission cars and trucks. On January 20, 2025, President Trump signed Executive Order (EO) 14096, titled “Unleashing American Energy”, which declared that “state emissions waivers that function to limit sales of gasoline-powered automobiles” should be “terminat[ed] where appropriate.” Consistent with this EO, in February 2025, the EPA announced that it would retroactively submit the three waivers to Congress as rules for consideration under the Congressional Review Act, 5 U.S.C. §§ 801–808, (CRA) and subsequently Congress passed, and Trump signed, joint resolutions (the “Resolutions”) purporting to disapprove the waivers, thereby preventing the affected states from enforcing their own vehicle emissions standards.A coalition of 11 states who have adopted the stricter standards filed suit against the EPA, claiming that the use of the CRA by Congress to invalidate the prior EPA decisions granting California waivers reversed decades of consistent statutory interpretation, because the EPA’s waivers are adjudicatory orders, not federal rules, and thus outside the scope of the CRA. Plaintiffs allege that the defendants’ actions in invoking the CRA without any legal rationale or administrative process, and in opposition to determinations by the Government Accountability Office and the Senate Parliamentarian, are ultra vires as conduct in excess of statutory authority, contrary to law, and arbitrary and capricious under the Administrative Procedure Act. The plaintiffs also contend that the resolution process violated foundational constitutional principles, including separation of powers, the Take Care Clause, and the Tenth Amendment, and seek declaratory and injunctive relief to vacate the Resolutions, restore the validity of the disputed waivers, declare the reclassification of the waiver decisions as ultra vires, unconstitutional, and unlawful, and prevent enforcement of the Resolutions against their vehicle emission programs.
Case Updates: Dec. 2, 2025:The courtdeniedseveral motions to intervene filed by trade groups and the state of Texas, stating that the issue before the court is only as to whether the use of the CRA to disapprove the waivers was appropriate, and that therefore arguments that the proposed intervenors might face challenges if the plaintiffs’ emissions standards are implemented “are ancillary to the actual issues raised in this case.”Jan. 23, 2026:Several tradegroupsappealedthe district court’s Dec. 2, 2025rulingto the Ninth Circuit.

Case Name: CleanAIRE NC v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00233
Executive Action: Exemption from Emission Standards
Case Summary: On July 17, 2025, President Donald Trump issued aproclamationgranting forty commercial sterilizers a two-year exemption from strengthened emission standards (Sterilizer Proclamation). These emission standards, referred to as the EtO Rule, were implemented by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to reduce the severe health risks, including cancer, associated with ethylene oxide exposure, especially for populations residing near these industrial sites. On Jan. 28, 2026, a group of environmental nonprofit organizations filedsuitagainst Trump, the EPA, and EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin, challenging the Sterilizer Proclamation. The complaint argues that this blanket exemption is ultra vires and unlawfully exceeds the President’s authority under the Clean Air Act, asserting that Trump’s findings that compliance technology is “not available” and that the exception serves “national security interests” are factually unsupported and pretextual. Plaintiffs specifically point out that the necessary control technologies are widely available, and several exempted facilities are already in compliance. Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief to invalidate the Sterilizer Proclamation and ensure the forty facilities remain subject to the EtO Rule’s original compliance deadlines.

Case Name: Massachusetts v. EPA(D.C. Cir.)26-1061
Executive Action: Exemption from Emission Standards
Case Summary: A coalition of 22 states and territories, the Governor of Pennsylvania, and 12 cities and counties filed apetitionfor review in the D.C. Circuit seeking judicial review of the EPA’s Feb. 18, 2026final rulerescinding the 2009 Endangerment Finding, which had determined that greenhouse gases endanger public health and welfare, and repealing all related vehicle greenhouse gas emission standards. EPA justified the action on grounds that the Clean Air Act does not authorize regulation of emissions in response to global climate change, that the major questions doctrine requires clear congressional authorization for such a program, and that greenhouse gas emission standards for new motor vehicles have de minimis impact on global temperature and sea level trends. Petitioners seek vacatur of the final rule.
Case Updates: March 20, 2026:The courtconsolidatedAmerican Public Health Association v. EPA, 26-1037, withMassachusetts v. EPA, 26-1061. These cases will proceed underAmerican Public Health Association v. EPA.

Case Name: Conserve Southwest Utah v. U.S. Department of the Interior(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00317
Executive Action: Construction on Public Lands
Case Summary: On January 21, 2026, the Trump administration granted the Utah Department of Transportation (UDOT) a right-of-way (ROW) to construct a four-lane highway through the Congressionally-designated Red Cliffs National Conservation Area (NCA). A similar ROW had been granted during the first administration, but the construction never began as environmental litigation halted the action. With the reissuance, on Feb. 4, 2026, a group of environmental non-profits filed acomplaintagainst the Department of the Interior, the Bureau of Land Management (BLM), and the Fish and Wildlife Service. Plaintiffs are requesting that the approval of the 2026 Decision Record and reissuance of the ROW grant be reversed and blocked by the court as violating the Omnibus Public Land Management Act (OPLMA), the Land and Water Conservation Fund Act (LWCFA), the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA), the Endangered Species Act (ESA), and the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), specifically arguing the administration’s actions are arbitrary and capricious in violation of the APA, 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A) and (D). Plaintiffs allege that the administration’s actions will cause dangers to critical habitats and endangered species therein, and historic properties and contain inconsistent legal directions provided. Plaintiffs request that the court hold unlawful and vacate the 2026 ROW Decision and related agency opinions.
Case Updates: March 1, 2026:Judge Randolph Daniel MossgrantedPlaintiffs' motion for preliminary injunction.

Case Name: Center for Biological Diversity v. Germann(D. Mont.)4:26-cv-00021
Executive Action: Federal Land Use and Environmental Rollbacks
Case Summary: In late 2025, the Bureau of Land Management (BLM) approved Resource Management Plan (RMP) Amendments (the 2025 Amendments) concerning the greater sage-grouse and its habitats. In 2015, BLM enacted land use plans designed to avoid the greater sage-grouse’s designation as an endangered or threatened species under the Endangered Species Act. The 2025 Amendments depart from the 2015 plans by, for example, easing restrictions on oil and gas development or livestock grazing in sage-grouse habitats. The Center for Biological Diversity, the Sierra Club, and local environmental groups brought suit against the BLM Directors from states across the greater sage-grouse’s range who signed off on the 2025 Amendments in their official capacity, arguing the amendments would interfere with Plaintiffs and their members’ use and enjoyment of the affected habitat and ability to observe and photograph the sage-grouse and other wildlife. Plaintiffs argue that the 2025 Amendments are arbitrary and capricious under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), and that BLM violated the APA by failing to offer reasoned explanation for the Amendments’ departure from the 2015 plans. Plaintiffs further argue the Amendments violate BLM’s positive obligations under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA) to sustain wildlife species and its environmental review obligations under the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA). Plaintiffs seek to have the Amendments set aside as arbitrary and capricious, and request injunctive relief ordering the Bureau to undertake NEPA review and a new land use planning process.

Case Name: Montana Environmental Information Center v. Burgum(D. Mont.)1:26-cv-00021
Executive Action: Federal Land Use and Environmental Rollbacks
