Plaintiffs allege that abruptly terminating or refusing to continue multi-year grants mid-project disrupts tutoring, mentoring, afterschool programs, and other school-based services, causing operational and educational harms to students, schools, and community partners, as well as the permanent loss of congressionally appropriated funds if they expire unspent.Plaintiffs further allege that ED’s actions are arbitrary and capricious, contrary to law, and constitute agency action unlawfully withheld or unreasonably delayed by failing to obligate appropriated funds and by altering grant continuation policies without required procedures in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) 5 U.S.C. §§ 706(1), 706(2)(A). Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief vacating the challenged actions, requiring the Department to obligate the appropriated funds and continue the grants consistent with statutory requirements, (and if necessary, extend their period of availability), and awarding fees and costs.  In the alternative, Plaintiffs seek a writ of mandamus to compel the agency to perform its statutory duties.
Case Updates: Dec. 31, 2025:In a minute order, the court denied Plaintiffs’ motion for a temporary restraining order (TRO) as moot after Defendants filed a declaration representing to the court that the entire amount of disputed FSCS funds has been obligated.Feb. 27, 2026:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaint, reflecting additional plaintiffs joining the action and more detailed factual allegations concerning the ultimate impact of challenged non-continuance of FSCS grants. The amended complaint adds claims that ED’s action violated the First Amendment’s protections of free association and against viewpoint discrimination, but removed the claim that ED’s actions constitute agency action unlawfully withheld or unreasonably delayed in violation of the APA.

Case Name: State of Maryland v. United States Department of Education(D. Md.)1:25-cv-04298
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: On Dec. 30, 2025, two states and the District of Columbiafiled suitagainst the U.S. Department of Education (ED) and the U.S. Secretary of Education, Linda McMahon, challenging the decision to discontinue grants awarded under the Full-Service Community School (FSCS) grant programs. The FSCS grant programs provide funding to nonprofit organizations, local school districts, and universities to support the planning, implementation, and operation of full-service community schools, and make continuation awards after one year based on grantees’ performance. The complaint alleges that on Dec. 12, 2025, ED issued Notices of Non-continuation of Grant Award (Non-continuation Decisions) to FSCS grantees in each Plaintiff state stating that it would not continue funding beyond the first year based on a purported conflict with the current administration’s priorities. Plaintiffs allege that the ED’s actions violate the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as the agency failed to adhere to its own continuation regulations, 34 C.F.R. 75.253, which require that grant continuation decisions be based on published priorities issued through notice and comment rulemaking and decided solely on grantee performance. They further allege a violation of the APA as arbitrary and capricious, particularly due to a violation of the “change-in-position doctrine”, as well as the Spending Clause. Plaintiffs seek preliminary and permanent injunctions barring the ED from discontinuing FSCS grant program funding for the approved budget years based on the reasoning in the Non-Continuation Decision and from implementing or enforcing new grant conditions based on ED priorities not in effect when the grants were originally awarded, and requiring the ED to make continuation decisions based solely on grantee performance.

Case Name: State of New York v. Administration for Children and Families(S.D.N.Y.)1:26-cv-00172
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: In January 2026, the Administration for Children and Families (ACF), Assistant Secretary Alex J. Adams, of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), and Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. (the Defendants) froze all federal funding for three social services and child care programs for low-income families and individuals with disabilities in the states of New York, Illinois, California, Colorado, and Minnesota. On Jan. 8, 2026, the five states (the Plaintiffs)filed suitagainst the Defendants, alleging that they abruptly halted funding for the programs under the pretext of rooting out potential fraud, without statutory authority, any substantiated evidence, or compliance with the specific procedural requirements established by Congress for sanctioning states. Plaintiffs further claim was imposed in a discriminatory and arbitrary manner on “Democrat-led states.” Plaintiffs allege funding freeze exceeds statutory authority and is contrary to law, and arbitrary and capricious agency action under the Administrative Procedure Act. Plaintiffs also allege that the funding freeze violates the separation of powers, and the Appropriations Clause and the Spending Clause of the Constitution. Plaintiffs argue that the Executive Branch’s refusal to disburse congressionally appropriated funds constitutes unlawful impoundment and usurpation of Congress’s exclusive authority. Plaintiffs are seeking a court order that the funding freeze exceeds statutory authority, is contrary to law and arbitrary and capricious in violation of the APA, and is unconstitutional andultra vires. Plaintiffs also request that the court vacate and block the funding freeze and agency letters implementing the funding freeze and restore program funding.
Case Updates: Jan. 9, 2026:The courtgrantedPlaintiff States’ emergency motion for a temporary restraining order (“TRO”), blocking the Administration for Children and Families from implementing its funding freeze on the Child Care Development Fund, TANF, and Social Services Block Grants, and ordering the agency to immediately lift all non-statutory restrictions on states’ ability to draw down those funds.Jan. 23, 2026:Judge Broderickextendedthe TRO until Feb. 6, 2026.Feb. 6, 2026:Judge BroderickgrantedPlaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction and stay, enjoining and staying Defendants from implementing the ACF funding policy at issue and directing Defendants to immediately remove any restrictions outside of permitted statutory authority on Plaintiffs’ ability to draw down funds from the relevant child care and social services programs.March 10, 2026:The courtgranteda preliminary injunction and stay blocking HHS and ACF’s conditional freeze of approximately $10 billion in CCDF, TANF, and SSBG funds to Plaintiff States, finding them likely to succeed on APA grounds because ACF’s letters were arbitrary and capricious and contrary to law. The court also denied the government's request for a nearly $300 million bond, finding that imposing one would have an impermissible chilling effect on states' rights to judicial review of unlawful agency action.

Case Name: State of New York et al v. U.S. Department of Health and Human Services(D.R.I)1:26-cv-00022
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: On Jan. 20, 2025, President Trump issuedExecutive Order (EO) 14,168titled “Defending Women from Gender Ideology Extremism and Restoring Biological Truth to the Federal Government” which declares that “[i]t is the policy of the United States to recognize two sexes, male and female. These sexes are not changeable and are grounded in fundamental and incontrovertible reality.” The EO directs federal agencies to “assess grant conditions and grantee preferences and ensure grant funds do not promote gender ideology,” as defined in the EO, and orders agencies to “take all necessary steps, as permitted by law, to end the Federal funding of gender ideology.” Pursuant to the EO, starting in May, 2025 the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) and its sub-agencies informed States receiving federal funds for federal health, education, and research grants that as part of the Title IX certification process they were required to certify they are in compliance with the EO (the “Gender Conditions”). On Jan. 13, 2026, 12 statesfiled suit, alleging that the new guidance is vague, scientifically inaccurate and uncertain, potentially in conflict with Plaintiff States’ own civil rights laws, and violates the Administrative Procedures Act (APA) as action that is arbitrary and capricious as without observance of procedure, contrary to law, and in excess of the agency’s authority. They further allege violations of the Spending Clause and Separation of Powers by usurping Congress’s spending authority. They seek a declaration that the imposition of the Gender Conditions is unlawful and unconstitutional, and injunctive relief to vacate the new requirements and enjoin their implementation.

Case Name: National Family Planning and Reproductive Health Association v. Kennedy(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01265
Case Status: Case Closed in Favor of Plaintiff
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: On April 24, 2025, National Family Planning & Reproductive Health Association (NFPRHA) filed acomplaintagainst U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. and other health officials challenging the HHS March 31 decision to temporarily withhold the fiscal year 2025 continuation grants of 16 Title X grantees pursuant to 45 C.F.R. § 75.371(a).  These grants represent the year-four funding of five-year competitive awards and constitute approximately 25 percent of all Title X grant awards.  NFPRHA represents 14 of the 16 grantees.  HHS premised the withholding of grant funding on "possible violations" of federal civil rights laws (Title VI and Title VII) andExecutive Order 14218("Ending Taxpayer Subsidization of Open Borders") based on public statements from the grantees' websites regarding diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) and opposition to racism.  HHS also cited concerns that some members might be providing care to undocumented immigrants in violation of the EO.Plaintiff alleges that the withholding of grant funding is unlawful because 45 C.F.R. § 75.371(a) only authorizes such action if the Agency has determined that a grantee failed to comply with federal requirements and that such noncompliance cannot be remedied by additional conditions and that HHS made no such determination prior to withholding the funds.  Plaintiff further alleges that the action violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as it is arbitrary and capricious, was taken without advance notice or an opportunity for voluntary corrective action, and ignored the devastating impact on public health.  Plaintiff seeks a judgment declaring that the Agency's withholding of grant funding was unlawful and in violation of the APA and HHS regulations. They request a preliminary and permanent injunction to prohibit HHS from withholding Title X funds without providing proper notice of violations and an opportunity for corrective action.  Plaintiff also requests that the court vacate and set aside the March 31 decision letters.
Case Updates: March 31, 2025:HHS issued letters to sixteen grantees abruptly withholding their 2025 grants effective April 1, based on suspected non-compliance with civil rights laws and EO 14218.April 1, 2025:Fiscal year 2025 funding was scheduled to begin; funds remained withheld.April 24, 2025:NFPRHA filed a complaint for declaratory and injunctive relief in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia.July 15, 2025:Judge Ana C. Reyes held a pre-motion conference and established a briefing schedule for summary judgment.July 22, 2025:Plaintiff filed a motion for an order directing Defendants to hold the disputed funds pending a resolution on the merits.July 28, 2025:The parties filed cross-motions for summary judgment.August 14, 2025:The Court held a motion hearing regarding the cross-motions for summary judgment and took the matters under advisement.September 12, 2025:Judge Reyes granted Plaintiff’s motion to hold funds, ensuring the grant money remained available during the litigation.Jan. 13, 2026:The National Family Planning and Reproductive Health Association (NFPRHA) voluntarilydismissedits lawsuit following the government’srestorationof all Title X family planning grants that were the subject of NFPRHA’s litigation.

Case Name: Porwancher v. National Endowment for the Humanities(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01180
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: On April 3, 2025, the National Endowment for Humanities (NEH) conducted a mass termination of grants, under direction from the Trump Administration’s Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE). Included in this mass-termination was a Public Scholar grant awarded to Andrew Porwancher, a Professor of History at Arizona State University, and the termination notice stated that funding the Public Scholar grant he was awarded to write a book on the topic of combatting antisemitism was not aligned with the agency’s priorities or the Trump Administration’s agenda. On April 16, 2025, Plaintiff Andrew Porwanchersuedthe NEH after his grant was mass-terminated without individualized review, arguing the NEH’s action was arbitrary and capricious in violation of the Administrative Procedures Act (APA), violated federal grant procedures, and harmed his career. Porwancher filed a first amended complaint on June 2, 2025, making additional claims pursuant to the First Amendment. He seeks an injunction reinstating his Public Scholar grant, prohibiting the funds intended for his grant from being reallocated by the agency, and prohibiting future termination outside the bounds of applicable laws and regulations.
Case Updates: July 25, 2025:Judge Carl J. NicholsgrantedPorwancher’s June 2, 2025motionfor a preliminary injunction preventing the NEH from treating the grant as terminated and enjoining future actions regarding the grant unless they comply with procedural requirements under applicable regulations.

Case Name: Gateway Development Commission v. United States(Fed. Cl.)1:26-cv-00176
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: On Feb. 2, 2026, the Gateway Development Commission, a NY/NJ bi-state authority, filed acomplaintagainst the United States for breach of the grant and loan agreements associated with the Hudson Tunnel Project, alleging that the Department of Transportation unlawfully suspended over $205 million in reimbursements beginning Sept. 30, 2025. The Department of Transportation allegedly halted funding pending a compliance review under new federal contracting rules. The complaint contends that the Department of Transportation’s withholding previously approved federal funding under executed infrastructure financing agreements has caused immediate financial and operational harms to Plaintiff that disrupt cash-flow and threaten project continuity. On Jan. 27, 2026, Plaintiff announced it had exhausted available cash and credit and that construction would pause absent restoration of federal funding, and on Feb. 6, 2026, construction on the Project was suspended, prompting legal and political reactions. The complaint asserts breach of contract claims for the grants DOT paused funding for and the loan agreements DOT has failed to comply with. Plaintiff seeks a monetary judgment compelling the United States to pay the withheld amounts owed under the grants and loan agreements.
Case Updates: March 12, 2026:Judge Hertlingdismissedthe first six counts of Plaintiffs’ complaint,reportedlytelling parties during oral argument that the government’s reimbursement to the Gateway Development Commission rendered the claims moot. The court’smemorandum opinionwas filed concurrently under seal pending the parties’ proposed redactions. Two counts seeking reimbursement for additional costs remain pending. Judge Hertling did not dismiss two counts of the complaint that sought reimbursement for additional costs.

Case Name: New Jersey v. Department of Transportation(S.D.N.Y.)1:26-cv-00939
Case Status: Government Action Not Blocked Pending Appeal
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: Coming soon.On Feb. 3, 2026, the states of New York and New Jersey filedsuitagainst the administration over itsdecisionto suspend federal loans and grants for the Hudson Tunnel Project. Alleging arbitrary and unlawful action under the Administrative Procedure Act, the states claim that the stated reason for the suspension — review of compliance with regulations related to contracts with disadvantaged business enterprises — was in reality political retribution against the states.
Case Updates: Feb. 6, 2026:Judge Vargasgranteda temporary restraining order, enjoining Defendants from implementing the suspension of federal disbursements for the Hudson Tunnel Project pending the hearing of Plaintiffs’ application for a preliminary injunction.Feb. 8, 2026:Defendants filed anotice of interlocutory appealto the Second Circuit.Feb. 9, 2026:Judge VargasdeniedDefendants’ motion for a stay of the court’s TRO pending appeal but grantedsua spontea short administrative stay to allow Defendants to seek a stay from the Second Circuit.Feb. 11, 2026:Judge VargasdeniedPlaintiffs’motion for reconsiderationof the administrative stay.

Case Name: County of Santa Clara v. Noem(N.D. Cal.)3:25-cv-08330
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: On April 18, 2025, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) issued new Standard Terms and Conditions for Fiscal Year 2025, which imposed new conditions required for a range of grants administered by DHS and Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA). These conditions require grant recipients to comply with federal antidiscrimination laws (including those that ban DEI), to agree to cooperate with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and other immigration enforcement activities, and to agree to comply with all executive orders (EOs) President Donald Trump has issued and might issue in the future.On Aug. 20, 2025, DHS and FEMA published the FEMA Preparedness Grants Manual, which repeats and implements these binding requirements on grantees of FEMA’s preparedness grant programs. DHS and FEMA have also adopted a requirement that prohibits any grantee that has been designated by the administration as a “Sanctuary Jurisdiction” from making financial obligations under grants and subgrants, and prohibits grantees from issuing subgrants or payments under subgrants to any other designated “Sanctuary Jurisdiction.” On Sep. 30, a collective of 29 cities and counties in Western states sued DHS, FEMA, and their respective head executives, challenging the imposition of these new conditions on disaster and terrorism-related funding that the cities and counties have received or expect to receive. Plaintiffs argue that these new conditions violate the separation of powers; violate the Constitution’s restrictions on Congress’ spending power; are ambiguous and lack a reasonable relationship to the grants at issue; require Plaintiffs and other grant recipients to violate constitutional rights; are arbitrary and capricious, contrary to the Constitution, and in excess of statutory authority, in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA). Plaintiffs request that the court declare that the new conditions and their implementation are unlawful, prevent the Defendants from imposing the conditions, and prevent the Defendants from retaliating against the Plaintiffs for this lawsuit.
Case Updates: Oct. 7, 2025:The case wasorderedrelated toCity and County of San Francisco v. Donald J. Trump, et al.(N.D. Cal.) Case No. 3:25-cv-01350. As a result, the case was reassigned to Judge William Orrick.Nov. 21, 2025:Judge Orrickgrantedplaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction, temporarily preventing Defendants from withholding, freezing, or restricting grant funds based on the conditions requiring compliance with federal antidiscrimination laws and Trump’s EOs.Jan. 20, 2026:Defendantsappealedto the Ninth Circuit (Case No.26-402).Jan. 20, 2026:Judge Orrickdeniedthe government’s motion to dismiss.Jan. 30, 2026:The Ninth Circuit appellate proceedings were stayed pending the decision inCounty of King v. Turner(No. 25-3664).March 17, 2026:The case was ordered related toCity of Fresno v. Noem(N.D. Cal.) Case No. 5:26-cv-01535.

Case Name: City of Fresno v. Turner(N.D. Cal.)3:25-cv-07070
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: In January 2025, the Trump Administration issued a series of executive orders directing federal agencies—including the Departments of Housing and Urban Development (HUD), Transportation (DOT), Health and Human Services (HHS), and the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)—to impose new sweeping conditions on the award and administration of federal grants to states, cities, and local government authorities. These executive orders included prohibitions on diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) initiatives; mandates to facilitate federal immigration enforcement and restrict benefits to undocumented individuals; prohibitions on “promoting” gender ideology and elective abortion; and requirements related to federal nondiscrimination law compliance. Beginning in early 2025, these agencies began attaching the new conditions to a wide array of grants upon application or renewal, with the agencies warning grantees that funding would be withheld or clawed back unless the grantees agreed to accept the new requirements.On August 18, 2025, HUD sent an email to the City of Fresno, warning that unless Fresno removed all references to “equity,” “environmental justice,” transgender individuals, and provided assurances against “promoting gender ideology” as defined in Executive Order 14168, its Community Development Block Grant funds could be disapproved. Fresno and several other local entities faced imminent loss of millions in federal funds for refusing to make these changes. On August 20, 2025, these cities and counties filed acomplaintagainst the head of HUD and other government entities, alleging that the government’s demands are facially unlawful and expose the cities to legal liability for discrimination suits, with the alternative being the loss of millions in previously awarded grants. Plaintiffs brought claims based on the Separation of Powers, the Spending Clause, the Tenth Amendment, and the Fifth Amendment Due Process for vagueness. Plaintiffs also brought multiple claims under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) for arbitrary and capricious rules, rules that are contrary to the Constitution, rules that are in excess of statutory authority, agency action contrary to regulations (for the HUD grant conditions only), and agency action without procedure required by law (for the HUD, Federal Transit Administration (FTA), and Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) grant conditions). Plaintiffs sought declaratory and injunctive relief.Aug. 27, 2025:Judge Seeborg filed anordergranting Plaintiffs’ motion for a temporary restraining order (TRO).Sept. 8, 2025:Plaintiffs submitted anamended complaintalongside a motion for a preliminary injunction. The complaint added new plaintiffs and relevant facts regarding those plaintiffs’ funding dependencies. The complaint also provides more detail regarding government policies and implementation.
Case Updates: Sept. 23, 2025:Judge Seeborg filed anordergranting Plaintiffs’ preliminary injunction.Jan. 16, 2026:After Defendants appealed the court’s grant of the Plaintiffs’ preliminary injunction, the Ninth Circuit filed anordergranting Defendants’ unopposed motion to hold the case in abeyance pending the Ninth Circuit’s resolution of the appeal inCounty of King v. Turner, No. 25-3664 (9th Cir.).Feb. 9, 2026:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaintpursuant to a joint stipulation with Defendants. This complaint again added new plaintiffs and two additional government defendants (sub-agencies of the Department of Transportation). With the addition of these defendants, Plaintiffs expanded the scope of their factual allegations to include other threatened sources of funding.April 28, 2026:Chief District Judge Seeborn issued anorderdenying Defendant’s motion to dismiss or in the alternative transfer and granting in part and denying in part Plaintiff’s motion for preliminary injunction. The court rejected the government’s arguments of claim-splitting, venue, and justiciability, and extended injunctive relief to the additional plaintiffs. However, the court refused to enter a stay of the agency action, since the preliminary injunction granted complete relief.

Case Name: State of California v. U.S. Department of Education(N.D. Cal.)3:26-cv-01259
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: On March 27, 2025, the U.S. Department of Education (ED) informed the California Department of Education (CDE) that it was opening an investigation into whether local educational agencies (LEAs) in California were violating the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA), which requires educational agencies to disclose a student’s educational records to their parents only at the parents’ affirmative request (but does not allow disclosure to third parties and does not impose a duty to disclose records to the parents absent a request). This investigation followed California’s adoption of Assembly Bill 1955, wherein a California LEA employee cannot be “required to disclose any information related to a pupil’s sexual orientation, gender identity, or gender expression to any other person without the pupil’s consent unless otherwise required by state or federal law.” On January 28, 2026, the ED sent a letter of findings to CDE, concluding that California and some of its LEAs were not in substantial compliance with FERPA, as they enabled schools to maintain information regarding students’ gender identities in “unofficial records” such that they are not subject to disclosure under FERPA. The letter asked CDE to undertake six corrective actions, including a written assurance that CDE “will allow [California LEAs” to use “pro-parental notification approaches.” These approaches primarily focus on providing parents, without their request, education records pertaining to their child’s gender identity, especially in the case of transgender students. The findings letter provided that, if California did not comply, the government would terminate all of the state’s federal education funding.On Feb. 11, 2026, California’s Attorney Generalsuedthe ED, alleging that the potential loss of funding presents an imminent injury to California and infringes upon the State’s interests. California brought claims pursuant to the Declaratory Judgment Act, the Spending Clause, and the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) for unlawful agency action, as well as a claim of ultra vires. They seek a declaration that the ED’s threat is unlawful, a preliminary and permanent injunction against any action to enforce the demand, and a stay of the ED’s findings that California violated the law.
Case Updates: Feb. 11, 2026:Judge Noel Wise granted a limitedtemporary restraining order(TRO) prohibiting the government from withholding any of the $4.9 billion in federal education funds until the merits of the motion are fully resolved.

Case Name: State of Illinois v. Vought(N.D. Ill.)1:26-cv-01566
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: Coming soon.On Feb. 11, 2026, the states of Illinois, California, Colorado, and Minnesotasuedthe administration, challenging the CDC’s decision to cut over $600 million in grants to the four states. The states allege that the administration arbitrarily cut CDC funds based on political animus, violating the APA’s protections against such decisions, exceeding the agency’s statutory authority, and violating the separation of powers and the Constitution’s Spending Clause. The states alsomovedfor a temporary restraining order against the funding decision based on their claims.
Case Updates: Feb. 12, 2026:Judge Shahgranteda temporary restraining order against the government’s plan to withhold public-health grants from states with sanctuary city policies.March 3, 2026:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaintnaming the Department of Transportation, Department of Homeland Security, and Federal Emergency Management Agency as new defendants and alleging new pretext claims and additional programs targeted.March 12, 2026:Judge Manish Shahgranted in part and denied in partPlaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction, enjoining OMB directives targeting Illinois, California, Colorado, and Minnesota for HHS funding cuts based on their status as sanctuary jurisdictions, while declining to enjoin the broader multi-agency “Targeting Directive” as to DOT and DHS. The court found Plaintiffs likely to succeed on their APA claims, holding that OMB’s directive to HHS was likely a final agency action that was arbitrary, capricious, and in excess of statutory authority, and that the states’ sovereign and public health interests outweighed the government’s interests.

Case Name: Confederated Tribes of the Colville Reservation v. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration(E.D. Wash.)2:26-cv-00061
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: On Feb. 3, 2026, the Confederated Tribes of the Colville Reservation and the Confederated Tribes of the Chehalis Reservation, both federally recognized Indian tribes with adjudicated or treaty-reserved fishing rights and active hatchery operations in Washington State, filed thiscomplaintto challenge the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s (NOAA) and the Bureau of Indian Affairs’ (BIA) arbitrary exclusion of the tribes from a $240 million salmon hatchery funding pool established under the Inflation Reduction Act. The administration's conduct involved NOAA leadership pre-selecting a "fixed" list of only 27 eligible tribes—providing each with $2 million non-competitive grants while excluding Plaintiffs—without providing a reasoned decision or public notice. These actions result in severe harms, as the lack of funding prevents the Colville Tribes from constructing a $13.2 million "third water source" necessary to mitigate rising temperatures that cause high broodstock mortality, and denies the Chehalis Tribe $8.5 million for critical renovations to replace failing water towers and insufficient wells. The Colville Tribes assert that its off-reservation fishing rights are substantively identical to those of "treaty tribes" included on the list, and its operations are comparable to the Metlakatla Indian Community, which was deemed eligible. The Chehalis Tribe argues its on-reservation fishing rights and hatchery operations are comparable to the Hoopa and Yurok Tribes, both of which were included. Furthermore, Plaintiffs allege that NOAA failed to provide consistent notice of tribal consultation sessions, further marginalizing their ability to advocate for inclusion. The complaint alleges that Defendants’ exclusion of the Colville and Chehalis Tribes is in violation of the Indian Reorganization Act’s "Privileges and Immunities" clause, 25 U.S.C. § 5123(f), the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), 5 U.S.C. § 706, for arbitrary and capricious actions and the unlawful withholding of a reasoned decision, and a breach of the federal government’s fiduciary trust responsibilities. To remedy these violations, Plaintiffs seek a declaratory judgment of their eligibility, an order for the immediate distribution of $2 million grants to each Tribe, and a requirement that Defendants preserve $18 million in remaining funds until their project proposals are evaluated.
Case Updates: March 6, 2026:Chief District Judge Stanley A. BastiangrantedPlaintiffs’ motion to expedite and denied Plaintiffs’ Motion for Temporary Restraining Order, writing in a subsequently issuedorderthat Plaintiff Tribes “do not meet the standard for injunctive relief as they are unlikely to succeed on the merits of their APA claims” and the timing of the suit and Plaintiffs’ request for injunctive relief do not support Plaintiffs’ claim of irreparable harm in the absence of judicial intervention.

Case Name: City of Fresno v. Noem(N.D. Cal.)5:26-cv-01535
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: The administration issued a series of Executive Orders (EOs) and Departmental Memoranda that unilaterally imposed new conditions on federal grants administered by the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), the Department of Justice (DOJ), and the Department of the Interior (DOI). These directives require Plaintiffs, a coalition of eleven California and Oregon cities and counties, to certify they do not operate programs promoting "diversity, equity, and inclusion" (DEI) (EO 14173) or "gender ideology" (EO 14168), and that they will strictly cooperate with federal immigration enforcement (EO 14287). The administration allegedly further designated compliance with these ideological conditions as "material" to grant payment decisions, specifically to facilitate investigations and litigation under the False Claims Act.On Feb. 20, 2026, PlaintiffssuedDHS, FEMA, DOJ, and DOI (collectively, Agency Defendants) as well as their leaders, alleging that these unauthorized and vague conditions force them into an "impossible choice" between forfeiting millions of dollars in essential funding for disaster mitigation, firefighter staffing, and victim services, or accepting legal jeopardy by complying with mandates that conflict with existing law and court precedent. The complaint asserts seven counts of legal violations, including breaches of the Separation of Powers Doctrine, Spending Clause, Tenth Amendment, and Fifth Amendment "void-for-vagueness" doctrine. Additionally Under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), the complaint argues that these agency actions are arbitrary, capricious, and in excess of statutory authority. Plaintiffs seek a judicial declaration that the grant conditions are unlawful and a permanent injunction to prevent the administration from enforcing these requirements or retaliating against local governments.

Case Name: State of Colorado v. United States(Fed. Cl.)1:26-cv-00315
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: The Targeted Violence and Terrorism Prevention (TVTP) grant program provides federal dollars dedicated to funding local, proactive targeted violence and counterterrorism efforts. On July 23, 2025, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) allegedly notified certain TVTP grant recipients that their Fiscal Year 2022-2024 awards, totaling approximately $2.85 million, would be terminated, effective “immediately.” DHS allegedly justified this move by claiming the awards no longer “effectuate program goals” and were previously used to fund “openly partisan” organizations and “sanctuary jurisdictions.”On Feb. 25, 2026, six states filed acomplaintin the U.S. Court of Federal Claims under the Tucker Act alleging that the United States (via DHS and the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA)) breached multiple TVTP grant agreements. The Plaintiff States allege these terminations are a breach of the TVTP Grant Agreements because these Agreements and Notices of Funding Opportunity (NOFOs) do not permit termination based on shifting "program goals or agency priorities." Instead, the contracts limit termination to cases of material noncompliance or mutual consent–neither of which occurred here, according to the Plaintiff States. The complaint further argues that DHS breached the TVTP Grant Agreements’ implied duty of good faith and fair dealing through several of its actions, including unilaterally terminating the agreements on a basis that was not provided for in the contracts and without adequate explanation. The Plaintiff States seek money damages, interest, and attorney’s fees.

Case Name: State of Minnesota v. Oz(D. Minn.)0:26-cv-01701
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: On Jan. 6, 2026, the administration announced that more than $2 billion of annual Medicaid funding would be withheld from Minnesota based on assertions of “noncompliance” with the Social Security Act and threatened withholding more than $515 million quarterly. On Feb. 25, 2026, the administration deferred $259 million in Medicaid funding from Minnesota. On March 2, 2026, the State of Minnesota and the Commissioner of the Minnesota Department of Human Services filed acomplaintagainst the Administrator for the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, the Secretary of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS), and DHHS. Plaintiffs argue that Defendants’ actions are ultra vires and violate the Fifth Amendment’s Due Process Clause, the Spending Clause, and the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), 5 U.S.C. § 706(2), for being without observance of procedure required by law and arbitrary, capricious, and contrary to law. Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief, a temporary restraining order, preliminary and permanent injunctive relief, and vacatur of the deferral notice.
Case Updates: April 6, 2026:The courtdeniedMinnesota’s motion for a preliminary injunction, holding that the state was unlikely to succeed on its APA, due process, spending power, and ultra vires claims primarily because the deferral notice was not final agency action, the record did not support a pretext finding, and because the alleged harms were too speculative and contingent to establish irreparable injury.May 7, 2026:The courtgrantedthe parties’joint motion for a stayand denied Defendants’ motion to dismiss without prejudice to refiling in compliance with the District of Minnesota’s local rules following the lifting of the stay.

Case Name: American Federation of State, County, and Municipal Employees, AFLCIO v. Vought(N.D. Ill.)1:26-cv-02656
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: On Feb. 9 and Feb. 11, 2026, the Center for Disease Control (CDC) notified Congress of its intention to terminate upwards of $600 million in public health grants being awarded to four states: Illinois, California, Colorado, and Minnesota (CDC Grant Termination Decision). On March 9, 2026, American Federation of State, County, and Municipal Employees and its Illinois affiliate (collectively, Plaintiffs) filedsuitagainst Russell Vought, Robert F. Kennedy Jr., and federal agencies including HHS and CDC, challenging the administration’s directive ordering the termination of these critical public health grants administered by the CDC (Targeting Directive). Plaintiffs, including a labor organization and trade union, allege that the grant terminations occurred at the direction of President Donald Trump because these states contain so-called “sanctuary cities,” jurisdictions which enact immigration policies antithetical to the administration’s. The complaint alleges Defendants unlawfully conditioned or withdrew grant funding from Illinois, California, Colorado, and Minnesota in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) (5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A) and 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(C)) as well as the Constitution’s Spending Clause, Separation of Powers, First Amendment, and Fifth Amendment Equal Protection Clause. Plaintiffs seek a declaration that the Targeting Directive and CDC Grant Termination Decision are unlawful and injunctive relief to prevent Defendants from implementing the Targeting Directive, the CDC Grant Termination Decision, and any other actions taken pursuant to the Targeting Directive.

Case Name: University Corporation for Atmospheric Research v. National Science Foundation(D. Colo.)1:26-cv-01061
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: The University Corporation for Atmospheric Research (UCAR), a Colorado-based nonprofit consortium of 129 colleges and universities that manages the National Center for Atmospheric Research (NCAR), a federally funded research and development center sponsored by the National Science Foundation (NSF), filed acomplaintalleging that federal agencies launched a coordinated campaign of unconstitutional retaliation against Colorado and its institutions. The complaint claims that defendants’ actions were triggered by Colorado’s refusal to yield its sovereign authority over election administration and criminal justice — specifically, its rejection of federal demands to release state prisonerTina Peters, a former county clerk convicted of election-related offenses, and its refusal to discontinue mail-in voting — and were publicly linked to those disputes by a White House spokesperson.The complaint alleges that NSF, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), the Department of Commerce, the Office of Management and Budget (OMB), and their respective heads carried out the retaliation through four categories of unlawful action: (1) NSF’s decision to transfer stewardship of the NCAR-Wyoming Supercomputing Center to a third party without contractual or regulatory authority; (2) NOAA’s termination of a multi-million-dollar cooperative agreement without advance notice, without the procedural protections, and without any identified performance deficiency; (3) NSF’s imposition of heightened reporting requirements without the risk-based assessment; and (4) NSF’s blanket prohibition on UCAR and NCAR making any public comment on restructuring activities, in excess of any contractual authority and in violation of the First Amendment. UCAR brings claims under the APA, 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A)–(D), and the First Amendment, and seeks declaratory judgments, injunctive relief vacating the challenged agency actions, and a stay pending judicial review under 5 U.S.C. § 705.

Case Name: Metropolitan Transportation Authority v. United States(Fed. Cl.)1:26-cv-00422
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: In November 2023, the U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) entered into an agreement with the New York City Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA), committing up to $3.4 billion (over 40% of the estimated cost) to help build New York City’s Second Avenue Subway, extending the Q line several miles into East Harlem with three new stations. On Sept. 30, 2025,  the same day DOT signed aninterim final ruleremoving race- and sex-based eligibility presumptions from its Disadvantaged Business Enterprise (DBE) contracting program, DOT sent the MTA a letter notifying it that no further disbursements for the project would be made pending a review of the project’s DBE compliance. DOT stated that Secretary Duffy’s position was that subsidizing infrastructure contracts on the basis of race and sex was unconstitutional, and that the review was needed to determine whether the project’s contracting practices complied with equal protection requirements. In an Oct. 1, 2025press statement, DOT also stated that the review would take longer due to the government shutdown that began that day, describing the delay as “another unfortunate casualty of radical Democrats’ reckless decision to hold the federal government hostage.”On March 17, 2026, the MTA filed acomplaintin the U.S. Court of Federal Claims alleging that DOT breached their contractual agreement by withholding $58,643,339 in reimbursement requests without providing the 90-day written notice of breach and opportunity to cure required and without satisfying the conditions for withholding under federal regulations. The complaint further alleges breach of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, seeks money damages, and requests expedited partial summary judgment on the withheld reimbursements.
Case Updates: April 9, 2026:Judge Hadjiorderedthe parties to discuss filing public redacted versions of the grant agreement attached to the complaint and the administration’s response to Plaintiff’s motion for summary judgment. Judge Hadji also ordered the unsealing of a DOT letter that was erroneously sealed.

Case Name: Afterschool for Children and Teens (ACT) Now v. United States Department of Education(N.D. Ill.)1:25-cv-15704
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: Afterschool for Children and Teens Now (ACT Now) is an Illinois organization that provides Illinois students with after-school programming, funded in part by Full-Service Community Schools (FSCS) grants. Metropolitan Family Services (MFS) is an Illinois nonprofit that serves as ACT Now’s financial sponsor. On Dec. 12, 2025, the Department of Education (ED or Department) notified ACT Now and MFS that they would not receive continuation awards for their two FSCS grants. On Dec. 29, 2025, the Department denied the organizations’ request for reconsideration. That same day, ACT Now and MFS filed acomplaintagainst the ED and Linda McMahon, the Secretary of Education. Plaintiffs alleged that the ED’s discontinuation of the grants violated the Administrative Procedure Act because the agency action was arbitrary and capricious, contrary to the law, and in excess of statutory authority. Plaintiffs also argued that the Department acted in violation of the Spending Clause, the separation of powers by exercising authority not granted to the Executive Branch, the Fifth Amendment Due Process Clause, and the First Amendment Free Speech Clause. Plaintiffs sought declaratory relief and preliminary and permanent injunctions.
Case Updates: Dec. 31, 2025:Judge Martha Pacold filed anorderdenying Plaintiff's motion for temporary restraining order.

Case Name: State of New York v. U.S. Department of Transportation(2nd Cir.)
Executive Action: Denial of Federal Grants
Case Summary: In its 2025 annual review, the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMSCA), a subunit of the Department of Transportation, found the New York State Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV) failed to comply with federal standards for issuing commercials driver’s licenses (CDLs) to nondomiciled individuals. FMSCA issued aFinal Determination of Substantial Noncomplianceon April 16, 2026 (Final Determination), which explains that FMSCA is withholding approximately $73.5 million in National Highway Performance Program and Surface Transportation Block Grant Program funds from the State of New York, beginning in fiscal year 2027. Moreover, the DMV program could be decertified and additional funds could be withheld in future years. These funds provide for the safety, maintenance, and reliability of New York’s entire transportation system, and their delayed receipt may endanger the safety of motorists and pedestrians.The State of New York and the DMVpetitionedthe Second Circuit to review FMSCA’s Final Determination. The petition alleges that Final Determination is arbitrary and capricious, an abuse of discretion, in excess of statutory authority, and unsupported by substantial evidence, in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), 5 U.S.C. §§ 706(2)(A), (C)-(E). Plaintiffs seek review of the FMCSA’s Final Determination under the Hobbs Administrative Orders Review Act, 28 U.S.C. § 2342(3)(A) and for the Second Circuit to find the Final Determination unlawful and set it aside.

Case Name: RFE/RL, Inc. v. Lake(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-00799
Executive Action: Governance and Defunding Global Media and Global Internet Freedom (Executive Order 14217 - Commencing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14238 - Continuing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14290 - Ending Taxpayer Subsidization of Biased Media)
Case Summary: On March 14, President Donald Trump passed an Executive Order (EO) that eliminated non-mandatory functions and sections of seven federal agencies and required them to reduce mandatory functions and personnel to the legal minimum, with the agencies being the Federal Mediation and Conciliation Service, the United States Agency for Global Media (USAGM), the Woodrow Wilson International Center for Scholars in the Smithsonian Institution, the Institute of Museum and Library Services, the United States Interagency Council on Homelessness, the Community Development Financial Institutions Fund, and the Minority Business Development Agency. Plaintiff Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty (RFE/RL) is a private, nonprofit news organization funded by congressionally appropriated funds distributed by USAGM. On March 15, Kari Lake, a Senior Advisor to the Acting Chief Executive Officer of USAGM, purported to terminate RFE/RL’s grant agreement on the basis of the March 14 EO. USAGM has informed RFE/RL that it will not disburse to RFE/RL its congressionally appropriated funds for March 1, 2025, through Sep. 30, 2025. Defendants are USAGM, Kari Lake, and Victor Morales, Acting Chief Executive Officer of USAGM. Plaintiff requests that the court find USAGM’s impoundment of RFE/RL’s congressionally appropriated funds and termination of RFE/RL’s grant agreement to be unlawful, to obligate USAGM to disburse to RFE/RL the appropriations of ~$7.5 million covering March 1-14, 2025, and ~$70 million covering March 15-Sep. 30, 2025, and to issue a permanent injunction. Plaintiff’s counts include an alleged violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (for “arbitrary and capricious” action and action in violation of statutory non-discretionary duty); the Presentment, Spending, and Take Care Clauses; and the separation of powers.

Executive Action: Governance and Defunding Global Media and Global Internet Freedom (Executive Order 14217 - Commencing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14238 - Continuing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14290 - Ending Taxpayer Subsidization of Biased Media)

Case Name: Widakuswara v. Kari Lake(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01015
Executive Action: Governance and Defunding Global Media and Global Internet Freedom (Executive Order 14217 - Commencing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14238 - Continuing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14290 - Ending Taxpayer Subsidization of Biased Media)
Case Summary: On March 14, 2025, President Trump signed an Executive Order (EO) that eliminated non-mandatory functions and sections of seven federal agencies, including the U.S. Agency for Global Media (USAGM), and required them to reduce mandatory functions and personnel to the legal minimum. Voice of America (VOA) is a government-funded media outlet under the USAGM. VOA journalists, a director at USAGM, Reporters without Borders, and numerous unions representing federal employees, journalists, and foreign service workers brought a suit against Kari Lake, Senior Advisor to the Acting CEO of USAGM, Victor Morales, Acting CEO of USAGM, and USAGM for the dismantling of USAGM. The Plaintiffs argue that the government’s conduct violates the First Amendment, the Administrative Procedure Act (as “arbitrary and capricious” action, not in accordance with law, and unreasonably withholding agency action), the Statutory Firewall law, and the Appointments Clause. Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief and a preliminary and permanent injunction to return USAGM and its employees, contractors, and grantees to their prior status and to restore all programming and grant funding.

Lake, 26-5087.May 14, 2026:Judge LamberthdeniedDefendants’motionto reconsider a portion of the court’s March 7, 2026opiniongranting partial summary judgment regarding whether actions taken by Kari Lake, under authority delegated by acting CEO Victor Morales, could be ratified under the Vacancies Act. The court first concluded that Defendants could not use reconsideration to raise a ratification argument they had failed to address during the earlier summary judgment proceedings. On the merits, the court held that the Vacancies Act’s anti-ratification bar applied because Lake served as the “de facto acting CEO of USAGM” without complying with the Act, regardless of whether the underlying authorities she exercised were otherwise subject to delegation. Consequently, the court concluded, Lake’s actions “have no force or effect” and “cannot be ratified post hoc.”

Case Name: Radio Free Asia v. United States(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-00907

Case Summary: On March 14, 2025, President Trump signed an executive order (EO) that eliminated non-mandatory functions and sections of seven federal agencies, including the U.S. Agency for Global Media (USAGM), and required them to reduce mandatory functions and personnel to the legal minimum. Plaintiff is Radio Free Asia (RFA), a private non-profit news organization whose goal is to provide uncensored reporting in Asian countries that otherwise lack access to free press. RFA is funded directly by Congress through appropriated funds distributed by USAGM. On March 15, Kari Lake, Senior Advisor to the Acting Chief Executive Officer of USAGM, purported to terminate RFA’s grant agreement on the basis of the March 14 EO, on the grounds that RFA “no longer effectuates agency priorities”. USAGM has informed RFA that it will not disburse to RFA its congressionally appropriated funds for March 1, 2025, through Sep. 30, 2025. Defendants are USAGM, Kari Lake, Victor Morales, Acting Chief Executive Officer of USAGM, the Office of Management and Budget (OMB), Russell Vought, Director of the OMB, the U.S. Department of Treasury, and Scott Bessent, United States Secretary of the Treasury. RFA alleges that USAGM has unlawfully impounded RFA’s congressionally appropriated funds and that its conduct violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA). Specifically, RFA alleges that USAGM’s actions are contrary to law under the International Broadcasting Act and related appropriations laws; contrary to the U.S. Constitution in violation of the Separation of Powers, and the Appropriations, Presentment, Spending, and Take Care Clauses of the Constitution; and “arbitrary and capricious” under the APA. RFA has asked the court to declare the grant termination unlawful and has requested a temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction barring Defendants from impounding RFA’s funding, as well as a permanent injunction requiring Defendants to disburse the funds.

Case Name: Corporation for Public Broadcasting v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01305
Case Status: Case Closed/Dismissed in Favor of Government
Executive Action: Governance and Defunding Global Media and Global Internet Freedom (Executive Order 14217 - Commencing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14238 - Continuing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14290 - Ending Taxpayer Subsidization of Biased Media)
Case Summary: On April 28, 2025 Trent Morse, Deputy Director of Presidential Personnel, allegedly sent an email to three of the Board members of the Corporation for Public Broadcasting (CPB), asserting that President Trump had terminated their positions on the Board. On April 29, the three individual board members and CPB brought suit against Trump, Morse, the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) and several other officials seeking (1) a judicial declaration that the email had no legal effect and (2) a Temporary Restraining Order (TRO) blocking defendants from taking actions to give effect to the email or otherwise seeking to interfere with or control the governance and operations of the CPB. The plaintiffs assert that the President has no authority to remove or terminate CPB Board members, because they contend that the Public Broadcasting Act of 1967 (the CPB Act), legal precedent, and government guidance make clear that CPB was established as “a private corporation [to] be created to facilitate the development of public telecommunications and to afford maximum protection from extraneous interference and control.” The plaintiffs assert that defendants’ actions violate the Administrative Procedure Act as an excess of statutory authority; a violation of the separation of powers; and a violation of the Presentment, Appropriations, and Take Care Clauses.
Case Updates: May 6, 2025:Defendants filed theiroppositionto Plaintiffs’ TRO motion, asserting that Trump has the lawful authority to remove CPB Board members. All other Plaintiffs are otherwise proceeding with their claims.July 25, 2025:The remaining Plaintiffsfileda motion for summary judgment requesting that the district court enter judgment declaring Trump’s attempt to terminate CPB’s Board members as unlawful.Aug. 1, 2025:Plaintiff Board member Thomas Rothmanvoluntarily dismissedhis claims, which was approved by the district court on that same day. All other Plaintiffs are otherwise proceeding with their claims.Nov. 14, 2025:The district courtorderedthe parties to file a joint status report by Jan. 5, 2026, on whether the case still presents a live controversy, and stayed further briefing on the cross-motions for summary judgment pending further order.Jan. 14, 2026:The courtdismissedthe case based on the parties’joint status reportand agreement that the Rescissions Act of 2025 and the subsequent dissolution of the Corporation for Public Broadcasting moot the case.

Case Name: National Public Radio Inc v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01674
Executive Action: Governance and Defunding Global Media and Global Internet Freedom (Executive Order 14217 - Commencing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14238 - Continuing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14290 - Ending Taxpayer Subsidization of Biased Media)
Case Summary: Through the Public Broadcasting Act of 1967, (the “Act”) Congress established the infrastructure for a public radio station, including the appropriation of federal funding to support independent public broadcasting and the creation of a separate private entity, the Corporation for Public Broadcasting (CPB), to act as an independent structure between the government and the recipient of funds. The Act expressly denies “authoriz[ation to] any department, agency, officer, or employee of the United States to exercise any direction, supervision, or control over public telecommunications, or over the Corporation or any of its grantees or contractors….”. 47 U.S.C. § 398(a). NPR, an independent, nonprofit media organisation, produces, acquires and distributes programming and makes it available to independent locally owned and operated public radio stations around the country, including the plaintiff member stations. It also manages the Public Radio Satellite System (PRSS), the nation’s public radio interconnection system. A portion of NPR’s funding comes from membership fees and fees for the management of the PRSS from member stations, as well as direct grants from CPB. Each of the plaintiff member stations also receives federal funding from the CPB, some of which is used to pay membership and programming fees to NPR. On May 1, 2025, President Trump issued Executive Order (EO) 14290, entitled “Ending Taxpayer Subsidization of Biased Media”. The EO directs federal agencies as well as the CPB to withhold all federal funding, direct or indirect, from NPR and the Public Broadcasting Service (PBS) as well as ensuring that local stations that receive grants from CPB do not use those funds to acquire NPR or PBS programming, and to revise existing grant agreements to prohibit grantees from funding NPR or PBS. The EO stated that it was expressly predicated on the view that their news was not “fair, accurate, or unbiased portrayal of current events to taxpaying citizens.” An accompanying Fact Sheet confirmed that the EO was based on the opinion that NPR and PBS distributed “left-wing propaganda” and listed specific news coverage and editorial choices with which the President disagrees. The National Endowment for the Arts (NEA) subsequently terminated a grant award to NPR in compliance with the EO. Plaintiffs sued, contending that the EO is retaliatory and viewpoint-based discrimination, and that it interferes with plaintiff local member stations’ freedom of expressive association and editorial discretion by preventing them from affiliating with NPR. They argue that the EO violates specific requirements of the Act as enacted by Congress, statutes governing the NEA’s grant-making functions, the Separation of Powers and the Spending Clause of the Constitution by unilaterally imposing restrictions and conditions on funds in contravention of Congress, and the First Amendment’s guarantees of freedom and speech and of the press. They also argue that the EO violates the Due Process Clause by disregarding NPR’s reliance interests in its continued eligibility for such funding without process and due to vagueness, that it violates the APA for lack of statutory authority and violations of the Constitution, and is ultra vires. Plaintiffs seek an order declaring the EO and all actions implementing it unlawful and unconstitutional and that the NEA and the CPB may not condition the receipt of funding or withhold funding from NPR and any local member station on the basis of the EO, and an order preliminarily and permanently enjoining the implementation of the EO.
Case Updates: June 20, 2025:State Attorneys General submitted anamicus briefin support of Plaintiffs.Feb. 20, 2026:The court consolidatedNational Public Radio Inc v. Trump, 1:25-cv-01674, withPublic Broadcasting Service v. Trump, 1:25-cv-01722. These cases will proceed underNational Public Radio Inc v. Trump, 1:25-cv-01674.March 31, 2026:In anorderandopinion, Judge Moss granted summary judgment for NPR and PBS on their First Amendment claims, holding that EO 14290’s instruction to all federal agencies to cease funding NPR and PBS constitutes unconstitutional viewpoint discrimination and retaliation, permanently enjoining the agencies from implementing or enforcing that directive. The court dismissed as moot claims relating to the CPB, which dissolved in February 2026 following Congress's rescission of its FY 2026 and FY 2027 appropriations, and declined to rule on Sections 3(b) and 3(c) of the EO.April 23, 2026:Judge Mossentereda partial final judgment for Plaintiffs upon ajoint motionby the parties following the court’s memorandum granting summary judgment.

Case Name: Public Broadcasting Service v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01722
Executive Action: Governance and Defunding Global Media and Global Internet Freedom (Executive Order 14217 - Commencing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14238 - Continuing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14290 - Ending Taxpayer Subsidization of Biased Media)
Case Summary: Through the Public Broadcasting Act of 1967, (the “Act”) Congress established the infrastructure for public television, including the appropriation of federal funding to support independent public broadcasting and the creation of a separate private entity, the Corporation for Public Broadcasting (CPB), to act as an independent structure between the government and the recipient of funds. The Act expressly mandates that CPB should not be an agency of the government in order to “afford maximum protection from extraneous interference and control.” PBS, a private non-profit membership organization comprising 336 local public television broadcast studios who pay dues to PBS, provides a broad array of commercial-free educational television programming and services throughout the United States. PBS produces, acquires and distributes programming and makes it available to its member stations, including Lakeland, and also serves as a source of support for its member stations through the procurement and distribution of development training, brand management, and other services, as well as providing a public safety wireless emergency alert system. A portion of PBS’s funding comes from membership fees and fees for providing services to member stations, with the majority of the remaining funding coming from federal grants. Member stations, including Lakeland, also receive federal funding from the CPB, which is used both to produce local programming and to pay membership and programming fees to PBS. Lakeland relies on PBS for both a large percentage of its programming and for the support PBS offers its member stations. On May 1, 2025, President Trump issued Executive Order (EO) 14290, entitled “Ending Taxpayer Subsidization of Biased Media”. The EO directs federal agencies as well as the CPB to withhold all federal funding, direct or indirect, from PBS and NPR as well as ensuring that local stations that receive grants from CPB not use those funds. to acquire PBS or NPR programming, and to revise existing grant agreements to prohibit grantees from funding PBS or NPR. The EO stated that it was expressly predicated on the view that their news was not “fair, accurate, or unbiased portrayal of current events to taxpaying citizens.” An accompanying Fact Sheet confirmed that the EO was based on the opinion that NPR and PBS distributed “left-wing propaganda” and listed specific news coverage and editorial choices with which the President disagrees. On May 2, Defendant United States Department of Education (ED) notified CPB that in compliance with the EO it was terminating a federal award under which CPB had granted funds to PBS for educational programs. The Plaintiffs sued, arguing that the EO violates the First Amendment’s guarantees of freedom of speech, freedom of the press and editorial discretion and is ultra vires conduct as it violates specific requirements of the Act as enacted by Congress. They contend that the EO is retaliatory and viewpoint-based discrimination, and that it also violates the First Amendment by imposing unconstitutional restrictions and requirements as a condition of funding. They also argue that the EO violates the Administrative Procedure Act as arbitrary and capricious and contrary to constitutional rights. Plaintiffs seek an order declaring the EO and all actions implementing with respect to PBS and PBS member stations unlawful and unconstitutional, and injunctive and expedited relief barring the termination of the funding.

Trump, 1:25-cv-01674.March 31, 2026:In anorderandopinion, Judge Moss granted summary judgment for NPR and PBS on their First Amendment claims, holding that EO 14290’s instruction to all federal agencies to cease funding NPR and PBS constitutes unconstitutional viewpoint discrimination and retaliation, permanently enjoining the agencies from implementing or enforcing that directive. The court dismissed as moot claims relating to the CPB, which dissolved in February 2026 following Congress's rescission of its FY 2026 and FY 2027 appropriations, and declined to rule on Sections 3(b) and 3(c) of the EO.April 23, 2026:Judge Mossentereda partial final judgment for Plaintiffs upon ajoint motionby the parties following the court’s memorandum granting summary judgment.

Case Name: Newhouse v. U.S. Agency for Global Media(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00980
Executive Action: Governance and Defunding Global Media and Global Internet Freedom (Executive Order 14217 - Commencing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14238 - Continuing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy) (Executive Order 14290 - Ending Taxpayer Subsidization of Biased Media)
Case Summary: This action arises from the administration’s systemic efforts to dismantle the Voice of America (VOA) followingExecutive Order (EO) 14238(“Continuing the Reduction of the Federal Bureaucracy”) and official communications labeling the outlet as “The Voice of Radical America.” Acting through defendants Michael Rigas and Kari Lake, the administration is accused of shattering the “Statutory Firewall” (Firewall) by censoring coverage of Iranian anti-government protests and forcing the Mandarin Service to publish White House talking points—including Al-generated imagery of the President—as objective "news." Plaintiffs, which include a coalition of high-level VOA journalists currently on administrative leave and advocacy organizations,suedRigas, Lake, and the U.S. Agency for Global Media (USAGM), challenging this alleged transformation of a taxpayer-funded, independent news agency into a partisan “mouthpiece.” Plaintiffs allege these actions have caused irreparable harm by chilling protected speech, usurping editorial discretion, and depriving the global public of a critical flow of objective information from regions where VOA is an indispensable source. The complaint asserts that such conduct (1) constitutes unconstitutional viewpoint discrimination under the First Amendment; (2) violates the right to receive information under the First Amendment; (3) violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) by being arbitrary, capricious; and (4) is contrary to the mandates of the International Broadcasting Act, §§ 6202 and 6204(b), which requires USAGM leadership to “respect the professional independence and integrity” of its news networks and observe the Firewall insulating these publicly funded newsrooms from political calculations. To remedy these violations, Plaintiffs seek a permanent injunction to restore the Firewall, a formal declaration of these constitutional breaches, and a writ of mandamus to compel the USAGM to fulfill its statutory duty to provide accurate and comprehensive news coverage.

Case Name: State of Colorado v. U.S. Department of Health and Human Services(D.R.I.)1:25-cv-00121
Executive Action: Termination of Public Health Grants
Case Summary: Beginning on March 24, 2025, the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) terminated $11 billion in public health funding on the ground that the funds were no longer necessary because they were appropriated under various laws responding to the Covid-19 pandemic. Plaintiffs are 20 states, the District of Columbia, and two Democratic governors. They assert that Congress appropriated these funds to strengthen public health programs and that the funds were not limited to addressing the Covid-19 pandemic, and that Congress and HHS continued to make these funds available even after the end of the public health emergency was declared in May 2023. Plaintiffs allege that these terminations of funding are arbitrary and capricious and contrary to law in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (as “arbitrary and capricious” conduct and contrary to law, for example, by allegedly unlawfully applying the “for cause” provisions of 42 U.S.C. § 300x-55). They request that the Court declare the terminations unlawful and enjoin Defendants from implementing and enforcing the terminations.
