Case Name: A. v. Noem(D. Mass.)3:26-cv-30031
Executive Action: Immigration Detention Policy
Case Summary: On Dec. 18, 2025, the Acting Executive Associate Director of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) issued amemorescinding a2010 ICE policythat prohibited the detention of refugees admitted solely on the basis that they had not yet adjusted their status to lawful permanent resident. On Feb. 18, 2026, ICE and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) jointly issued a secondmemopurporting to establish a new policy interpreting immigration laws to mandate potentially indefinite detention of all unadjusted refugees after one year of physical presence in the United States.Six individual refugees with pending adjustment of status applications — from Rwanda, Iraq, Syria, Afghanistan, and Sudan — together with two nonprofit refugee resettlement organizations, the International Institute of New England and Jewish Family Service of Western Massachusetts, filed aputative class actionagainst the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) on behalf of all unadjusted refugees subject to the policy. The complaint alleges that the memos exceed defendants’ statutory authority under the INA; are arbitrary and capricious and were issued without the notice-and-comment procedures required by the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), 5 U.S.C. § 553; and violate the Fourth Amendment’s prohibition on warrantless seizure and the Fifth Amendment’s due process and equal protection guarantees. Plaintiffs seek vacatur of the memos, class certification, and preliminary and permanent injunctive relief.

Case Name: Herrera Avila v. Bondi(D. Minn.)0:25-cv-03741
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Executive Action: Immigration Detention Policy
Case Summary: On Oct. 21, 2025, a Mexican national, Joaquin Herrera Avila, filed a petition for a writ of habeas corpus in the U.S. District Court for the District of Minnesota challenging his continued immigration detention without a bond hearing. The executive conduct at issue was the government’s determination that he was subject to mandatory detention under the Immigration and Nationality Act (INA) rather than discretionary detention. Petitioner is a noncitizen detainee held in immigration custody, who alleges that his prolonged detention without an individualized bond hearing violates the INA, Fifth Amendment Due Process Clause, Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as contrary to law and arbitrary and capricious agency action, and regulations pertaining to apprehension and detention. The petition asserts that the government improperly classified his detention under a mandatory detention provision and seeks relief under 28 U.S.C. § 2241. The court granted the petition in part,holdingon October 22, 2025 that Petitioner’s detention is governed by discretionary detention authority, not mandatory detention. The court ordered the government to provide a bond hearing within seven days or release him, and enjoined his transfer or removal pending that hearing.
Case Updates: Nov. 7, 2025:Defendants appealed Judge Tunheim’s October 22nd judgment granting the habeas petition to the Eighth Circuit.March 25, 2026:In a divided decision, the Eighth Circuitreversedthe district court's grant of habeas relief to Avila, holding that he was properly subject to mandatory detention under 8 U.S.C. § 1225(b)(2) and, therefore, not entitled to a bond hearing. The court concluded that even non-citizens long present in the U.S. can be treated as “applicants for admission,” dismissing the district court’s narrower interpretation and remanding it for further proceedings. The dissent argued that the majority’s reading departed from longstanding practice and improperly expanded mandatory detention.

Case Name: Carolina Migrant Network v. Immigration Customs Enforcement(W.D.N.C.)3:26-cv-00028
Executive Action: Immigration Detention Policy
Case Summary: On June 9, 2025, Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE)allegedlyissued a policy memorandum requiring staff to place ankle monitors on all people enrolled in ICE’s Alternatives to Detention (ATD) program “whenever possible.”  On Jan. 14, 2026, two immigrant rights non-profits filed acomplaintagainst ICE, alleging Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) violations arising from ICE’s failure to publish its ATD program guidance or instructions, including the alleged June 9 memorandum, in the Federal Register.  Plaintiffs seek injunctive and declaratory relief, including the disclosure of requested records and an order preventing ICE from withholding records in the future.

Case Name: L.T. v. U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE)(C.D. Cal.)5:26-cv-00322
Executive Action: Immigration Detention Policy
Case Summary: Plaintiffs are four individuals detained at the Adelanto ICE Processing Center in San Bernardino County, California with medical conditions requiring treatment or medical attention while in custody and an immigrant advocacy organization. Plaintiffsfiled suiton Jan. 26, 2026 on behalf of themselves as well as a proposed class of all people who are or will be detained at Adelanto against Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE),  the acting director of ICE and the acting director of ICE’s Los Angeles field office, as well as the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), and Kristi Noem, in her official capacity as DHS Secretary. Plaintiffs allege that the facilities at Adelanto, which are owned and operated by GEO, a for-profit government contractor, have repeatedly been investigated for dangerous living conditions. They allege that conditions at the facilities, such as rampant disease and illness, prevalent mold, insufficient food, clean drinking water, proper medical care, and disability accommodations, are inhumane and illegal.Plaintiffs allege Defendants’ detention practices at Adelanto violate the migrant-detainees’ Fifth Amendment Due Process rights on theories of punitive conditions of confinement and deliberate indifference to detainees’ medical needs. Plaintiffs also allege that Defendants fail to provide reasonable accommodations to detained individuals with disabilities as required by § 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, as well as DHS’ § 504 implementing regulations. Plaintiffs further allege the government’s decision to repopulate Adelanto and failure to adequately inspect or enforce ICE’s 2011 Performance-Based National Detention Standards (“PBNDS”) are arbitrary and capricious, an abuse of discretion, contrary to law, contrary to constitutional rights, and in excess of statutory authority under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA).Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief and injunctive relief preventing Defendants from further violations of Plaintiffs’ rights or retaliating against Plaintiffs, and requiring Defendants comply with their own detention standards. Plaintiffs also seek to certify a class of current and future Adelanto detainees and a subclass of current and future detainees with disabilities under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure Rule 23.

Case Name: Barbosa Da Cunha v. Moniz(W.D.N.Y.)6:25-cv-06532
Executive Action: Immigration Detention Policy
Case Summary: Coming soon.
Case Updates: April 28, 2026:The Second Circuitaffirmedthe district court’s grant of habeas relief to Ricardo da Cunha, holding that his detention was governed by 8 U.S.C. §1226(a), which allows for release on bond, rather than mandatory detention under §1225(b)(2)(A). The court explained that detention is not mandatory for noncitizens who are no longer actively “seeking admission” into the country and instead have been unlawfully living in the United States for a substantial period.

Case Name: United States Conference of Catholic Bishops v. Department of State et al.(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-00465
Executive Action: Suspension of the U.S. Refugee Admissions Program (Executive Order No. 14163) and Refugees Funding Suspension (Dept. of State Notice)
Case Summary: On Jan. 20, 2025, President Trump issued an executive order indefinitely suspending refugee admissions and processing. The State Department issued a Jan. 24noticesuspending federal funding for resettlement programs. Plaintiff, the United States Conference of Catholic Bishops (USCCB), is part of a public-private partnership with the federal government through the U.S. Refugee Admissions Program, and was providing transitionary resettlement services to more than 6,700 refugees when the State Department suspended funding. USCCB brought suit, arguing the government policy suspending funds for the Refugee Admissions Program is unlawful under the Administrative Procedure Act because it (1) violates the Immigration and Naturalization Act, the Refugee Act of 1980, and the Impoundment Control Act; (2) is an arbitrary and capricious abuse of discretion; and (3) is a substantive role promulgated without required notice-and-comment rulemaking. They seek a declaratory judgment that the suspension is unlawful, and temporary, preliminary, and permanent injunctions prohibiting the government from implementing the suspension and requiring the government to make reimbursements pursuant to the terms of its cooperative agreements.

Case Name: Guevara v. Sterling(S.D. Ga.)5:25-cv-00086
Case Status: Case Closed/Dismissed in Favor of Government
Executive Action: Other Habeas and Removal Actions
Case Summary: On June 14, 2025, Mario Guevara was arrested while reporting on a protest against the Trump Administration and was transferred to U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement custody on June 18. Guevara was granted bond by the Immigration Judge on July 3 and all criminal charges against him were dismissed by July 10, but the bond was stayed by the Board of Immigration Appeals. On August 20, Guevara filed a habeas petition, arguing that his continued detention is a violation of his First Amendment rights as it constitutes a prior constraint and retaliation. Guevara further argued that his continued detention is a violation of the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment because it is unrelated to any legitimate government purpose.
Case Updates: Oct. 6, 2025:The habeas petition wasvoluntarily dismissedafter the Eleventh Circuit denied Guevara’s emergency motion to stay the Board of Immigration Appeal’s removal order and Guevara was subsequently deported to El Salvador.

Case Name: U.H.A. et al v. Bondi et al(D. Minn.)0:26-cv-00417
Executive Action: Other Habeas and Removal Actions
Case Summary: In early January 2026, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) launched Operation Post-Admission Refugee Reverification and Integrity Strengthening (“Operation PARRIS”) which had the stated purpose of targeting and re-examining the legal status of Minnesota’s 5,600 refugees who have not yet secured permanent resident status (green cards). The individuals targeted are lawful refugees who were admitted to the country, have followed the rules, and are waiting to have their status adjusted to lawful permanent residents, which is possible after being present in the US for at least one year. On Jan. 18, 2026, Plaintiff U.H.A.fileda Petition for Writ of Habeas Corpus following his arrest and detention under Operation PARRISS and on the same day the courtenjoinedthe government from removing, transferring, or otherwise facilitating the removal of Petitioner from the District of Minnesota. On Jan. 24 a group of refugees living in Minnesota, along with a legal services nonprofit, joined U.H.A. to filea class action complaintagainst DHS seeking to end Operation Parris and the policies under which it has been implemented, which they allege have included the forcible arrest of unadjusted refugees without warrants or probable cause that they are removable or a flight risk; unauthorized detention, including in facilities outside the state; and coercive interrogation of refugees about their initial refugee applications (“the Refugee Detention Policy”).The complaint asserts that internal DHS procedures make clear that a refugee’s failure to adjust status or to apply for adjustment of status is not a ground for removal and therefore cannot be grounds for detention, and that DHS regulations authorize arrest only when an immigration officer has reason to believe that a person has committed an offense or is illegally in the U.S., and requires that officers obtain a warrant unless the officer believes the person will escape. Plaintiffs allege that the Refugee Detention Policy violates the Accardi Doctrine, which requires that agencies follow their own procedures, the Administrative Procedure Act as contrary to law, arbitrary and capricious and in disregard of statutorily required procedures, the Fourth Amendment’s prohibition on warrantless seizure, and the Fifth Amendment’s guarantees of Due Process and Equal Protection. They seek to certify a class of “[a]ll individuals with refugee status who are residing in the state of Minnesota who have not yet adjusted to lawful permanent resident status and have not been charged with any ground for removal under the [Immigration and Nationality Act]” (the Class) and a “detained subclass” defined as “[a]ll members of the Class who are or will be detained by DHS pursuant to the Refugee Detention Policy” (the Detained Subclass), a declaration that the Refugee Detention Policy is unlawful and unconstitutional, and to vacate and postpone the effective date of the Refugee Detention Policy. On the same day, plaintiffs filed aseparate motion to certify the Class and Detained Subclassand amotion for a temporary restraining orderseeking to enjoin the arrest or detention of any unadjusted refugee and an order that any member of the Class currently in detention be released.
Case Updates: Jan. 18, 2026:The courtenjoinedthe government from removing, transferring, or otherwise facilitating the removal of Petitioner from the District of Minnesota until further order of the court.Jan. 24, 2026:Plaintiffs filed an amendedcomplaintas a putative class action.Jan. 28, 2026:Judge TunheimgrantedPlaintiffs’ motion for a temporary restraining order ofOperation PARRIS, enjoining the administration from arresting or detaining a putative class of Minnesota refugees who have not yet adjusted to lawful permanent resident status and have not been charged with any ground for removal under the Immigration and Nationality Act solely on the basis of their status. He also ordered refugees detained on these grounds be immediately released.Feb. 9, 2026:Judge Tunheimdeniedthe administration’s motion to dissolve the temporary restraining order ofOperation PARRIS, which blocks DHS from arresting and detaining lawful refugees in Minnesota.Feb. 18, 2026:Judge Tunheimgrantedthe motion to stay consideration of Plaintiffs’ contempt motion for seven days.Feb. 27, 2026:The courtgrantedPlaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction, holding that DHS likely lacks statutory authority under 8 U.S.C. § 1159(a) to arrest or detain Minnesota refugees solely because they have not yet adjusted to lawful permanent resident status, and enjoining enforcement of that detention policy against the putative class while denying preliminary relief as to the already-released detained subclass.

Case Name: Doe v. U.S. Citizenship & Immigration Services(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01336
Executive Action: Ending Automatic Work Permit Extensions
Case Summary: On Oct. 30, 2025, U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) issued anInterim Final Rule(IFR), abruptly eliminating automatic extensions of employment authorization documents (EADs) for noncitizen workers. Since 2016, noncitizens with pending EAD renewal applications could continue working under automatic extensions of up to 540 days. This policy was designed to prevent lapses in work authorization caused by USCIS’s chronic processing backlogs. USCIS allegedly did not offer an alternative mechanism to prevent authorization lapses. The agency claimed the APA’s good-cause and foreign-affairs exceptions and national security concerns exempted it from notice-and-comment rulemaking.On April 20, 2026, a lawfully-present noncitizen who had been granted immigration relief under the Violence Against Women Act — appearing under a pseudonym — filed acomplaintchallenging the interim rule. Plaintiff, the sole financial provider for her U.S. citizen daughter, alleges her EAD expires June 23, 2026, and that USCIS has not processed her November 2025 renewal application, leaving her at substantial risk of losing work authorization. Plaintiff alleges the IFR violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) by failing to provide notice-and-comment procedures under 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(D) and is arbitrary and capricious under 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A) by failing to consider reliance interests, disregarding the factual record underlying the December 2024 rulemaking on the same topic, and ignoring foreseeable harms to workers, families, and employers. Plaintiff seeks a stay under 5 U.S.C. § 705, preliminary and permanent injunctions, declaratory relief, and vacatur of the IFR.
Case Updates: May 15, 2026:Judge BoasberggrantedPlaintiff's motion to proceed under a pseudonym, subject to further consideration by the district judge to whom the case is assigned. The court found that the overall balance favored pseudonymity because Plaintiff’s VAWA-based work authorization would reveal domestic abuse by her former husband, a “sensitive, personal, and traumatic experience” she has not disclosed even to her daughter, and because Plaintiff had already disclosed her identity to the government under the seal.

Case Name: Amica Center For Immigrant Rights v. Executive Office for Immigration Review(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00696
Executive Action: Issuance of New Rules in Immigration Proceedings
Case Summary: On Feb. 26, 2026, a coalition of immigrant aid nonprofits filed acomplaintagainst the Executive Office for Immigration Review (EOIR) and the Department of Justice (DOJ) challenging aninterim final rule(IFR) with an effective date of March 9, 2026, which Plaintiffs allege “in the vast majority of cases” would “eliminate access to appellate review before the Board of Immigration Appeals [(BIA).]” According to Plaintiffs, the IFR requires summary dismissal of most appeals without record production, truncates review timelines and briefing opportunities, and removes the judge’s authority to extend deadlines or hold cases in abeyance.Plaintiffs argue that the IFR, effectively eliminating appellate review, will dismantle the congressionally mandated system that accord basic fairness and statutory rights to noncitizens before expulsion, and errors of law and denial of due process, otherwise addressable on appeal, will become final executable agency decisions. Plaintiffs also claim that the IFR will irreparably harm their core work by making it difficult or impossible to represent potential clients, requiring more resources for fewer individuals, and forcing them to divert resources to respond to the IFR's effects. Plaintiffs allege the IFR was promulgated in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), 5 U.S.C.§ 706(2)(A), (B), and (D)--alleging Defendants actions failed to follow required procedure, were contrary to law, and were arbitrary and capricious—the Regulatory Flexibility Act (RFA), 5 U.S.C. 601et seq., the Immigration and Nationality Act (INA), and the Fifth Amendment Due Process Clause. Plaintiffs ask for a stay of the IFR, a declaration that the IFR is unlawful, and an injunction against its enforcement. In the alternative, Plaintiffs seek a declaration that appeal to the BIA is not an administrative remedy available as of right and thus is not required for exhaustion purposes, under 8 § U.S.C. 1252(d)(1) or otherwise.
Case Updates: March 8, 2026:Judge Mossgranted in part and denied in partPlaintiffs’ motion for partial summary judgment,vacatingand setting aside the substantive portions of the IFR as unlawfully and unenforceably promulgated “without observance of procedure required by law.” Judge Moss remanded the matter to the EOIR for proceedings consistent with the decision.

Case Name: Dorcas International Institute of Rhode Island v. United States Citizenship and Immigration Services(D.R.I.)1:26-cv-00132
Executive Action: Issuance of New Rules in Immigration Proceedings
Case Summary: Beginning in November 2025, U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Service (USCIS) issued three memoranda implementing four interrelated policies now challenged in this lawsuit. The first, a "Country-Specific Factors Policy" effective Nov. 27, 2025, revised the USCIS Policy Manual to require officers to treat an applicant's country of origin as a "significant negative factor" in any discretionary adjudication if that country appears on the Travel Ban — Presidential Proclamations 10949 and 10998, issued under 8 U.S.C. § 1182(f) and justified by the administration as necessary to protect the United States from terrorist attacks and national security threats. A Dec. 2, 2025memorandumthen imposed an indefinite hold on all affirmative asylum adjudications (the "Global Asylum Hold"), an indefinite hold on all immigration benefit adjudications for nationals of Travel Ban countries (the "Benefits Hold"), and a directive to re-review all previously approved benefits for Travel Ban nationals who entered the United States on or after Jan. 20, 2021 (the "Comprehensive Re-Review"); a Jan. 1, 2026memorandumextended the Benefits Hold and Re-Review to 20 additional countries.On March 5, 2026, a coalition of immigration legal services nonprofits and national labor unions — including organizations whose members and clients are nationals of Travel Ban countries — filed acomplaintchallenging all four policies. The complaint alleges that Plaintiffs' members have lost jobs and work authorization, had naturalization interviews cancelled, faced revocation of previously approved green cards and asylum grants, and been placed in indefinite immigration limbo, while Plaintiff organizations allege diversion of substantial staff resources and frustrated organizational missions. Plaintiffs allege the four policies violate the Administrative Procedure Act as contrary to law and in excess of statutory authority, 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A) and (C); as arbitrary and capricious, 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A); as issued without required notice-and-comment rulemaking, 5 U.S.C. §§ 553 and 706(2)(D); and as contrary to constitutional right, 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(B). The complaint further alleges that all four policies violate the Fifth Amendment's equal protection guarantee by discriminating on the basis of race, religion, and national origin, and that the Comprehensive Re-Review separately violates Fifth Amendment procedural due process by revoking previously granted benefits without notice or an opportunity to be heard. Plaintiffs seek a stay of the challenged policies under 5 U.S.C. § 705, a preliminary injunction, vacatur, a permanent injunction, and a declaratory judgment that the policies are unlawful.

Case Name: Hines Immigration Law, PLLC v. Executive Office for Immigration Review(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01018
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Executive Action: Issuance of New Rules in Immigration Proceedings
Case Summary: Beginning in late January 2026, the Executive Office for Immigration Review (EOIR) at the Department of Justice (DOJ) allegedly implemented an unannounced “Somali Fast-Track Policy,” placing the immigration cases of non-detained Somali nationals on a dedicated national docket before a small subset of selected immigration judges on an accelerated timeline. The policy allegedly schedules master calendar hearings with as little as one week’s notice and sets individual merits hearings, many of which were previously scheduled for 2027 or 2028, for no later than summer 2026. On March 24, 2026, two Minnesota-based legal service providers filed acomplaintagainst EOIR, DOJ, EOIR Director Daren K. Margolin, and Attorney General Pamela Bondi. Plaintiffs allege the policy makes it effectively impossible for immigration attorneys to comply with their ethical obligations to prepare for merits hearings forcing attorneys to withdraw from cases and leaving noncitizens unrepresented in proceedings with life-or-death stakes. For example, one Plaintiff estimates she would need over 8,500 hours of preparation over 19 weeks for her 71 affected Somali clients. Plaintiffs assert the policy violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as contrary to law and arbitrary and capricious, the Fifth Amendment’s due process and equal protection guarantees, and the First Amendment rights of attorneys and clients to consult and associate. Plaintiffs seek a stay, vacatur, and permanent injunction of the policy.
Case Updates: April 10, 2026:In anorderandopinion, Judge Nichols denied Plaintiffs’ emergency motion to stay the administration’s alleged “Somali Fast-Track Policy,” holding that Plaintiffs had not demonstrated a likelihood of success on the merits because their injuries were likely not redressable by the court and the challenged policy likely did not constitute final agency action reviewable under the APA. The court further held that two INA provisions — 8 U.S.C. § 1252(f)(1), which bars courts from enjoining operation of the removal statute’s provisions, and § 1252(b)(9), the “zipper clause” channeling removal-related claims to courts of appeals — likely preclude the district court from entering the relief necessary to redress Plaintiffs’ harms.

Case Name: Duru v. Mullin(E.D. Va.)1:26-cv-01132
Executive Action: Holds on Immigration Benefit Applications
Case Summary: On Dec. 2, 2025, USCIS issuedPolicy Memorandum 602-0192, placing an indefinite hold on all pending immigration benefit applications filed by nationals of countries listed inPresidential Proclamation 10949, which President Trump issued on June 24, 2025, citing authority under INA §§ 212(f) and 215(a) to suspend entry of foreign nationals from certain countries for national security reasons. On Jan. 1, 2026, Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) issuedPolicy Memorandum 602-0194, extending the adjudication hold to nationals of additional countries identified in Presidential Proclamation 10998, including Nigeria.On April 28, 2026, a Nigerian national and medical student at the University of Virginia filed acomplaintalleging that the adjudication hold has blocked USCIS from processing her Form I-765 Application for Employment Authorization, which she needs to begin a surgical residency at Oregon Health & Science University by June 11, 2026. Plaintiff alleges that USCIS has a non-discretionary duty to adjudicate her application under 8 C.F.R. § 214.2(f)(11)(iii), which mandates that “USCIS will adjudicate” Form I-765 applications forOptional Practical Training, providing work authorization for temporary employment directly related to a student’s major area of study. The complaint alleges violations of the APA for unreasonable delay and unlawful withholding of agency action (5 U.S.C. § 706(1)); arbitrary and capricious agency action in excess of statutory authority (5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A), (C)); failure to conduct notice-and-comment rulemaking for a substantive rule (5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(D)); and that Defendants acted ultra vires, as INA §§ 212(f) and 215(a) — the authority cited in the Presidential Proclamation — do not authorize USCIS to withhold adjudication of applications filed by individuals lawfully present in the United States. Plaintiff seeks a declaratory judgment that the adjudication hold is unlawful, an order compelling adjudication of her I-765 within 15 days, and injunctive relief barring application of the Policy Memoranda to her case.

Case Name: Fuentes v. United States(N.D. Ill.)1:26-cv-05737
Executive Action: Federal Immigration Enforcement Conduct — FTCA Damages Claim
Case Summary: Chicago Alderperson Jessie Fuentes filedsuitagainst the United States under the Federal Tort Claims Act (FTCA) on May 18, 2026, after an Oct. 3, 2025 encounter with federal agents at Humboldt Park Health Hospital during the Department of Homeland Security’sOperation Midway Blitzin Chicago, part of which was captured on video. The complaint alleges that hospital leadership summoned Fuentes to the emergency room after federal agents entered uninvited and detained an injured constituent. When Fuentes identified herself as an elected official and asked whether the agents had a warrant, agents shoved her, swore at her, grabbed her arms, handcuffed her, removed her from the hospital, and threatened to criminally charge her if she returned. Fuentes asserts FTCA claims based on Illinois tort law, including battery, assault, intentional and negligent infliction of emotional distress, false imprisonment, false arrest, and gross negligence. She seeks compensatory damages, costs, attorneys’ fees, and any further relief the court finds equitable and just.

Case Name: Smith v. Trump(D. Me.)1:25-cv-00158
Executive Action: Actions toward International Criminal Court (Executive Order 14203 Imposing Sanctions on the International Criminal Court)
Case Summary: On Feb. 6, 2025 President Donald Trump issuedExecutive Order (EO) 14203, “Imposing Sanctions on the International Criminal Court”, which imposed economic and travel sanctions against the International Criminal Court’s (ICC’s) Prosecutor and head of the Office of the Prosecutor (OTP) Karim Khan, under the authority of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The EO prohibits all US citizens, permanent residents and companies from providing him with services and material support, on pain of significant civil and criminal penalties. Plaintiffs are two U.S. citizens who have advised and supported the ICC and the OTP in their capacity as human rights advocates. They allege that their work with the OTP to carry out its investigative and prosecutorial mandate is speech protected by the First Amendment, and that the speech restrictions imposed by the EO violate their First Amendment rights because they have been forced to stop working with the OTP due to the threat of criminal prosecution and civil fines. They also argue that the EO is an ultra vires action in violation of the IEEPA because Congress specifically denied the President under the IEEPA the authority to regulate or prohibit the importation or exportation of any “information or informational materials.”50 U.S.C. §1702 (b)(3). Plaintiffs have asked for a declaratory judgement that the EO violates the First Amendment, and does not comply with the requirements of the IEEPA, and for an injunction to prevent the government from implementing or enforcing the speech restrictions in the EO.
Case Updates: April 25, 2025:Plaintiffs filed amotionfor a preliminary injunction requesting that the court prohibit Defendants from imposing civil or criminal penalties on them under the EO and the IEEPA based on their provision of speech-based services to the OTP.July 18, 2025:Judge Nancy TorresengrantedPlaintiffs’ preliminary injunction motion, thereby enjoining Defendants from imposing civil or criminal penalties under the EO and the IEEPA.July 22, 2025:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaint, which noted that the Treasury Department’s Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) had set forth regulations promulgating the EO. The amended complaint added a claim under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) alleging the regulations are not in accordance with law and requesting that the court declare the regulations to be unlawful.Sept. 12, 2025:The case wasreassignedfrom Judge Torresen to Judge John A. Woodcock, Jr.Oct. 7, 2025:Magistrate Judge Karen Frink Wolfgranteddefendants’ unopposed motion to stay due to the lapse in appropriations. The stay wasliftedon Nov. 20.

Case Name: Rona v. Trump(S.D.N.Y.)1:25-cv-03114
Executive Action: Actions toward International Criminal Court (Executive Order 14203 Imposing Sanctions on the International Criminal Court)
Case Summary: Case Summary:On February 6, 2025, President Trump issued Executive Order (EO) 14203, “Imposing Sanctions on the International Criminal Court,” which imposed economic and travel sanctions against the International Criminal Court’s (ICC) Prosecutor and head of the Office of the Prosecutor (OTP) Karim Khan, under the authority of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The EO prohibits all US citizens, permanent residents, and companies from providing him with services and material support, on pain of significant civil and criminal penalties. Plaintiffs are two U.S. citizens who are law professors who have advised and interacted with ICC and the OTP. Plaintiff Davis was appointed as a Special Advisor to Khan in December 2024, and Plaintiff Rona filed a complaint against the Trump Administration during Trump’s first term following the release of EO 13928, which was materially identical to EO 14203 but was ultimately rescinded. Plaintiffs now allege that their work with the OTP is speech protected by the First Amendment, and that the speech restrictions imposed by EO 14203 violate their First Amendment rights because they have been forced to stop working with the OTP due to the threat of criminal prosecution and civil fines. They assert that this EO is in violation of their Fifth Amendment right to Due Process because it provides no notice to Plaintiffs as to what acts are prohibited and permits the arbitrary enforcement of the EO. Plaintiffs also argue that the EO is an ultra vires action in violation of the IEEPA because Congress specifically denied the President the authority to regulate or prohibit the importation or exportation of “any information or informational materials” under the IEEPA. 50 U.S.C. § 1702(b)(3). Plaintiffs have asked for a declaratory judgment that the EO violates the First and Fifth Amendments, and does not comply with the requirements of the IEEPA, and for an injunction to prevent the government from implementing or enforcing the EO’s speech restrictions. Immediately after submitting their initial complaint, Plaintiffs filed amotionfor a preliminary injunction.
Case Updates: May 13, 2025:Defendants filed theiroppositionto Plaintiffs’ preliminary injunction motion.July 30, 2025:Judge Furmanpermanently blockedthe administration from enforcingExecutive Order 14,203, which imposes sanctions on International Criminal Court officials, against two U.S. law professors who had advised or supported the ICC’s Office of the Prosecutor. The court held that the order violates the plaintiffs’ First Amendment rights because “it constitutes a content-based regulation of their speech-based activities and cannot survive strict scrutiny.”Aug. 18, 2025:Judge Furman entered plaintiffs’ proposedjudgment.

Case Name: Iverson v. New York(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01353
Executive Action: Actions toward International Criminal Court (Executive Order 14203 Imposing Sanctions on the International Criminal Court)
Case Summary: On February 6, 2025, President Trump issued EO 14203, “Imposing Sanctions on the International Criminal Court,” which stated that “any effort by the ICC to investigate, arrest, detain, or prosecute protected persons” constitutes a threat to the national security of the United States. The EO authorized blocking sanctions to counter any ICC actions against protected persons, defined as a national of the US, Israel or any other “ally of the United States”, which is in turn defined as a “major non-NATO ally” (MNNA). In addition, the EO designated Karim Khan, the Prosecutor and head of the Office of the Prosecutor (OTP) of the ICC, a Specially Designated National (SDN) and thus subject to sanctions under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The EO prohibits all US citizens, permanent residents, and companies from providing a SDN with services and material support, directly or indirectly, on pain of significant civil and criminal penalties.Plaintiff is a U.S. citizen and attorney who has been a prosecutor in the ICC since 2010. He is presently the lead prosecutor in cases supported by the United States against individuals accused of committing war crimes, genocide, and crimes against humanity in the Darfur region of Sudan. Plaintiff alleges that although he does not work on any investigation involving protected persons or a MNNA, the EO and the designation of the ICC as an SDN directly impairs, if not totally prohibits, him from engaging in the practice of law and exercising his professional duties as a prosecutor in the OTP. He asserts that there is a non-speculative risk that activities necessary for him to conduct investigations and build cases for the OCD will be deemed the provision of prohibited services to an SDN and the receiving of material benefits from an SDN. Plaintiff alleges that his work with the OTP is speech protected by the First Amendment, and that the speech restrictions imposed by the EO violate his First Amendment rights because he is unable to do his work with the OTP due to the threat of criminal prosecution and civil fines. Plaintiff also argues that the EO violates provisions of the IEEPA because Congress specifically denied the President the authority to regulate or prohibit the importation or exportation of “any information or informational materials” under the IEEPA, 50 U.S.C. § 1702(b). He further contends that the IEEPA explicitly requires that sanctions only be invoked in connection with a “new threat” to the U.S., 50 U.S.C. § 1701(b), which Plaintiff alleges is not the case. He asserts that the EO is overbroad and in conflict with legislation by Congress authorizing the provision of direct support to the ICC in connection with its activities, including on cases involving Darfur. The Plaintiff has asked for a declaratory judgment that the EO violates the First Amendment and does not comply with the requirements of the IEEPA, and for a judgement enjoining the Defendants from commencing or authorizing any prosecution or civil enforcement against him in relation to his work on Darfur.
Case Updates: May 5, 2025:Immediately following the filing of his complaint on May 5, the Plaintiff filed amotionfor a temporary restraining order (TRO) as well as a motion for a preliminary injunction andsupporting memorandum. Plaintiff requested that the court issue an order prohibiting Defendants from imposing civil or criminal penalties on him under the EO.May 13, 2025:The Plaintiffvoluntarily dismissedthe case because he had received a “specific license” that mooted the relief requested. Judge Christopher Cooper ordered the dismissal that same day.

Case Name: Protect Democracy Project v. OMB(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01111
Issue: Removal of Information from Government Websites
Executive Action: Removal of Apportionment Information From OMB Website
Case Summary: On or around March 24, 2025, the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) took down a publicly accessible website and database that it had previously maintained making available documents related to OMB decisions regarding apportionment of congressionally appropriated funds. On April 15, Protect Democracy Project, a nonpartisan nonprofit organization,suedOMB and its director, Russell Voight, alleging that OMB is statutorily required to maintain this database and website. The Plaintiff claims that taking down the website has made it impossible for them to monitor the appropriation of funds and, in particular, has forced them to shut down OpenOMB, a site they operate, which pulls primary source data from OMB’s site and creates a user-friendly interface used regularly by Congress, litigants and a wide variety of other users seeking to monitor appropriations. Protect Democracy argues that OMB’s decision to take down this website and database is a violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) because it violates (1) the 2022 and 2023 Consolidated Appropriations Acts, which together established the directive to create and maintain this database and website; and (2) the Paperwork Reduction Act, which requires that agencies provide adequate notice when initiating, substantially modifying, or terminating significant information dissemination products. Project Democracy also argues that the decision was arbitrary and capricious under the APA. Protect Democracy has asked the court to declare that OMB’s decision unlawful, and to grant a preliminary and permanent injunction requiring OMB to restore the website and make all relevant information available.
Case Updates: April 22, 2025:Protect Democracy filed anexpedited motion for summary judgment, requesting a preliminary injunction or writ of mandamus in the alternative.April 27, 2025:Protect Democracy withdrew its April 22 motion and filed amotion for a preliminary injunction or summary judgmentin the alternative. In itsmemorandum in supportof the updated motion, Protect Democracy requests that the Defendants be enjoined from refusing to publicly post apportionments of OMB.May 2, 2025:Defendants filed theiroppositionto Protect Democracy’s motion for a preliminary injunction. Protect Democracyrepliedin further support of its motion on May 5. Defendants filed asur-replyto Protect Democracy’s preliminary injunction motion on May 5 as well.July 21, 2025:Judge Emmet Sullivangranteda summary judgment in part for the plaintiffs on the Administrative Procedure Act claim, holding that the administration’s deletion of thePublic Apportionments Databaseviolated public disclosure requirements. The court furtherorderedthat the Defendants must restore the Public Apportionments Database, make available the apportionment information required by the 2022 and 2023 Acts, and are permanently enjoined from removing the database without statutory authorization. The court noted “there is nothing unconstitutional about Congress requiring the Executive Branch to inform the public of how it is apportioning the public’s money.”July 21, 2025:Defendantsappealedthe court’s grant of summary judgement to the D.C. Circuit.July 23, 2025:Judge Sullivan denied Defendants’ motion to stay pending appeal, however the D.C. CircuitstayedJudge Sullivan’s orders from July 21 pending their review of the appeal on that same day.July 28, 2025:Judge Sullivanentered final judgmentin favor of Plaintiffs.Aug. 9, 2025:The D.C. Circuitdissolvedits July 23 administrative stay and denied the Government’s request for a stay pending appeal. The order applied to the consolidated appeal,Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington v. OMB, No. 25-5266.Jan. 28, 2026:Judge SullivangrantedPlaintiffs’ two motions to enforce the court’s July 21, 2025 Summary Judgment, finding that OMB incorporated-by-reference the terms of certain spend plans in legally binding footnotes and ordering these spend plans be made publicly available on the Public Apportionments Database.

Case Name: Sierra Club v. EPA(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01112
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Issue: Removal of Information from Government Websites
Executive Action: Removal of Climate Change and Environmental Information
Case Summary: On or around Jan. 20, 2025, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), Council on Environmental Quality (CEQ), Department of Energy (DOE), Department of Transportation (DOT), and Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) took down publicly accessible webpages and map tools they had previously maintained which served as key sources for information about environmental justice and climate change. On April 14, several nonprofit organizations relying on the information these webpages provided sued these Defendant Agencies because, without the data, they must expend additional resources to serve their communities by disseminating information about the environment, pollution, and climate change. Plaintiffs argue that the Defendant Agencies’ decisions to take down these webpages and databases violates of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) because it violates the Paperwork Reduction Act, which requires that agencies provide adequate notice when initiating, substantially modifying, or terminating significant information dissemination products. Plaintiffs also argue that the decisions were arbitrary and capricious under the APA. Plaintiffs asked the court to declare that the Defendant Agencies’ decisions to remove the webpages is unlawful, and to grant a preliminary and permanent injunction requiring these agencies to restore the webpages and make all relevant information available.
Case Updates: July 1, 2025:Plaintiffsvoluntarily dismissedtheir claims against DOE.July 9, 2025:The Courtdeniedthe plaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction.July 31, 2025:The remaining defendants–CEQ, DOT, EPA, and FEMA–moved to dismiss Plaintiffs’ complaint.

Case Name: Doctors for America v. Office of Personnel Management et al(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-00322
Issue: Removal of Information from Government Websites
Executive Action: Removal of Information from HHS Websites Under Executive Order on "Gender Ideology Extremism" (Executive Order 14168; Policy Memo)
Case Summary: On Jan. 29, the Office of Personnel Management (OPM) issued a memorandum (OPM Memo) directing agencies to implementExecutive Order 14168, “Defending Women from Gender Ideology Extremism and Restoring Biological Truth to the Federal Government.” Two days later, on Jan. 31, the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) issued “Initial Guidance” (HHS Guidance) instructing HHS components, including the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and Food and Drug Administration (FDA), to review and revise their public-facing materials in light of EO 14168. In response, those agencies removed health-related data and other information from publicly accessible websites. In asuitfiled Feb. 4, Plaintiffs, suing on behalf of doctors and scientists who rely on those datasets, allege that both the OPM Memo and the HHS Guidance are unlawful final agency actions and that the resulting removals are arbitrary and capricious under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), The complaint also challenges the individual webpage removals and substantial modifications as violating the Paperwork Reduction Act (PRA) and Evidence-Based Policymaking Act and, thereby, being arbitrary, capricious, and not in accordance with law under the APA.  They seek declaratory judgments that the OPM memorandum is unlawful and that the relevant agencies have violated the law; injunctions on further removal of information from agency websites; notice of any further modifications to webpages; and restoration of previously publicly-available datasets.
Case Updates: Feb. 11, 2025:Judge John D. Bates issued atemporary restraining orderandmemorandum opinion. The TRO includes a requirement that Defendants restore webpages and datasets identified by the Plaintiffs.March 24, 2025:The governmentfiled a motionopposing Plaintiffs’ motions for preliminary injunction and summary judgment. The motion also cross-moves for summary judgment on the basis that plaintiffs lack standing and do not have a claim under the APA.April 3, 2025:Plaintiffs filed areply in further support oftheir motions for a preliminary injunction and summary judgment in which they also opposed the government’s cross-motion for summary judgment. Plaintiffs assert that they do have standing to bring their claims and that the court should grant the preliminary injunction if it does not grant summary judgment in Plaintiffs’ favor.April 16, 2025:Defendants filed areplyin support for their cross motion for summary judgment. Defendants argue the court lacks subject matter jurisdiction and that Plaintiffs have failed to establish a final agency action subject to review under the APA.July 2, 2025:Judge Bates issued an order and opiniongrantingin part and denying in part the parties’ cross-motions for summary judgment. The courtvacatedin part the OPM Memo and HHS Guidance implementingEO 14168, which recognized only two sexes (“male and female”) and directed agencies to remove content that the EO labels as “gender ideology.” The court found that the OPM Memo exceeded OPM’s statutory authority and that both the OPM Memo and HHS Guidance were arbitrary and capricious in violation of the APA. The court vacated andremandedboth the OPM Memo and HHS Guidance and ordered defendants to restore or revert plaintiffs’ identified health-information webpages and datasets to their pre-Jan. 29 versions, and furthernotedthat the case involved “government officials acting first and thinking later.”July 18 and 25, 2025:Defendants provided status reports to the court tracking their compliance with the final judgment order issued on July 2. In both theJuly 18andJuly 25reports, Defendants noted that the pace at which they could restore the webpages is limited by staffing and resource constraints.Aug. 1 - Sept. 26, 2025:Defendants provided further status reports to the court regarding their compliance with the court’s July 2 summary judgment order onAug. 1,Aug. 8,Aug. 15,Aug. 22,Aug. 29,Sept. 5,Sept. 12,Sept. 19,andSept. 26.Oct. 1, 2025:The courtgrantedthe parties’ joint motion for attorneys’ fees and costs under 28 U.S.C. § 2412 and ordered Defendants to pay Doctors for America $55,000 within 30 days to fully satisfy any claim by Plaintiffs for fees, costs, and litigation expenses in this matter.Nov. 21 -Dec. 5, 2025:Defendants provided additional status reports regarding their compliance with the court’s July 2 summary judgment order onNov. 21,Dec. 1, andDec. 5.Dec. 12, 2025:Defendants provided theirfinal status reportregarding their compliance with the court’s July 2 summary judgment order, noting that it had completed the compliance process as to all 222 identified webpages.

Case Name: Schiff v. U.S. Office of Personnel Management(D. Mass.)1:25-cv-10595
Case Status: Government Action Temporarily Blocked in Part; Temporary Block Denied in Part
Issue: Removal of Information from Government Websites
Executive Action: Removal of Information from HHS Websites Under Executive Order on "Gender Ideology Extremism" (Executive Order 14168; Policy Memo)
Case Summary: On Jan. 31, 2025, agencies within the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), including the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and Food and Drug Administration (FDA) removed health-related data and other information from publicly-accessible websites in response to an Office of Personnel Management memorandum (dated Jan. 29) enforcingExecutive Order (EO) 14168, “Defending Women from Gender Ideology Extremism and Restoring Biological Truth to the Federal Government.” On March 12, 2025, plaintiffs Dr. Gordon Schiff and Dr. Celeste Royce, both Harvard Medical School faculty members, filed this lawsuit to challenge actions by the U.S. Office of Personnel Management (OPM) and HHS. The lawsuit alleges that OPM’s directive to remove content promoting "gender ideology" from government-run forums violated their First Amendment rights and the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) on the ground that “OPM has no authority, by statute or otherwise, to require agencies” to remove such content. Plaintiffs seek to reinstate removed materials and prevent further implementation of the OPM directive.
Case Updates: April 1, 2025:Plaintiffs filed amotionfor a preliminary injunction andsupporting memorandum of law. Plaintiffs seek to enjoin Defendants’ ongoing restrictions on Plaintiffs’ speech because they are (1) viewpoint-based and unreasonable in violation of the First Amendment; and (2) arbitrary and capricious, in excess of OPM’s statutory authority, and contravene a constitutional right in violation of the APA.April 14, 2025:OPM filed itsoppositionto Plaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction. OPM argues that Plaintiffs lack standing to receive their requested relief, Plaintiffs’ requests will become moot, and Plaintiffs failed to show they are likely to suffer irreparable harm.April 22, 2025:Plaintiffs filed areplyin response to OPM’s opposition of Plaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction. Plaintiffs argue they have standing to bring their claims and that these claims are likely to succeed on the merits.May 23, 2025:Judge Leo Sorokin issued anorderon Plaintiffs’ preliminary injunction motion in which he ordered Defendants to restore specific articles and studies authored by Plaintiffs Schiff and Royce as well as studies by private authors to the public forums, noting the removal of this content was likely in violation of the First Amendment. In hissupporting memorandum,Judge Sorokin noted that he declined to extend the preliminary injunction order to all content removed pursuant to the Executive Order and declined to enjoin the Defendants from further implementing the OPM Memo in any way.June 16, 2025:Defendants filed amotion to dismiss, arguing that Plaintiffs’ claims are moot in light of Defendants’ compliance with the preliminary injunction order issued on May 23.Sept. 3, 2025:Judge Sorokin issued anorderdenying Defendants’ June 16 motion to dismiss, noting that dismissal would be inappropriate given that compliance with preliminary injunctions, which by nature expire, do not guarantee permanent relief that extends beyond the pendency of a lawsuit and does not provide an “escape hatch” for the remainder of the litigation process, nor does it establish mootness or deprive a court of subject matter jurisdiction.

Case Name: State of New York v. McMahon(D. Mass.)1:25-cv-10601
State A.G.'s: State A.G. Plaintiffs, Defendant-side State Amicus
Case Status: Government Action Not Blocked Pending Appeal
Executive Action: Dismantling/Restructuring of the Department of Education (Executive Order of March 20, 2025)
Case Summary: On March 13, 2025, the Attorneys General of twenty states and the District of Columbia sued the Department of Education (ED) and the Trump administration to halt a planned Reduction in Force (RIF), which would reduce ED’s staff of 4,133 by approximately 1,378. The RIF is itself only an announced “first step” in a “total shutdown” by Secretary of Education Linda McMahon, the complaint alleges. The suit points to statutory authority that mandates ED functions under the 1979 Department of Education Organizing Act and other Acts including the Elementary and Secondary Education Act (1965) and Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (1975), and other education, disability, and civil rights laws. It states the Secretary of ED is only authorized to reorganize by “allocat[ing] or reallocat[ing] functions among the officers of the Department” or modifying “organizational entities within the Department as may be necessary or appropriate,” 20 U.S.C. § 3473(a). Under counts alleging constitutional violations, ultra vires (acts outside of statutory authority), and violations of the Administrative Procedure Act, the states move the Court to vacate efforts to “dismantle” the ED, to declare them illegal, and to enjoin the RIF.
Case Updates: March 24, 2025:Plaintiffssubmitteda motion for a preliminary injunction.April 11, 2025:Defendants filed theiroppositionto Plaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction. Defendants argue that the court should deny relief because the programs identified by Plaintiffs are within the discretion of the Secretary of the ED instead of being statutorily mandated and that Plaintiffs have failed to identify how the reduction in workforce is tied to the ED’s inability to perform its statutory duties.April 14, 2025:State Attorneys General submitted anamicus briefin support of Defendants.April 18, 2025:Plaintiffs filed areplyin support of their motion for a preliminary injunction in which they claim Defendants’ opposition mischaracterized the mass termination of employees as minor changes.May 22, 2025:Judge Myong Joun granted a joint motion for apreliminary injunctionfiled by the plaintiffs in this case and inSomerville Public Schools v. Trump, No. 1:25-cv-10677 (D. Mass.). That same day, the courtconsolidatedthe two cases and designated McMahon as the lead case. Defendants also filed a notice ofappealto the First Circuit following Judge Joun’s May 22 preliminary injunction order.June 4, 2025:The First Circuitdeniedthe government’s motion for a stay pending appeal.July 14, 2025: The Supreme Courtruled6-3 to grant a stay of a preliminary injunction that had blocked the administration’splansto dismantle the Department of Education and fire thousands of department employees. The majority did not provide any reasoning. Indissent, Justice Sotomayor, joined by Justices Kagan and Jackson, warned that the Court’s order enables the executive branch to functionally “abolish” an entire department “by executive fiat.”Aug. 11, 2025:Upon the request of Plaintiff-Appellees, the district courtissuedan indicative ruling stating that on remand from the First Circuit, it would vacate the preliminary injunction. On September 15, the First Circuitdismissedthe appeal.Sept. 17, 2025:The First Circuitissuedan order on the Government’s filing an “Assented-To Motion for Reconsideration” seeking either on order of remand or a mandate for the judgment immediately. The First Circuit issued itsfinal mandateon the same day.Oct. 1, 2025:Judge Joun granted the assented-tomotionto vacate the preliminary injunction and vacated the injunction.Nov. 25, 2025:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaint.Jan. 9, 2026:Plaintiff States filed anamended complaintadding new allegations regarding the administration’s attempt to dismantle the Department of Education, arguing that the Department’s transfer of its critical functions to other agencies violates the Constitution and the APA.
