Case Summary: On Jun. 4, 2025, Alvin Brown, the former vice chair of the National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB), filed alawsuitin the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia challenging his removal by President Trump the previous month.  Brown was nominated by President Biden in 2022, confirmed by the Senate in March 2024 to a term ending Dec, 31, 2026, and later designated as the Board’s vice chair. On May 5, 2025, Brown received a brief email from a White House official stating that his service on the Board was terminated effective immediately, without citing any of the statutory grounds for removal. The next day, NTSB officials cut off his access to Board systems, offices, and staff, preventing him from performing his duties. Brown, represented by Democracy Forward Foundation and Justice Legal Strategies PLLC, alleges that his ouster violates the Independent Safety Board Act of 1974, which permits removal of NTSB members only for “inefficiency, neglect of duty, or malfeasance in office.” He argues that Congress intended the NTSB to function as an independent, expert, multi-member body insulated from presidential political pressure, and that Trump therefore acted beyond his lawful authority in directing Brown’s removal. The suit seeks a declaration that the removal was unlawful and that Brown remains a Board member, as well as injunctive and mandamus relief requiring the NTSB and its chair to stop treating him as removed and to restore his full access to the office, facilities, and resources needed to carry out his responsibilities. The case isassignedto Judge Dabney L. Friedrich.

Case Name: Comans v. Executive Office of the President(E.D. Va.)1:25-cv-01237
Executive Action: Removal of Independent Agency Leaders
Case Summary: On Feb. 11, 2025, the Department of Homeland Security and the Federal Emergency Management Agency terminated FEMA’s Chief Financial Officer Mary Comans and publicly accused her of misconduct in a DHS press release. On July 24, 2025, Ms. Comans filed acomplaintchallenging her termination from the agency. Ms. Comans alleges reputation damage, loss of income and harm to future employment prospects due to the abrupt and unlawful termination. She argues that the termination violated Due Process and the Administrative Procedure Act, and that the administration’sExecutive Orderthat enabled her dismissal unconstitutionally and unlawfully limits civil service protections for members of the Senior Executive Service (SES). Comans also argues that the administrative body designated to hear cases of workers seeking reinstatement, the Merit System Protections Board, has become so dysfunctional that it no longer provides an effective remedy. She seeks reinstatement, backpay, declaratory and injunctive relief, and a name-clearing hearing.

Case Name: Cook v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-02903
Executive Action: Removal of Independent Agency Leaders
Case Summary: On Aug. 25, 2025, President Trump posted on Truth Social that he had fired Federal Reserve Board Governor Lisa D. Cook, after calling for her to resign on August 20. On August 28, Governor Cook filedsuitchallenging her dismissal by President Trump. Describing her removal as “unprecedented and illegal,” Cook alleges that under the Federal Reserve Act she can only be removed for “just cause,” which President Trump has failed to demonstrate. She further argues that her termination violates the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment. She is seeking a declaratory judgment that her firing is unlawful as well as an injunction against the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System from removing her from office.
Case Updates: Aug. 29, 2025:President TrumpopposedCook’s motion for a temporary restraining order, arguing the Federal Reserve Act gives the President broad discretion to remove a Governor “for cause,” and that Cook’s alleged false mortgage statements meet that standard. The Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System and Chairman Jerome Powell separately filed aresponseto Cook’s motion, stating they were named in the suit only to ensure complete relief if Cook prevails and do not plan to argue the TRO motion. They expressed interest in a prompt ruling to remove uncertainty and confirmed they will comply with any court order.Sept. 9, 2025:In place of a TRO, District Court Judge Jia Cobb issued apreliminary injunctionrestoring Cook to her position as Governor of the Federal Reserve Board, finding that President Trump’s attempt to remove her is likely unlawful. The court held that the cited mortgage fraud allegation predated her appointment and therefore did not meet the “for cause” removal standard. Judge Cobb also found that the administration likely violated Cook’s due process rights by not giving her a meaningful opportunity to be heard prior to her firing.Sept. 10, 2025:The Trump administration filed anappealof thepreliminary injunctionissued by Judge Cobb that restored Governor Cook to her position. The administration has also filed amotionfor an administrative stay and a stay pending appeal to the D.C. Circuit, attempting to prevent Cook from remaining in her position while litigation proceeds.Sept. 15, 2025:In a 2-1 decision, the D.C. Circuitdeniedthe administration’s emergency motion for an administrative stay and a stay pending appeal, keeping Governor Cook in her position while litigation proceeds. The majority determined that Cook’s due process claim was “very likely meritorious” because she was given no notice or opportunity to respond before removal. The order allowed for Cook toattendthe Board’sFederal Open Market Committeemeeting on Tuesday, Sept. 16 in her capacity as a Federal Reserve Board Governor. Judge Katsas dissented, arguing that “public office is not property” and that the administration offered a permissible cause for her dismissal without violating due process requirements and the for-cause requirement in the Federal Reserve Act.Sept.18, 2025:Following the D.C. Circuit’sdenialof a stay in this case, Trumprequestedthat the Supreme Court stay the Sept. 9preliminary injunctionpreventing the administration from removing Governor Cook from her position. The Justice Department argued that the two lower courts wrongly interfered with the President’s constitutional removal authority over Federal Reserve Board members.Oct. 1, 2025:The Supreme Courtdeclinedto rule immediately on the administration’srequestto stay the Sept. 9preliminary injunctionpreventing the administration from removing Governor Cook from her position. The decision keeps Cook in her position pending oral arguments scheduled for January 2026.Jan. 21, 2026:This case was argued before the Supreme Court.

Case Name: Gonzalez v. Gor(D. Puerto Rico)3:25-cv-01508
Executive Action: Removal of Independent Agency Leaders
Case Summary: On Aug. 1 and 13, 2025, the Deputy Director of the Presidential Personnel Office sent emails informing Plaintiffs, three members of the Federal Oversight and Management Board for Puerto Rico, that they were being removed from office. Under the Puerto Rico Oversight, Management, and Economic Stability Act of 2016 (“PROMESA”), members of the Board may only be removed for cause. Plaintiffs allege that their terminations were ultra vires actions in violation of PROMESA, the Fifth Amendment, separation of powers, and the Administrative Procedure Act. They seek declaratory and injunctive relief to enjoin Defendants from effectuating their termination.
Case Updates: Oct. 3, 2025:District Judge María Antongiorgi-Jordángranteda preliminary injunction to three members of the Financial Oversight and Management Board for Puerto Rico who were removed by President Trump, preventing the administration from effectuating their removal. The court found that the plaintiffs are likely to succeed on the merits, establishing that the President’s actions plainly violated the “for cause” standard of the Puerto Rico Oversight, Management, and Economic Stability Act, and violated the Fifth Amendment’s Due Process Clause. Rejecting the “idea that the federal courts are incapable of fashioning any remedy against the President,” the court found that “[d]efendants have advanced arguments that both lack support in the law and run afoul of basic rule of law principles.”Dec. 2, 2025:Defendants filed notice ofinterlocutory appeal.Dec. 22, 2025:Defendants filed unopposedmotionto hold case in abeyance pending the Supreme Court’s resolution ofCook v. Trump.

Case Name: Primus v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03521
Executive Action: Removal of Independent Agency Leaders
Case Summary: On Oct. 1, 2025, Plaintiff Robert E. Primus, a member of the Surface Transportation Board (STB), filed acomplaintagainst President Donald J. Trump, STB Chairman Patrick J. Fuchs, and the STB, challenging his removal by the President without cause in violation of 49 U.S.C. § 1301(b)(3), which allows removal of STB members only for inefficiency, neglect of duty, or malfeasance in office. Plaintiff seeks declaratory and injunctive relief to declare his removal unlawful, restore his membership and access to STB resources, and to issue a writ of mandamus compelling defendants to allow him to perform his duties for the remainder of his term ending Dec. 31, 2027.
Case Updates: Dec. 4, 2025:Plaintiff filed anamended complaintadding a new constitutional claim for denial of due process under the Fifth Amendment, alleging that Plaintiff was removed because he is a Black man.Jan. 28, 2026:Defendants filed amotion to dismissthe Fifth Amendment claim added in the first amended complaint for failure to state a claim.

Case Name: Hamilton v. Scavino(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00290
Executive Action: Removal of Independent Agency Leaders
Case Summary: On Oct. 8, 2025, President Donald Trump removed Deirdre Hamilton from her position as a Member of the National Mediation Board (NMB). On Feb. 2, 2026, Ms. Hamilton, Plaintiff in this action, filed acomplaintchallenging her removal. Defendants include Trump, as well as Dan Scavino, the Director of the White House Presidential Personnel Office, Mary Sprowls, an Advisor in the same office that sent the one-sentence e-mail notifying Plaintiff of her termination, the Chairman of the NMB, and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent.  Ms. Hamilton alleges that her termination violated the statutory for-cause removal protections that specify NMB Members can only be removed by the President “for inefficiency, neglect of duty, malfeasance in office, or ineligibility, but for no other cause.” Plaintiff alleges that Defendants acted ultra vires and abridged her “clear entitlement” under the Railway Labor Act to serve out her three-year term and until a successor is appointed without cause. Plaintiff alleges these unlawful actions are in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) and remedial under the APA. Plaintiff seeks declaratory relief affirming her removal was unlawful and injunctive relief recognizing her status as a member of the NMB subject to only for-cause removal, prohibiting the NMB from taking action without regard to her status as a member. Plaintiff also seeks wages, benefits, and back pay denied to her as a result of her allegedly wrongful removal.

Case Name: Brown v. Deleeuw(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01249
Executive Action: Removal of Independent Agency Leaders
Case Summary: On May 5, 2025, President Donald Trump directed the removal of Alvin Brown from his seat on the National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB), an independent agency Congress established under the Independent Safety Board Act of 1974,49 U.S.C. § 1111. The removal allegedly occurred despite a statutory provision limiting removal of NTSB members to “inefficiency, neglect of duty, or malfeasance in office.” No cause for the removal was allegedly provided. Brown, a Black man who had been unanimously confirmed by the Senate in March 2024 to a term expiring Dec. 31, 2026, continued to maintain he was the rightful occupant of his seat. Following President Trump’s nomination of John DeLeeuw to Brown’s unexpired term, the Senate confirmed DeLeeuw on Feb. 25, 2026, and DeLeeuw took the oath of office on March 16, 2026.Brown filed apetitionin the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia seeking leave to issue a writ of quo warranto against DeLeeuw (a legal mechanism to challenge whether someone is lawfully occupying a public office), arguing that DeLeeuw is unlawfully usurping Brown’s NTSB seat under D.C. Code § 16-3501 because Brown was never lawfully removed. The petition alleges two independent grounds: first, that the removal violated49 U.S.C. § 1111(c)’s for-cause removal protection; and second, that the removal violated the Fifth Amendment’s equal protection guarantee because it was racially motivated, noting that Brown was the only Black NTSB member and the only member removed without stated cause, while similarly situated white Democratic members were not. Brown brings this petition “out of an abundance of caution” alongside his pending suit inBrown v. Trump, Case No. 1:25-cv-01764 (D.D.C.); the petition notes that the U.S. Solicitor General has taken the position that quo warranto is the proper vehicle when two claimants compete for the same office. Brown seeks a determination that he, not DeLeeuw, is the rightful NTSB member; DeLeeuw’s removal; and reinstatement to the seat.

Case Name: Gordon v. Executive Office of the President(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-02409
Executive Action: Removal of Career Personnel
Case Summary: Over the course of June and July 2025, Attorney General Pamela Bondi fired three longtime employees of the Department of Justice (DOJ), allegedly without notice, explanation or adherence to statutory protections governing federal employment and following Plaintiffs’ involvement in several high-profile prosecutions, including January 6th related cases. On July 24, the Plaintiffs filed alawsuitagainst the administration, alleging that the firings have caused them reputational harm, loss of income and harm to future employment prospects, and claiming that their removals were politically motivated and retaliatory. They argue that their terminations violated the Administrative Procedure Act, the Fifth Amendment’s due process protections, and failure to follow certain statutory protections under the Civil Service Reform Act and Merit Systems Principles. Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief, including reinstatement to their positions within the department, backpay, and a name-clearing hearing.

Case Name: Driscoll v. Patel(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03109
Executive Action: Removal of Career Personnel
Case Summary: On August 8, 2025, three senior officials at the Federal Bureau of Investigations (FBI), including former FBI Acting Director Brian J. Driscoll, Jr., received letters from FBI Director Kash Patel informing them that they were being “summarily dismissed” from their positions. The agentsfiled suiton Sept. 9, claiming that they were entitled to the statutory protections of theFBI Senior Executive Service(SES), a scheme specific to the FBI established by Congress and codified at 5 U.S.C. § 3151, which sets out specific procedures and processes for the dismissal of senior FBI officials. Among other things the SES mandates that a senior executive may be removed only for “misconduct, neglect of duty, malfeasance, or failure to accept a directed reassignment or to accompany a position in a transfer of function.” 5 U.S.C. § 7543(a). Plaintiffs also argue that their terminations were based on an incorrect perception that Plaintiffs’ involvement in legitimate law enforcement activities was born of political motives and constituted acts of political disloyalty to President J. Trump and that they should be entitled to a name-clearing hearing regarding the false public statements regarding their actions and the basis for their respective removals.The Plaintiffs allege violations of the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment and the First Amendment as the actions were in retaliation for perceived political affiliation, and claim legal nullity as the dismissals were undertaken without lawful authority. They seek an order requiring Defendants to immediately reinstate Plaintiffs and to enjoin Defendants from taking any further adverse personnel action against them without providing appropriate procedural and substantive due process.

Case Name: Civil Servant 1 v. U.S. Office of Special Counsel(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03107
Executive Action: Removal of Career Personnel
Case Summary: On Sept. 10, 2025, five federal civil servants, initially proceeding under pseudonyms,filed suitagainst the Office of Special Counsel (OSC) and its Acting Director Jamieson Greer. Plaintiffs, all terminated during the administration’s February 2025 mass probationary layoffs, allege that OSC unlawfully abandoned its statutory duty to investigate prohibited personnel practices (PPPs) involving these terminations by issuing the Probationary Directive, which mandated closing investigations of mass probationary terminations without individualized review. Plaintiffs argue this directive was arbitrary, capricious, and contrary to law under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), violating OSC’s obligations to protect probationary federal employees from political retaliation and unlawful personnel actions. They seek declaratory and injunctive relief to compel OSC to fulfill its investigatory duties.
Case Updates: Oct. 6, 2025:The courtdeniedPlaintiffs’ motion to proceed pseudonymously, ruling that the five civil-servant Plaintiffs must file under their true names.Oct. 20, 2025:Civil Servant 1 and Civil Servant 3 from the original Sept. 10 complaint filed anew complaintunder their true names, in compliance with the court’s order.

Case Name: Comey v. United States Department of Justice(S.D.N.Y.)1:25-cv-07625
Executive Action: Removal of Career Personnel
Case Summary: On July 16, 2025, Maurene Comey received an email memorandum informing her that she was immediately fired from her position as an Assistant United States Attorney for the Southern District of New York “[p]ursuant to Article II of the Constitution and the laws of the United States”. She filed acomplainton Sept. 15 stating that she had received no prior notice of or reason for the termination and was given no opportunity to respond, and alleging that her termination was politically motivated due to her perceived political beliefs and to her affiliation with her father, former FBI Director James Comey. The complaint alleges that her termination violates the Civil Service Reform Act of 1978 (CSRA) by ignoring established pre- and post- removal procedures, Articles I and II of the Constitution and Separation of Powers since the dismissal violated Congress’s authority to regulate the removal of federal employees created by the CSRA, her First Amendment right to free speech and association, her Fifth Amendment due process, property interest and liberty rights, and the Administrative Procedure Act as ultra vires in violation of statutory authority. She seeks declaratory relief, reinstatement to her role as AUSA and backpay.
Case Updates: April 28, 2026:Judge Furmandeniedthe government’s motion to dismiss, finding that the court has jurisdiction to hear Maurene Comey’s case for improper removal because the Civil Service Reform Act (CSRA) does not channel Comey’s challenge to the Merit Systems Protection Board (MSPB) because she was removed pursuant to Article II of the Constitution rather than CSRA’s removal provisions.

Case Name: Paz v. Internal Revenue Service(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03256
Executive Action: Removal of Career Personnel
Case Summary: On Sept. 18, 2025, former IRS Commissioner of the Large Business and International Division Holly Paz filed acomplaintagainst the IRS alleging the agency unlawfully disclosed confidential personnel information to multiple media outlets during disciplinary proceeding, including false statements that she had been terminated, followed by a proposal to fire her, which was also shared with the media. The complaint identifies three media articles, which allegedly contained information disclosed by the IRS, in violation of the Privacy Act:Bloomberg Tax,FoxNews.com,Washington Examiner. Paz held the role of IRS Commissioner of Large Business and International Division from August 2022 until she was placed on paid administrative leave without explanation on July 29, 2025. On Sept. 2, Paz received a Notice of Proposed Adverse Action which informed her that the IRS was proposing removing her from federal service. Paz alleges that media articles regarding her employment contained private information retrieved from Privacy Act Systems of Records that the IRS was not entitled to release. She alleges violations of the Privacy Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552a, and seeks declaratory and monetary damages.
Case Updates: Dec. 1, 2025:Paz filed anamended complaintthat detailed the injuries she has faced as a result of Defendants’ actions, including health effects and difficulty obtaining employment.

Case Name: Reilly v. Bondi(M.D. Fla.)2:25-cv-00950
Executive Action: Removal of Career Personnel
Case Summary: On June 23, 2024, during the Biden Administration, the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) revoked the security clearance of Kelli-ann Reilly, a 26-year career FBI Financial Analyst, following an internal investigation into statements she made about irregularities during the 2020 election which led to a suspension without pay starting on Feb. 21. On Oct. 22, 2025, Reillysued the FBI, the Department of Justice (DOJ), FBI Director Kash Patel, and Attorney General Pam Bondi, alleging that her security clearance had been unlawfully revoked and she had faced politically motivated retaliation. Reilly alleges that FBI officials revoked her security clearance and thus “forced her into early retirement” due to her support for Trump and belief that he had won the 2020 presidential elections, as well as for other views the FBI characterized as “conspiracy theories” and proof of “mental instability.” She alleges that the revocation and other “political targeting” violate First Amendment protections against political retaliation and viewpoint discrimination, Fifth Amendment protections for procedural due process, the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), and the Back Pay Act. She seeks declaratory judgment that the revocation violated her rights and the APA, expungement of all records alleging “mental instability, psychological conditions, delusional beliefs, or misconduct,” and restoration of back pay and lost benefits.
Case Updates: Dec. 17, 2025:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaint, dropping APA and Back Pay Act claims to streamline the action as a constitutional challenge.

Case Name: Nemer v. Bondi(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04170
Executive Action: Removal of Career Personnel
Case Summary: On December 1, 2025, former immigration judge Tania Nemer filed acomplaintin the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia against the U.S. Department of Justice and Attorney General Pam Bondi, challenging her abrupt termination from the Executive Office for Immigration Review shortly after the new administration took office. Nemer, a Lebanese American dual citizen and former Democratic judicial candidate in Ohio, alleges that she was fired on February 5, 2025 (just fifteen days into the administration) because of her sex and national origin in violation of Title VII, and because of her prior political activity and affiliation in violation of the First Amendment, despite having received top performance evaluations and no documented performance-based justification for her removal. Plaintiff asserts that DOJ’s Equal Employment Opportunity office dismissed her administrative discrimination charge on the ground that Title VII’s bar on discrimination in the federal workforce conflicts with the President’s Article II removal authority. Plaintiff contends that this theory is irreconcilable with long-standing Supreme Court precedent recognizing Congress’s power to regulate the civil service and prohibit discriminatory removals and violate Title VII, the First Amendment, the Declaratory Judgment Act, and mandamus principles. Plantiff seeks declaratory relief that her termination was unlawful, reinstatement to her immigration judge position, rescission of the termination decision, back pay, front pay, compensatory damages for emotional and reputational harm, and attorney’s fees and costs.

Case Name: Doe 1 v. Patel(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00959
Executive Action: Removal of Career Personnel
Case Summary: Between November 2022 and June 2023, two FBI Special Agents — proceeding anonymously as John Doe 1 and John Doe 2 — were temporarily assigned to “Arctic Frost,” an FBI investigation into an alleged conspiracy to overturn the 2020 presidential election that ultimately produced a federal grand jury indictment of then-former President Trump, which DOJ moved to dismiss after President Trump won reelection in November 2024. John Doe 1, a white-collar crime specialist who served at the FBI for over 21 years, played a largely administrative role in the investigation; John Doe 2, who served at the FBI for over eight years and who had recently briefed FBI Director Patel directly on a separate fraud case and received positive feedback, served in a supporting capacity before returning to unrelated public corruption work. Plaintiffs allege that beginning in early 2025, members of Congress released unredacted Arctic Frost records while publicly branding the investigation a “partisan dragnet.” Director Patel fired both Plaintiffs between October 31 and November 4, 2025.Plaintiffs filed acomplaintagainst FBI Director Patel, the FBI, Attorney General Bondi, and DOJ, alleging their terminations were unconstitutional and pretextual. Although their dismissal letters accused both men of “poor judgment and a lack of impartiality,” their official personnel records allegedly listed the sole basis for their removal as “Article 2 USC” — and an FBI HR employee confirmed to John Doe 1 that his separation was allegedly not “for cause,” a statement that  contradicts Patel’s earlier testimony that “no one will be terminated for case assignments.” Plaintiffs assert First Amendment retaliation for perceived political affiliation, arguing the government fired them based on a belief that their Arctic Frost assignment reflected anti-Trump views. Plaintiffs also claim Fifth Amendment procedural due process violations. They seek reinstatement, name-clearing hearings, rescission of their dismissal records, and attorney’s fees.

Case Name: Garman v. Patel(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01086
Executive Action: Removal of Career Personnel
Case Summary: Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) Director Kash Patel and Attorney General Pamela J. Bondi carried out a series of summary dismissals of career FBI agents who had been assigned to former Special Counsel Jack Smith’s investigations as well as agents allegedly targeted based on assignment to other politically disfavored investigations and teams. Three former career FBI agents, including two who served on the Washington Field Office federal public corruption squad assigned to Special Counsel Smith’s election interference investigation and one who served as supervisory special agent over that squad, filed acomplainton behalf of themselves and a proposed class of at least 50 former FBI employees terminated since Jan. 1, 2025. Plaintiffs claim that the administration “embarked on a public campaign to oust Plaintiffs from federal service because Defendants perceived them to be political opponents,” alleging that Defendants summarily dismissed them without notice of charges, opportunity to respond, individualized misconduct findings, or any of the procedural protections required under FBI policy, while publicly labeling them as having “weaponized” the government.Plaintiffs allege violations of the First Amendment (retaliation based on actual or perceived political affiliation), the Fifth Amendment Due Process Clause (deprivation of a protected property interest in continued employment without due process; and deprivation of a liberty interest in professional reputation), and that Defendants acted ultra vires in invoking Article II authority to remove career civil servants whose employment is governed by statute and internal FBI policy. Plaintiffs also designated the case as related toDoe 1 v. Patel, a pending action brought by FBI agents raising common issues of fact arising from the same wave of terminations. Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief, reinstatement, name-clearing hearings, and class certification.

Case Name: Espinoza v. Blanche(N.D. Ill.)1:26-cv-05157
Executive Action: Removal of Career Personnel
Case Summary: On May 5, 2026, Carla Espinoza, a former immigration judge in the Department of Justice’s Executive Office for Immigration Review (EOIR)suedthe Department after it failed to convert her two-year appointment as a probationary immigration judge into a permanent position. In July 2025, EOIR declined to convert her appointment to a permanent position and terminated her employment allegedly without cause, discipline, or complaint and contemporaneously with a pattern of firings that disproportionately affected women, people of color, and judges with immigrant-advocacy backgrounds. Espinoza further claims that in the weeks before her termination, the administration learned she had set bond in a high-profile case involving a Mexican national respondent, notwithstanding public statements by the White House and the Secretary of Homeland Security opposing bond.Plaintiff alleges that the Justice Department violated Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, 42 U.S.C. §§ 2000e et seq., by terminating her on the basis of sex, race, national origin, and association with Latin American and Hispanic immigrants; and violated the First Amendment by terminating her based on her prior association with organizations whose political ideologies conflicted with those of the current administration. Espinoza seeks back pay and lost benefits, reinstatement to a permanent Immigration Judge position (or pay in lieu thereof), compensatory damages, a declaratory judgment that the termination violated Title VII and the First Amendment, and in the alternative a writ of mandamus ordering her reinstatement.

Case Name: American Federation of Government Employees v. Department of Education(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03553
Executive Action: Compelled Political Speech in Government Agencies
Case Summary: After a government shutdown began on Oct. 1, 2025, numerous federal agencies posted messages on their official websites, blaming the “Democrats” or the “Radical Left” for the shutdown. Many agencies also sent mass emails to their own employees which stated: “Unfortunately, Democrats are blocking this continuing resolution in the U.S. Senate due to unrelated policy demands.” On the day of the shutdown, many Department of Education (ED) employees discovered that the language in the out-of-office messages associated with their government email accounts had been changed to include the following phrase: “Unfortunately, Democrat Senators are blocking passage of H.R. 5371 in the Senate which has led to a lapse in appropriations.” The modified out-of-office messages are written in the first person, as if the individual employee is the person conveying the message. ED employees were unable to remove this statement from their auto-reply message, which will be received by any member of the public and external stakeholders who try to contact these employees.On Oct. 3, 2025, the American Federation of Government Employeesfiled suitagainst ED, alleging that the inclusion of this language in their out-of-office email responses is a violation of the First Amendment’s prohibition on government compelled speech, as well as theHatch Act, which prohibits federal employees from engaging in partisan speech while on duty. They seek declaratory judgment that the modification of employee messages is illegal and preliminary and permanent relief barring the government from continuing to modify out-of-office messages to include partisan political speech.
Case Updates: Nov. 7, 2025:Judge Christopher R. Coopergrantedsummary judgment for the American Federation of Government Employees (AFGE), finding that the administration had violated the First Amendment by adding language to furloughed employees’ out-of-office messages blaming Democrats for shutting down the government. Judge Cooper further granted Plaintiffs a permanent injunction requiring the administration to remove “all partisan political language” from employees’ messages and prohibiting them from including such speech in any future messages.

Case Name: Jane Does 1-2 v. Office of Personnel Management(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-00234
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Executive Action: Solicitation of Information from Career Employees
Case Summary: The Office of Personnel Management announced it was testing a new system to email all civilian federal employees from a single email address,[email protected]. Individuals claiming to be OPM employees subsequently posted online that the emails were being stored on an unsecure server at OPM. Plaintiffs, employees of executive-branch agencies who received “test” emails from[email protected]requesting information, sued. The lawsuit alleges that the new procedure violates the E-Government Act of 2002 and asks the court to require the Office of Personnel Management to conduct a Privacy Impact Assessment before collecting any data from employees, as required under the law.
Case Updates: Feb. 4, 2025:The Plaintiffsrequesteda temporary restraining order.Feb. 6, 2025:Judge Randolph D. Mossdeniedthe TRO request and said an opinion will follow.Feb. 17, 2025:In aMemorandum Opinion and Order, Judge Moss denied plaintiffs’ motion for a TRO on the ground that they had not shown they were likely to have standing or face irreparable injury without emergency relief.

Case Name: Storch et al. v. Hegseth et al.(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-00415
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Executive Action: Termination of Inspectors General outside of Statutory Guidelines
Case Summary: On Jan. 24, 2025, the White House sent two-sentence emails to several Inspectors General (IGs) of federal departments and agencies informing them that they had been terminated from their positions. Plaintiffs, eight IGs, subsequently lost access to their government email accounts and computer systems, and were barred from entering their offices, among other actions. They filed suit, arguing their removal violates the Inspector General Act, which requires Congressional notification 30 days before an IG is removed and substantive, case-specific rationale for removal. The suit also argues defendants’ actions are ultra vires; and that plaintiffs are entitled to a writ of mandamus compelling defendants not to obstruct them in the exercise of their duties. They seek a declaratory judgment that the termination emails are legally ineffective and that plaintiffs remain lawful IGs in their agencies; and an injunction to prevent defendants from taking action to prevent plaintiffs from carrying out their duties as IGs.
Case Updates: Feb. 14, 2025:Plaintiffs filed amotionfor a temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction to prevent the Defendants’ allegedly illegal interference with their official duties. On that same day, the judge reportedly told lawyers representing the fired inspectors general to withdraw their motion for a temporary restraining order.Feb. 18, 2025:U.S. Senators filed anamicus briefin support of Plaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction.Feb. 24, 2025:State Attorneys General of Florida and 19 states submitted anamicus briefin support of Defendants. The State of Tennessee also submitted anamicus briefin support of Defendants.March 8, 2025:Judge Ana Reyes agreed to hold a consolidated hearing on the merits of Plaintiffs’ claims rather than a preliminary injunction hearing. This hearing was held on March 27.June 26, 2025:Plaintiffs filed anotice of recent developmentsrelated to the case in which they emphasized the urgency of the present litigation.Sept. 24, 2025:District Court Judge Reyesdeniedplaintiffs’motionfor a preliminary injunction, declining to reinstate eight fired Inspectors General, despite finding it likely that the administration acted unlawfully in firing them. The court found it “obvious” that President Trump had violated the Inspector General Act (IGA), but said that plaintiffs failed to show irreparable harm: reinstatement could only be temporary given the President’s ability to re-fire them with proper notice and rationale. While recognizing plaintiffs’ “exceptional service” and acknowledging their “legitimate grievances with being fired without explanation,” the court stayed this case pending Supreme Court review inTrump v. Slaughterand directed the parties to file a joint status report afterward on whether plaintiffs will pursue legal remedies such as back pay.Sept. 25, 2025:The case wasstayed.

Case Name: National Trust for Historic Preservation in the United States v. National Park Service(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04316
Case Status: Government Action Not Blocked Pending Appeal
Executive Action: Government Facilities, Property, and Security Modifications
Case Summary: On Dec. 12, 2025, the National Trust for Historic Preservation in the United Statessuedthe National Parks Service, the Department of the Interior, the General Services Administration, and the president over the demolition of the White House’s East Wing. Plaintiff alleges that the administration's actions destroyed historic and cultural resources, caused irreparable harm to the architectural integrity and symbolic appearance of the White House, deprived the public and the National Trust of their rights to informed participation and comment, and blocked access to statutory public review processes in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA). Plaintiff also alleges that Defendants violated the APA for their failure to seek and obtain required NCPC and CFA review and approval, improper segmentation of review, and failure to request or follow required agency advice pursuant to the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA). Plaintiff alleges Defendants failed to prepare and publish an adequate environmental assessment or required environmental impact statement; violated 40 U.S.C. § 8106, for erecting a structure on federal property in D.C. without express congressional authorization; and violated the Constitution’s Property and Separation of Powers Clauses, via the president’s unilateral demolition and construction actions on federal property and by reassigning statutory duties among agencies without congressional authorization. Plaintiff seeks a court order declaring commencement and continuation of the Ballroom Project unlawful, enjoining all further work unless and until all required statutory reviews and approvals are completed.
Case Updates: Dec. 17, 2025:Judge LeondeniedPlaintiff’s motion for a temporary restraining order, ruling that Plaintiff’s claims of potential procedural injury resulting from demolishing the East Wing of the White House were insufficient to demonstrate irreparable harm. Judge Leon also deferred ruling on Plaintiff’s motion for a preliminary injunction until after the court held a hearing scheduled for Jan. 22, 2026.Dec. 29, 2025:Plaintiffamendedits complaint to add an allegation of the violation of the Separation of Powers Clause of the Constitution for the unlawful reorganization of the Executive Branch.Jan. 22, 2026:The court held a hearing on the preliminary injunction motion.Feb. 26, 2026:Judge LeondeniedPlaintiff’s motion for a preliminary injunction, finding that the White House is not an “agency” under the APA and Plaintiff did not bring a necessaryultra viresclaim in their complaint. If Plaintiffs added that claim, Judge Leon wrote, he would consider it “expeditiously.”March 1, 2026:Plaintiffs filed asecond amended complaint.March 31, 2026:In anopinionandorder, Judge Leon granteda preliminary injunction stopping the planned White House ballroom project, finding the President is acting without statutory authority. “[U]nless and until Congress blesses this project through statutory authorization, construction has to stop!,” the judge wrote. The court stayed its order for 14 days.March 31, 2026:The government appealed to the court’s March 31 order to the D.C. Circuit, Case No.26-5101.April 3, 2026:The administration filed an emergencymotionfor a stay pending appeal of the District Court’s March 31 preliminary injunction. In the alternative, the administration requested “at least” a 14-day stay so the Solicitor General could seek emergency relief from the Supreme Court. Per the motion, the East Wing is actively under construction and the building materials onsite must be installed rather than “be exposed to the conditions and elements of an open construction site. Time is of the essence!”April 8, 2026:Plaintiffs filed across-appealto the Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit pursuant to the District Court’s holding that the Plaintiffs were unlikely to succeed on their constitutional claims and declination to enter a preliminary injunction on the Administrative Procedure Act claims. The cross appeal (26-5108) wasconsolidatedwith the appeal (26-5101).April 11, 2026:A three judge panel on the Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit issued a splitorderremanding the case to the District Court for further factfinding on the “safety and security” exception created by the preliminary injunction and extending the stay of the preliminary injunction until April 17 to allow the government to seek emergency relief from the Supreme Court. Judge Rao issued a dissenting statement saying that the district court erred in ruling that the Plaintiffs made a “clear showing” of Article III standing and that the Plaintiffs were likely to succeed on their ultra vires claim.April 16, 2026:Judge Leonamendedand clarified the scope of his earlierpreliminary injunctionafter the D.C. Circuitremandedthe case for further consideration of the order’s “safety and security” exception. Judge Leon wrote that the order fully stops construction of the above-ground ballroom, but allows for below-ground construction to proceed as well as above-ground construction strictly necessary to cover, secure, and protect the underground facility. The amended order also permits temporary measures for personal security of the President and permits construction necessary to protect the construction site and the structural integrity of the White House.April 16, 2026:The administrationappealedJudge Leon’s amended preliminary injunction to the D.C. Circuit (Case No.26-5123).April 17, 2026:The D.C. CircuitgrantedDefendants’emergency motionfor a stay pending appeal of Judge Leon’s April 16, 2026 order and set an expedited briefing schedule, noting: “The purpose of this administrative stay is to give the court sufficient opportunity to consider the emergency motion for a stay pending appeal and should not be construed in any way as a ruling on the merits of that motion.”April 24, 2026:The D.C. CircuitconsolidatedPlaintiff’scross-appealwith the administration’s appeal and directed Plaintiffs to file a statement of issues by May 26.April 27, 2026:Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche filed amotionto dissolve the court’s injunction, arguing that the National Trust for Historic Preservation “suffer from Trump Derangement Syndrome,” and that the shooting on Saturday night “could not have taken place in this new and highly secure facility!”

Case Name: Beatty v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04480
Executive Action: Government Facilities, Property, and Security Modifications
Case Summary: This lawsuit seeks to prevent the renaming of the “John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts” by the Center’s board of trustees to the “Donald J. Trump and The John F. Kennedy Memorial Center for the Performing Arts.” Plaintiff is a member of Congress and an ex officio trustee of the Center who opposes renaming the Center. Defendants include President Trump and the trustees and president of the Center. Plaintiff argues that the Board’s renaming of the Kennedy Center constitutes an ultra vires action that violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) and federal law: “By statute, Congress has named the center as ‘the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts.’ Under our constitutional system, only Congress can modify a statute, and Congress must do so by enacting a new statute. Congress has not changed the Kennedy Center’s name.” Plaintiff primarily asks the court to declare the name-change unlawful, enjoin implementation of the name-change, and restore the statutory name of the Center.
Case Updates: March 14, 2026:Judge Coopergrantedin part and denied in part Plaintiff’s motion for a TRO, finding that Representative Beatty was likely to succeed on her claim that the Kennedy Center's Board had violated her statutory fiduciary duties under 20 U.S.C. § 76l by denying her access to information about the planned closure and categorically barring her from participating in Board deliberations. The court ordered Defendants to provide Beatty with specified categories of documents — including construction plans, budgets, affected performance contracts, and expert reports — at least 24 hours before the March 16 Board meeting, and ordered that she not be categorically barred from speaking at the meeting; the court declined to order that Beatty be permitted to vote, finding the irreparable harm and balance of equities did not clearly support emergency relief on that question.

Case Name: DC Preservation League v. Board of Trustees of the Kennedy Center(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00981
Executive Action: Government Facilities, Property, and Security Modifications
Case Summary: On March 23, 2026, a coalition of eight historic preservation and architecture non-profitsfiled acomplaintagainst the Board of Trustees of the Kennedy Center, the Smithsonian Institute, the National Park Service (NPS), the Department of the Interior (DOI), the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (ACE), and the National Capital Planning Commission (NCPC). Plaintiffs allege that the proposed demolition, new construction, major reconstruction, and major aesthetic transformation of the Kennedy Center site and grounds violate the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA), the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA), the National Capital Planning Act (NCPA), and the Kennedy Center’s enabling statutes. Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief.

Case Name: Washington Area Bicyclist Association, Inc. v. Burgum(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00988
Executive Action: Government Facilities, Property, and Security Modifications
Case Summary: On March 23, 2026, the Washington Area Bicyclist Association (WABA), a cycling non-profit, filed acomplaintagainst the Department of the Interior (DOI), the National Park Service (NPS), and the Department of Transportation (DOT) seeking declaratory and injunctive relief from Defendants’ decision to remove the 15th Street, Ohio Drive, SW, and East Basin Drive bicycle lanes in Washington. WABA alleges the decision violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), the National Capital Planning Act (NCPA), the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA), and the National Park Service Organic Act (NPSOA).

Case Name: Cultural Landscape Foundation v. U.S. Department of the Interior(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01593
Executive Action: Government Facilities, Property, and Security Modifications
Case Summary: Beginning in mid-April 2026, the National Park Service (NPS) and the Department of the Interior (DOI) commenced a project to resurface the basin of the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool with a vivid blue coating in advance of the nation’s 250th anniversary celebration on July 4, 2026. The Reflecting Pool is part of the National Mall Historic District and is listed in the National Register of Historic Places; the NPS has previously identified its dark grey, achromatic basin, which was deliberately designed to create the pool’s reflective depth, as a “character-defining feature” subject to consultation and review processes under the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA). NPSreportedlyawarded a $6.9 million no-bid contract for the resurfacing without following proper consultation and review processes under the NHPA and the National Environmental Policy Act.On May 11, 2026, the Cultural Landscape Foundation (TCLF), a nonprofit dedicated to the stewardship of historic designed landscapes, and Charles A. Birnbaum, TCLF’s founder and a former NPS official who authored the federal Standards and Guidelines for the treatment of historic properties, filed acomplaintalleging that Defendants violated the Administrative Procedure Act (APA). They allege that the administration illegally proceeded without completing mandatory consultation and processes under the NHPA and without preparing an Environmental Impact Statement as required by the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA). Plaintiffs also allege that TCLF was denied the right to participate as a consulting party and that Birnbaum has suffered concrete aesthetic injury. Plaintiffs seek a declaration that the resurfacing violates the NHPA, NEPA, and the APA; vacatur of any authorizing agency decision; preliminary and permanent injunctions and a temporary stay halting further alteration; and restoration of the Reflecting Pool’s historic elements.

Case Name: The Interfaith Alliance v. Trump(S.D.N.Y.)1:26-cv-01075
Executive Action: Religious Liberty Commission
Case Summary: On May 1, 2025, President Donald Trump issued an Executive Order to create theReligious Liberty Commission, an advisory committee focused on “defend[ing] religious liberty for all Americans” and “celebrat[ing] America’s peaceful religious pluralism.” On Feb. 9, 2026, an inter-faith coalition of Plaintiffs, including organizations specifically representing Muslims, Sikhs, and Hindus, filed acomplaintagainst federal officials challenging the composition and disclosure failures of the administration’s Religious Liberty Commission. Defendants include Trump, Attorney General Pam Bondi, the Religious Liberty Commission, and Mary Margaret Bush, the designated federal officer of the Commission. Plaintiffs argue the Commission as currently constituted violates the Federal Advisory Committees Act (FACA) in two ways. First, Plaintiffs allege the Commission’s members are “almost exclusively” Christian, in violation of FACA’s requirement that advisory committees be “fairly balanced.” Second, Plaintiffs allege the Commission has failed to make available to the public meeting minutes and other Commission records required to be publicly disclosed under FACA. Plaintiffs allege these failures to comply with FACA in turn violate the Administrative Procedure Act’s (APA) mandates that the Administration act lawfully and with reasoned explanation. Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief under the APA and the Mandamus Act, declaring Defendant’s administration of the Commission to be arbitrary, capricious, and contrary to the law. Plaintiffs specifically request the Court declare the Commission’s makeup to be unlawfully unbalanced, and enjoin Defendants to both attach a disclaimer noting its unbalanced membership to any reports or recommendations it produces and release to the public documents produced for the Commission.

Case Name: American Federation of Government Employees AFL-C10 v. U.S. Federal Labor Relations Authority(D. Mass.)1:26-cv-11747
Executive Action: Changes to Union Petition Processes
Case Summary: Federal employees have historically sought to form unions by petitioning a Regional Director of the Federal Labor Relations Authority (FLRA). On March 24, 2026, the FLRA issued anInterim Final Ruleremoving Regional Directors from the process of adjudicating these types of representation petitions, effective April 23, 2026.A coalition of major federal employee unions filed acomplaintchallenging the rule. Plaintiffs allege the rule violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as arbitrary and capricious under 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A) — for failing to provide a reasoned explanation or address reliance interests — and as procedurally defective under § 706(2)(D) for bypassing required notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b)–(c). Per the complaint, the reorganization will delay the processing of pending and future representation petitions, eliminating an efficient tier of decisionmaking and extinguishing a level of review that is particularly critical given that unit-determination orders are expressly excluded from judicial review under 5 U.S.C. § 7123(a)(2).  Plaintiffs separately challenge the rule’s thirty-day effective date as independently arbitrary and capricious. Plaintiffs seek a declaratory judgment that the rule is null and void, preliminary and permanent injunctive relief, and a stay under 5 U.S.C. § 705.

Case Name: Dunn v. Bessent(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-0171
Executive Action: Anti-Weaponization Fund
Case Summary: On May 18, 2026, the Department of Justice (DOJ)announcedthe “Anti-Weaponization Fund” (the Fund) as part of the settlement ofTrump v. Internal Revenue Service— a Southern District of Florida suit brought in January 2026 by President Donald Trump, Donald Trump Jr., Eric Trump, and the Trump Organization over leaked tax-return information. In a same-dayletter, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche directed that $1.776 billion be paid from the Judgment Fund into an account for the Fund. Thesettlement agreementprovided for a five-member commission, with members appointed by the Attorney General and removable by the President, authorized to issue apologies and monetary relief to claimants asserting “Lawfare” or “Weaponization” claims. A May 19addendumthen purported to release and bar existing or potential claims, examinations, and reviews involving the Trump plaintiffs, related individuals and entities, and pre-settlement tax returns.Former U.S. Capitol Police Officer Harry Dunn and Metropolitan Police Department Officer Daniel Hodges, who defended the Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021, filedsuiton May 20, 2026, against President Trump, Blanche, and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, challenging the Fund’s creation and funding. Plaintiffs allege they have faced persistent threats from pardoned Jan. 6 rioters and Proud Boys, and that the Fund will likely embolden and finance people who assaulted or threatened Capitol defenders. Plaintiffs claim DOJ’s creation of the Fund violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) because no statute authorizes a new commission or “weaponization” claims process; that DOJ and the Department of the Treasury (Treasury) exceeded Judgment Fund authority under 31 U.S.C. § 1304 and 28 U.S.C. § 2414 and acted arbitrarily and capriciously; that payments to Jan. 6 rioters would violate the Fourteenth Amendment’s prohibition on assuming debts incurred in aid of insurrection; and that Defendants acted ultra vires. Plaintiffs ask the court to set aside the Fund’s creation, funding, and alleged authorization to assume insurrection-related debts; reverse any transfer already made into the Fund; bar payments to any claimant; and declare Defendants’ conduct unlawful and unconstitutional.

Case Name: Floyd v. Department of Justice(E.D. Va.)1:26-cv-01399
Executive Action: Anti-Weaponization Fund
Case Summary: Plaintiffs filed acomplaintseeking to bar the creation of a $1.776 billion “anti-weaponization” judgment fund. The plaintiffs include a fired January 6 prosecutor, a professor acquitted after prosecution for protesting an immigration raid, the City of New Haven, the National Abortion Federation, and Common Cause. Plaintiffs allege that the Fund constitutes unconstitutional viewpoint discrimination in violation of the First Amendment; violates the Fifth Amendment's equal protection guarantee by restricting eligibility to those claiming harm from Democratic — but not Republican — officials; violates the separation of powers by usurping congressional prerogatives; and violates the Administrative Procedure Act including by allegedly engaging in arbitrary and capricious decision-making (5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A)) and changing policies without observance of required procedure (§ 706(2)(D)). Plaintiffs also raise an Appointments Clause claim, a Fourteenth Amendment Section 4 insurrection-debt claim (which states that the United States shall not “assume or pay any debt or obligation incurred in aid of insurrection or rebellion against the United States”), and an ultra vires count.

Case Name: Learning Resources v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01248
Executive Action: Tariffs
Case Summary: Since Feb. 1, 2025, Trump has used Executive Orders to impose tariffs on products from several countries, including a 25% tariff on products imported from Canada and Mexico and a 10% tariff on products imported from China. Plaintiffs, family-owned businesses whose products will be impacted by the tariffs, sued Trump, alleging that the EOs exceed the authority delegated to the President in the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), violate the Administrative Procedure Act, and are an unconstitutional violation of separation of powers. They seek declaratory judgment that the administration’s actions are unlawful and unconstitutional, and preliminary and permanent injunctions against implementing the tariff EOs.
Case Updates: April 24, 2025:The administrationmoved to transferthe case to the U.S. Court of International Trade. On that same day, Plaintiffs’ filed amotionfor a preliminary injunction to prevent the Agency Defendants from taking any action to implement or enforce the tariff EOs and their amendments against Plaintiffs.May 1, 2025:Defendants filed theiroppositionto Plaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction. Defendants assert that the court lacks subject matter jurisdiction and that the issue of Trump’s tariffs under the IEEPA is a political question because Congress lawfully delegated authority to the President to regulate importation through the imposition of tariffs under special circumstances.May 7, 2025:Plaintiffs filed theiroppositionto the administration's motion to transfer, noting that the U.S. Court of International Trade has heard zero cases arising out of IEEPA since the IEEPA’s enactment. On that same day, Plaintiffsrepliedin support of their motion for a preliminary injunction.May 29, 2025:The District Court granted apreliminary injunction. On that same day, Defendantsappealedthe preliminary injunction order to the D.C. Circuit.June 3, 2025:The District Courtgrantedthe Defendants’ motion to stay enforcement of the Court’s order preliminarily enjoining the collection of tariffs from the Plaintiffs pending disposition of the pending appeal before the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit.July 10, 2025:The parties filed ajoint status reportrequesting a stay of the proceedings pending the D.C. Circuit’s resolution of the pending appeal. On July 18, Judge Rudolph Contrerasgranteda stay of the proceedings.Sept. 9, 2025:The Supreme Courtgrantedcertiorari and the administration’s motion to expedite a hearing on whether the administration possesses authority under the International Economic Emergency Powers Act (IEEPA) to institute wide ranging tariffs.Nov. 5, 2025:The Supreme Courtbriefingoccurred withoral argument.Feb. 20, 2026:The Supreme Courtruled6-3 that the president does not have authority to unilaterally impose tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA).Chief Justice Roberts delivered the opinion of the Court with respect to Parts I, II–A–1, and II–B, finding that IEEPA’s grant of authority to “regulate . . . importation” does not encompass the power to impose tariffs. In Part II-A-2, joined by Justices Gorsuch and Barrett, the Chief Justice invoked the major questions doctrine, reasoning that no president had ever used IEEPA to impose tariffs, that the economic stakes dwarfed prior major questions cases, and that Congress has consistently delegated tariff powers only in explicit and limited terms. The Court also held that the D.D.C. lacked jurisdiction and that the Court of International Trade had exclusive jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. §1581(i)(1).Justices Kagan, Sotomayor, and Jackson concurred in the result, but wrote that the major questions doctrine was not necessary to conclude that the policy lacked authorization. Justices Kavanaugh, Alito, and Thomas dissented, arguing that “regulate . . . importation” plainly encompasses tariffs and that the major questions doctrine should not apply to foreign affairs statutes.April 3, 2026:In light of the Supreme Court’s Feb. 20, 2026decisionon the case, the Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit filed a per curiamorderremanding the case back to the District Court with instructions to dismiss the case for lack of jurisdiction.

Case Name: V.O.S. Selections Inc. v. Trump(Ct. Intl. Trade)1:25-cv-00066
Executive Action: Tariffs
Case Summary: On April 2, 2025, President Trump issued Executive Order (EO) 14257 (the “Liberation Day Order”) imposing incremental tariffs of 10% on all imports into the United States as well as elevated tariffs on individual countries. Subsequent EOs, including EO 14266 and additional memoranda, paused many of the elevated tariffs but increased the tariff on most goods from China to 145%. As the statutory basis for the Liberation Day Order, the EO cites the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), which provides the President with certain authority in the event of “an unusual and extraordinary threat with respect to which a national emergency has been declared.” The Liberation Day Order asserts that the existence of bilateral trade deficits in goods constitutes a national emergency. On April 14, five owner-operated businesses, which are unable to source their products or raw materials domestically and therefore rely on international imports to run their companies, sued President Trump and various government agencies, alleging that the levying of tariffs is not authorized under the IEEPA. They contend that given the significance of the economic impact on the United States, the “major questions doctrine” requires that Congress must have clearly delegated authority for tariffs under the IEEPA. Plaintiffs also argue that in any event a trade deficit in goods is neither an emergency nor an unusual or extraordinary threat, and that if the IEEPA did extend such power to the President, it would be an unconstitutional delegation of Congress’s power to impose tariffs. Plaintiffs seek a declaratory judgement that the IEEPA does not provide statutory authority to unilaterally impose tariffs, that neither the national emergency nor the “unusual and extraordinary threat” required by the IEEPA has been identified, and that the imposition of tariffs under the EO’s is an unconstitutional delegation of legislative power, and seek the enjoin the operation of the Liberation Day Order and EO 14266.
Case Updates: April 18, 2025:Plaintiffs filed amotionrequesting the court to issue a temporary restraining order (TRO) and preliminary injunction and/or enter summary judgment for a permanent injunction. In this motion, Plaintiffs requested the Court block the imposition of all tariffs set forth in EO 14257 and award money damages to cover what Plaintiffs have already paid as a result of the tariffs.April 21, 2025:Defendants filed anoppositionto Plaintiffs’ April 18 TRO motion and asserted Plaintiffs’ motion fails to satisfy any of the requirements for extraordinary remedy. The court ultimatelydeniedPlaintiffs’ motion on April 22.April 29, 2025:Defendants responded inoppositionto Plaintiffs’ preliminary injunction and summary judgment motions, asserting the IEEPA authorizes the President to “regulate” importation during a national emergency by imposing tariffs and that Trump’s emergency declaration is a nonjusticiable political question.May 6, 2025:The Plaintiffs filed areply briefin support of their motion for preliminary injunction and motion for summary judgment, claiming that the Plaintiffs are likely to prevail on the merits, will suffer irreparable harm if the tariffs imposed by Executive Order 14257 remain in effect, and that the public interest favors the granting of a preliminary injunction.May 28, 2025:The court granted plaintiffs’summary judgmentholding the tariffs unlawful.May 29, 2025:The Federal Circuit granted Defendants’ motion to consolidate the appeal withState of Oregon v. Trump, No. 25-1813, and granted anadministrative staywhile the case is on appeal.June 10, 2025:The Federal Circuitgranted a staypending appeal and ordered an expedited en banc consideration on the merits.Aug. 29, 2025:The Federal Circuit, sitting en banc,rejectedthe government’s motion for appeal.Sept. 3, 2025:The administrationfileda Writ of Certiorari to the Supreme Court after the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuitaffirmedthe lower court’s initialfindingthat the administration did not possess authority under IEEPA to institute wide ranging tariffs.The administration also filed amotion to expedite the case, requesting oral arguments in the case by the first week of November.Sept. 9, 2025:The Supreme Courtgrantedcertiorari and the administration’s motion to expedite a hearing on whether the administration possesses authority under IEEPA to institute wide ranging tariffs. The Court also consolidated this case withLearning Resources v. Trump, No. 24-1287, for briefing and oral argument, with all subsequent filings to proceed on the latter lead docket.Nov. 5, 2025:The Supreme Courtbriefingoccurred withoral argumentheld.Feb. 20, 2026:The Supreme Courtruled6-3 that the president does not have authority to unilaterally impose tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA).Chief Justice Roberts delivered the opinion of the Court with respect to Parts I, II–A–1, and II–B, finding that IEEPA’s grant of authority to “regulate . . . importation” does not encompass the power to impose tariffs. In Part II-A-2, joined by Justices Gorsuch and Barrett, the Chief Justice invoked the major questions doctrine, reasoning that no president had ever used IEEPA to impose tariffs, that the economic stakes dwarfed prior major questions cases, and that Congress has consistently delegated tariff powers only in explicit and limited terms. The Court also held that the D.D.C. lacked jurisdiction and that the Court of International Trade had exclusive jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. §1581(i)(1).Justices Kagan, Sotomayor, and Jackson concurred in the result, but wrote that the major questions doctrine was not necessary to conclude that the policy lacked authorization. Justices Kavanaugh, Alito, and Thomas dissented, arguing that “regulate . . . importation” plainly encompasses tariffs and that the major questions doctrine should not apply to foreign affairs statutes.

Case Name: Susan Webber et al v. U.S. Department of Homeland Security et al(D. Mont.)4:25-cv-00026
Executive Action: Tariffs
Case Summary: On February 1, 2025, President Donald Trump issued Executive Order 14193 (Imposing Duties to Address the Flow of Illicit Drugs Across our Northern Border), and subsequently amended that Executive Order through Executive Orders 14197 and 14231. These Executive Orders declare a national emergency and invoke the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 (IEEPA) to impose certain tariffs, including on goods from Canada. Additionally, on February 10, Trump issued Proclamations 10895 and 10896 imposing additional tariffs on aluminum and steel products under the Trade Expansion Act of 1962. Finally, on April 2, 2025, President Trump issued Executive Order 14257 (Regulating Imports with a Reciprocal Tariff to Rectify Trade Practices that Contribute to Large and Persistent Annual United States Goods Trade Deficits) that imposed additional tariffs on countries around the world, including Canada. Plaintiffs are two members of the Blackfeet Nation in Montana who conduct personal and economic business across the U.S.-Canada border in Montana. They allege that the Executive Orders and related proclamations will increase the cost of goods for the tribal community in their personal and business lives. Plaintiffs allege that President Trump acted ultra vires in establishing the tariffs on goods from Canada, because the President does not have constitutional or statutory authority to impose tariffs, and that these tariffs would violate the Jay Treaty exempting Native Americans from being subject to duties or tariffs when crossing the border. Plaintiffs also argue that the IEEPA does not empower the President to establish tariffs. They further allege that the Executive Orders and related proclamations violate the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment, and that the Executive Orders and related proclamations are unconstitutionally vague. Plaintiffs requested that the court declare the Executive Orders and related proclamations unconstitutional as violative of the Separation of Powers, Article I, Article VI, and the Fifth Amendment of the Constitution, and of the Jay Treaty. They asked the court to preliminarily and permanently enjoin the orders, declare that tariffs cannot be imposed on cross-border transactions at Montana ports of entry, and that tariffs cannot be imposed on tribal members.
Case Updates: April 4, 2025:Plaintiffs filed amotionandaccompanying briefseeking a preliminary and permanent injunction. Plaintiffs claim that they are likely to succeed on the merits on the bases that the Executive Orders exceed Constitutional authority, statutory authority, and are in violation of the Jay Treaty, as well as due process.April 11, 2025:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaint, adding Plaintiffs David and Rhonda Mountain Chief.April 28, 2025:The Court granted the Department of Homeland Security’s motion to transfer the case to the US International Court of Trade andtransferredthe casewithout addressingany of the merits of the complaint. Plaintiffs haveappealed the transfer.June 2, 2025:Judge Dana ChristensendeniedPlaintiffs’ motion for reconsideration for the order transferring jurisdiction of the matter to the Court of International Trade.Sept. 5, 2025:The Ninth CircuitdeniedDefendants’motionto hold proceedings in abeyance.Sept. 12, 2025:The Ninth Circuit denied Defendants’ renewedmotionto hold proceedings in abeyance.Jan. 8, 2026:In a text order, the Ninth Circuit held the proceedings in abeyance pending the Supreme Court’s final disposition inTrump v. V.O.S. Selections, Inc.(No. 25-250) andLearning Resources, Inc. v. Trump(No. 24-1287).March 2, 2026:The appeal was dismissed for lack of jurisdiction, consistent with the Supreme Court’s recentdecisioninLearning Resources, Inc. v. Trump. All other pending motions were denied as moot.

Case Name: State of California v. Trump(N.D. Cal.)3:25-cv-03372
Case Status: Case Closed/Dismissed in Favor of Government
Executive Action: Tariffs
Case Summary: On Feb. 1, 2025, President Donald Trump issuedExecutive Order (EO) 14193(Imposing Duties to Address the Flow of Illicit Drugs Across our Northern Border), and on April 2, 2025, Trump issuedEO 14257(the “Liberation Day Order”). Together, these EOs (collectively, the Tariff EOs) impose a broad tariff regime on imports from countries around the world. On April 4, 2025, Plaintiffs California Governor Gavin Newsom and the State of California broughtsuitalleging that the President does not have authority to impose the tariffs outlined in these EOs. California claims that the tariffs will create substantial economic harm to the state in various forms, including lost tax revenue and impacts to the State’s ability to import and export goods. California alleges that the President lacks authority to impose the tariffs described by the Tariff EOs. California argues that the power to levy tariffs is exclusive to Congress, and that Congress can and has delegated this authority to the executive branch via statute. The Tariff EOs invoked the International Economic Emergency Powers Act (IEEPA) as the source of authority for the tariffs. However, California alleges that the IEEPA confers only limited authority to the President, and that it does not provide the President with authority to impose tariffs. Instead, California argues that other statutes grant the President authority to levy tariffs, but that those statutes require substantial process and congressional participation, which did not occur in this instance. The State brought two counts, arguing that the tariffs areultra viresand a violation of the Separation of Powers. California has asked the court to declare the tariffs unlawful and void, and to enjoin Defendants from taking any action to implement the Tariff EOs.
Case Updates: April 17, 2025:Defendants filed amotionto transfer the case to the U.S. Court of International Trade, claiming that that court has exclusive jurisdiction to hear civil actions challenging the imposition of tariffs.May 1, 2025:Plaintiffs filed theiroppositionto Defendants’ motion to transfer the case to the U.S. Court of International Trade, claiming that the current court has jurisdiction to decide the lawsuit because the action does not arise out of a law providing for tariffs.May 8, 2025:Defendantsrepliedin support of their motion to transfer the case to the U.S. Court of International Trade.May 13, 2025:Plaintiffsfileda motion for a preliminary injunction, requesting that the court restrain Agency Defendants from implementing or enforcing the IEEPA Tariff EOs.June 2, 2025:The district court issued anorderdenying the government’s motion to transfer anddismissed the casewithout prejudice for lack of jurisdiction.June 2, 2025:Plaintiffs filed anotice of appealto the Ninth Circuit from the district court’s June 2 order.Sept. 12, 2025: The Ninth CircuitgrantedDefendants’ Sept. 10 motion to hold the appeal in abeyance pending the Supreme Court’s resolution of V.O.S. Selections, Inc. v. Trump and Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump.March 3, 2026:The appeal wasdismissedfor lack of jurisdiction, consistent with the Supreme Court’s recentdecisioninLearning Resources, Inc. v. Trump.

Case Name: Oregon v. U.S. Department of Homeland Security(Ct. Intl. Trade)1:25-cv-00077
State A.G.'s: State A.G. Plaintiffs, Plaintiff-side State Amicus
Case Status: Government Action Not Blocked Pending Appeal
Executive Action: Tariffs
Case Summary: Since Feb. 1, 2025, President Donald Trump has used Executive Orders (EOs) to impose tariffs on products from several countries (collectively, Tariff EOs), including a 25% tariff on products imported from Canada and Mexico and a 10% tariff on products imported from China. They seek declarations that the Tariff EOs and agency guidance implementing the EOs are unlawful and preliminary and permanent injunctions against implementation of the EOs and agency guidance.

Case Name: Smirk & Dagger Games v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03857
Executive Action: Tariffs
Case Summary: Beginning Feb. 1, 2025, President Donald Trump issued Executive Orders to impose tariffs (Tariff EOs) on US trading partners (in particular China, Mexico, and Canada) under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 (IEEPA). Plaintiffs are a board game company that primarily imports from China, a champagne importer that imports from France, and a New York-based mannequin manufacturer and seller that primarily imports from China. Plaintiffs are suing the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), Customs and Border Protection (CBP), the Department of Treasury (Treasury), the Department of Commerce (DOC) and their officials, as well as the President and Executive Office, on behalf of a class of similarly situated individuals. Plaintiffs claim that Trump acted beyond his legal authority and violated the Vesting Clause of the Constitution by bypassing Congress’s exclusive power to impose tariffs and regulate foreign commerce in invoking the IEEPA. Plaintiffs allege that DHS, CBP, and their officials violated the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) by modifying the Harmonized Tariff Schedule (HTS). Plaintiffs seek a permanent injunction preventing implementation or enforcement of the Tariff EOs and to vacate all tariff modifications.
Case Updates: Dec. 17, 2025:Defendants filed amotionto stay this case pending the Supreme Court’s disposition ofLearning Resources, Inc. v. Trump, No. 24-1287, andTrump v. V.O.S. Selections, Inc., No. 25-250. The court granted this motion on that same day.March 6, 2026:In aminute order, Judge Contreras ordered the case be transferred to the Court of International Trade.

Case Summary: Beginning Feb. 1, 2025, President Donald Trump issued Executive Orders to impose tariffs (Tariff EOs) on US trading partners (in particular China, Mexico, and Canada) under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 (IEEPA). Trump, affirmed a court decision that determined the Tariff EOs exceeded Trump’s authority under IEEPA that the imposition of these tariffs is unlawful. On Feb. 27, 2026, Plaintiff, US-based importer of merchandise who was subjected to these tariffs on its imports,suedU.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP), CBP Commissioner Rodney S. Scott , and the United States, alleging that these Defendants continued to apply tariffs under the Tariff EOs to Plaintiff’s imports until Feb. 24, 2026–four days after the Supreme Court declared the Tariff EOs unlawful. The complaint asserts that the tariffs are ultra vires under the Supreme Court’s ruling inLearning Resources, Inc. v. Trump. Plaintiff asks that the court declare the collection of tariffs under the Tariff EOs unlawful, enjoin Defendants from imposing additional duties under the Tariff EOs, and order Defendants to issue full refunds and interest for all EEPA duties Plaintiff has paid as a result of the Tariff EOs.
