Case Name: The State of Oregon et al. v. Trump(Ct. Int’l Trade)1:26-cv-01472
Executive Action: Tariffs
Case Summary: Beginning Feb. 1, 2025, President Donald Trump issued Executive Orders to impose tariffs (Tariff EOs) on US trading partners (in particular China, Mexico, and Canada) under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 (IEEPA). On March 5, 2026, a coalition of 24 states filed acomplaintagainst the Trump administration challenging the ten percent tariff imposed on imports worldwide on the same day the Supreme Court held the IEEPA tariffs unlawful inLearning Resources, Inc. v. Trump. The states argue that the new tariffs, imposed underSection 122 of the Trade Act of 1974are also unlawful as exceeding the authority delegated to the President under that statute to deal with “balance-of-payments” deficits. The ten percent tariff, the states argue, is tied to the nation’s trade deficit, not its balance of payments, of which the trade deficit is merely one component. The states also argue that Section 122 was enacted as a result of currency crises in the 1970s prior to the end of the fixed-rate currency exchange system. The states allege that the new tariffs areultra vires, violate separation of powers, and were imposed without authority under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(C), and ask for an injunction blocking the new tariffs from being imposed and refund of Section 122 tariffs already collected.
Case Updates: May 7, 2026:In a separateorderandopinion, a panel of the U.S. Court of International Trade granted summary judgment for the Private Plaintiffs and the State of Washington, declaredProclamation 11012invalid as contrary to law, and entered a permanent injunction barring collection of the Section 122 surcharge against the Importer Plaintiffs. The majority held that Proclamation 11012 is contrary to law because Section 122 authorizes surcharges only to address “balance-of-payments deficits,” and the Proclamation instead relied on trade and current account deficits, which the statute treats as distinct. The court ordered Defendants to implement the injunction within five days and to refund with interest any Section 122 duties paid by Importer Plaintiffs before implementation. The court dismissed without prejudice the claims of the remaining non-importer Plaintiffs. Judge Stanceu dissented.May 8, 2026:The administrationappealedthe ruling to the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit, Case No.26-1804.May 12, 2026:The Federal Circuit Court of AppealsgrantedDefendants’motion to stay enforcementof the May 7, 2026, order enjoining President Trump’s Feb. 2026 tariff policies pending appeal, and to consolidate the appeals ofThe State of Oregon v. Trump, Case No. 26-1804, andBurlap and Barrel, Inc., v. Trump, Case No. 26-1805.

Case Name: American Oversight v. Hegseth(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-00883
Case Status: Government Action Temporarily Blocked in Part; Temporary Block Denied in Part
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On March 24, The Atlanticreportedthat members of the Trump administration used the commercial messaging app Signal to discuss military operations in Yemen (see also March 26 follow-upreport). Signal messages are not automatically forwarded to government email addresses, and the app has a functionality that allows messages to auto-delete, which was allegedly enabled during the Yemen discussions. American Oversight, a nonprofit focused on government transparency that regularly files Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests with the federal government, filed suit against federal agency officials and the National Archives, arguing they are injured because government use of Signal prevents Plaintiffs from receiving lawfully requested records. Plaintiffs allege Defendants’ use of Signal violates the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) because (1) Defendants' use Signal is not in accordance with the Federal Records Act (FRA) and amounts to removal and destruction of agency records; and (2) National Archives defendants should have known records were being removed and destroyed (the acting National Archivist, Marco Rubio, was allegedly in the group Signal chat) and failed to take nondiscretionary actions to preserve them under the FRA. Plaintiffs seek declaratory judgments that messages sent through Signal in the course of agency business are subject to the FRA, that failure to preserve them is a violation of the FRA, and that defendants have violated the FRA. They seek preliminary and permanent injunctive relief ordering defendants to preserve records in accordance with the law.
Case Updates: March 26, 2025:Plaintiff filed amotion for a temporary restraining orderto order Defendants to stop allegedly destroying records, initiate actions to recover any destroyed records related to the Yemen Signal chat, and take steps to confirm that any use of a messaging app with an auto-delete function complies with the Federal Records Act.March 27, 2025:After a hearing, Chief Judge James Boasberg issued anorder, “as agreed by the parties,” for “Defendants shall promptly make best efforts to preserve all Signal communications from March 11-15, 2025.” The court also ordered defendants to file a status report by March 31 “setting forth the steps that they have taken to implement such preservation.”April 10, 2025:Following a hearing, the courtorderedthe Defendants to provide supplemental declarations to address “specific clarifications” missing from the first round of declarations.April 21, 2025:Plaintifffiled an amended complaintreflecting additional details of Defendants’ alleged violations of federal record-keeping laws, including additional reporting on their use of Signal group chats and admissions by Defendants that they had failed to fully preserve records of their conversations. Case Name: CREW v. CDC(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01020

Case Summary: On April 1, 2025, the Trump administration began the process of cutting nearly 10,000 jobs in the Health and Human Services Department (HHS), including closing the office within the Centers for Disease Control (CDC) that handles information requests under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA). On May 8, Plaintiffrepliedin further support of their preliminary injunction motionJune 4, 2025:The Courtruledthat CREW has not shown a likelihood that it will suffer irreparable injury without injunctive relief, and thus denied CREW’s motion for a preliminary injunction.June 25, 2025:Plaintiffsamendedtheir complaint, removing their APA claim that the CDC’s agency action was arbitrary and capricious.Aug. 27, 2025:The courtgrantedthe parties’ joint motion to stay dispositive briefing on Count One (the FOIA wrongful-withholding claim) and their joint motion to modify briefing deadlines, ordering Defendants to provide an interim FOIA response by Sept. 15 and to file their opposition to Plaintiffs’ motion to dismiss by Sept. 4.Dec. 29, 2025:The parties submitted ajoint status reportsharing that Defendants had made progress processing Plaintiff’s five FOIA requests.March 31, 2026:The courtdeniedDefendants’ motion to dismiss and denied without prejudice both parties’ motions for summary judgment on Count Two (alleged policy-or-practice FOIA violation), finding the record too “mixed, stale, and incomplete” to support the injunctive relief CREW requested but leaving open the possibility of renewal on an updated record. The court granted Defendant’s motion to dismiss Count Three (the Plaintiff’s APA claim) for failure to state a claim.

Case Name: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington v. Office of Management and Budget(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01051
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On or around March 24, 2025, the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) took down a publicly accessible website and database that it had previously maintained to make available documents related to OMB decisions regarding apportionment of congressionally-appropriated funds. On April 8, Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW) brought suit against OMB, alleging that OMB is statutorily required to maintain this database and website. CREW argues that taking down the website makes it impossible for them to monitor for potential withholding of appropriated funds, identify misuse of funds, request related records, and otherwise rely on such information. CREW argues that OMB’s decision to take down this website and database is a violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA). CREW argues that the decision is contrary to law under the APA because it violates (1) the 2022 and 2023 Consolidated Appropriations Acts, which together established the directive to create and maintain this database and website; and (2) the Paperwork Reduction Act, which requires that agencies provide adequate notice when initiating, substantially modifying, or terminating significant information dissemination products. CREW also argues that the decision was arbitrary and capricious under the APA. CREW has asked the court to declare OMB’s decision unlawful, and to grant a preliminary and permanent injunction requiring OMB to restore the website and make all relevant information available.
Case Updates: April 18, 2025:CREW filed amotionfor a preliminary injunction and partial summary judgment on its APA claims along with asupporting memorandumof law. CREW requested that the court issue a preliminary injunction requiring OMB to restore the Public Apportionments Database and the apportionment information they unlawfully removed.April 30, 2025:OMB filed itsoppositionto CREW’s motion for a preliminary injunction and partial summary judgment. OMB asserted that CREW lacks standing and that its claims are without merit.May 4, 2025:CREWrepliedto OMB’s opposition to CREW’s motion for a preliminary injunction.May 4, 2025:OMB filed asur-replyto plaintiff’s motion for a preliminary injunction and for a partial motion for summary judgment reiterating their allegation that CREW’s request for preliminary injunction and partial summary judgement fails.July 21, 2025:Judge Sullivangranteda summary judgment in part for the plaintiffs, holding that the administration’s deletion of thePublic Apportionments Databaseviolates public disclosure requirements. The court noted “there is nothing unconstitutional about Congress requiring the Executive Branch to inform the public of how it is apportioning the public’s money.”July 23, 2025:Following Judge Sullivan’ssummary judgmentin part, which rules the administration’s deletion of thePublic Apportionments Databaseviolates public disclosure requirements, the administrationappealedto the D.C. Circuit and filed a motion to stay the judgment pending appeal.July 23, 2025:Judge Sullivandeniedthe administration’s motion to stay the court’ssummary judgment, which found the administration’s deletion of thePublic Apportionments Databaseviolates public disclosure requirements. Judge Sullivan noted the administration’s conduct “unequivocally violates federal law.” He further wrote “[t]he Court will not be complicit in further delaying Plaintiff and the public of information they are entitled to know as a matter of federal law!!” On that same day, the D.C. Circuit administrativelystayedJudge Sullivan’s July 21 summary judgment order.July 28, 2025:Judge Sullivan enteredfinal judgmentin favor of Plaintiffs.Aug. 9, 2025:The D.C. Circuitdissolvedits July 23 administrative stay and denied the Government’s request for a stay pending appeal. The order applied to the consolidated appeal,Protect Democracy Project v. OMB, No. 25-5267.Sept. 19, 2025:CREW filed amotion to enforcethe court’s July 21 summary judgment order.Jan. 28, 2026:Judge SullivangrantedPlaintiffs’ two motions to enforce the court’s July 21, 2025 Summary Judgment, finding that OMB incorporated-by-reference the terms of certain spend plans in legally binding footnotes and ordering these spend plans be made publicly available on the Public Apportionments Database.

The Department of Commerce (DOC) shared it had not found any responsive records to Plaintiff’s FOIA requests but was conducting supplemental searches and expected those searches would be complete by Jan. 15, 2026. The Department of Agriculture (DOA) shared that it had released 31 pages of responsive records to Plaintiff and was re-reviewing pages to determine whether additional information could be released. Case Name: American Oversight v. U.S. Department Of Government Efficiency(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-01251
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On April 23, 2025, American Oversight, a nonpartisan, non-profit corporation which collects, analyses and disseminates information about the activities and operations of the federal government,filed suitagainst the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE); Elon Musk, Steve Davis, and Amy Gleason in their official capacities as DOGE leaders; the National Archives and Records Administration (NARA); and Marco Rubio in his official capacity as Acting Archivist. Plaintiff argues that DOGE’s use of ephemeral messaging and document systems such as Signal and Google Docs violates the Federal Records Act (FRA) and the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) because such systems do not preserve records as required by the statutes. Plaintiff’s interest in ensuring Defendants’ compliance with their recordkeeping obligations stems from its filing a number of FOIA requests seeking information about DOGE’s activities, none of which have been fulfilled.Plaintiff argues that Musk, Davis, and Gleason violate the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) by acting in an arbitrary and capricious manner, abusing their discretion, and acting contrary to the law. They contend that as agency heads, Defendants are obligated to preserve federal records per the FRA, but their failure to prevent the automatic deletion of records in ephemeral systems is contrary to such obligations. Plaintiff argues that Defendants NARA and Rubio violate the APA, also for arbitrary and capricious action, abuse of discretion, and acting contrary to the law, because NARA and Rubio have an obligation under FRA to initiate an action for recovery of documents through the Attorney General when an agency head fails to do so, and that NARA and Rubio have failed to take such action. Plaintiff further argues that the DOGE Defendants are in violation of FOIA because they failed to conduct adequate searches for records responsive to Plaintiff’s FOIA requests and to provide all responsive non-exempt records. Plaintiff seeks declaratory and injunctive relief.
Case Updates: July 1, 2025:Defendantsmoved to dismissthe action, arguing that DOGE is not an agency under the FRA and that Defendants Musk and Davis should be dismissed regardless of DOGE’s agency status.July 22, 2025:Plaintiffamended its complaintto add additional APA claims and enumerate which of Defendants’ actions applied to each of the claims.Dec. 17, 2025:Judge Contreras denied Defendants’ motion to dismiss as moot due to Plaintiff’s filing of an amended complaint in a minute order.Dec. 18, 2025:In a minute order, Judge Contreras granted Defendants’ motion to stay pending the D.C. Circuit’s decision inIn re: US Doge Services(No. 25-5130).

Case Name: Council for Global Equality v. U.S. Department of State(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-02615
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Aug. 11, 2025, the Council for Global Equality (CGE), a fiscally sponsored project of the Community Initiatives, Inc. not-for-profit organization, filed acomplaintagainst the U.S. Department of State (State Department) seeking to enforce its Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, request and to compel the State Department to release information related to its publication of itsCountry Reports on Human Rights Practices(Human Rights Reports or HRRs). CGE alleges the State Department directed its employees to “streamline” these reports to include only the information legally required, resulting in public concern that the HRRs will omit key facts about the status of human rights in certain countries favored by the administration. As of the date this action was filed, the administration has yet to release the 2025 HRRs, which historically were released annually in the months of March or April. CGE submitted FOIA requests to the State Department seeking records relating to the HRRs and alleges the State Department violated FOIA by failing to respond to its request. CGE requests that the court order the State Department to search for and produce the requested files to CGE.
Case Updates: Dec. 4, 2025:DefendantansweredCGE’s complaint, which included the defenses that it did not violate Plaintiff’s FOIA request and that it had exercised due diligence in processing Plaintiff’s FOIA request.March 5, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that they had agreed on the scope of some FOIA requests and were continuing to confer on the scope of others. Defendant anticipated it would need 60-90 days to complete its search for potentially responsive records to the FOIA requests the parties had agreed on.

Case Name: Democracy Defenders Fund v. U.S. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-02791
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Aug. 21, 2025, the Democracy Defenders Fund (DDF), a democracy-oriented nonprofit organization, filed acomplaintchallenging the Department of Justice’s (DOJ) Criminal Division’s refusal to respond to a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request for records related to the “Epstein Files.” Plaintiff seeks disclosure of all DOJ investigative records concerning Jeffery Epstein that reference Donald Trump or his Mar-a-Lago estate. DDF alleges the DOJ violated FOIA by failing to conduct adequate searches for responsive records in the time stipulated by law, withheld non-exempt responsive records, failed to grant a request for expedited processing and a request for a fee waiver. DDF asks the court to order the DOJ to search for and produce responsive records and enjoin the DOJ from continuing to withhold non-exempt records responsive to their FOIA request.
Case Updates: Oct. 1, 2025:Plaintiff filed anamended complaintto include a second, broader Criminal Division (CRM) request and parallel requests to the Office of the Attorney General (OAG) and Office of the Deputy Attorney General (ODAG). These requests sought (1) communications related to Epstein and Ghislaine Maxwell, and (2) client-list records with detailed allegations about about DOJ’s Office of Information Policy’s (OIP’s) denial of expedition, unusual circumstance findings, complex-track assignment, and the 650-day estimate for OAG/ODAG processing time. The amended complaint correspondingly broadens the factual and legal theory from CRM’s non‑response alone to DOJ‑wide delay and withholding at the senior‑leadership level, while keeping the same four FOIA causes of action and prayer for relief.Dec. 30, 2025:Plaintiff filed athird amended complaint, further transforming the case into a department-wide FOIA challenge, by adding (1) July 22 and 28 requests to the Executive Office of the U.S. Attorneys (EOUSA) and FBI; (2) August 6 requests to OAG/ODAG and BOP regarding Maxwell’s transfer and BOP policy waivers; and (3) detailed non-response/complex-track allegations for CRM, OAG/ODAG, EOUSA, Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), and Bureau of Prisons (BOP). Plaintiff also updated the public interest and non-mootness narrative with recent factual developments, including the House Oversight Committee’s release of Epstein emails, media reports of additional files, and the Epstein Files Transparency Act (EFTA), while keeping the same four FOIA counts but applying them to a larger set of requests.April 15, 2026:Plaintiff submitted anoticeto the court which claims the documents produced by DOJ pursuant to EFTA do not satisfy DOJ’s obligation to respond to Plaintiff’s FOIA requests. Specifically, Plaintiff cited its request for  documents concerning Maxwell’s prison transfer as well as other communications involving Trump administration officials that remain outstanding following Plaintiff’s review of the Epstein Library on the DOJ's webpage.

Case Name: Democracy Forward Foundation v. U.S. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-02597

DFF sent FOIA requests to DOJ, FBI, and multiple Executive Branch offices for any communications between certain DOJ officials, certain FBI officials, and certain White House officials related to the Jeffrey Epstein matter, as well as any communications between President Donald Trump and Jeffrey Epstein. The suit asks the court to order Defendants to grant expedited processing of DFF’s requests and to produce the requested files to DFF.
Case Updates: Aug. 11, 2025:DFF filed amotion for summary judgmenton the enforcement of its FOIA requests.Nov. 24, 2025:Judge Chutkan issued anorderandopiniongranting in part DFF’s motion for summary judgment, ordering DOJ and the FBI to expedite processing of DFF’s July 25 and July 28 FOIA requests, except as to the overbroad search terms “whistleblower” and “flight logs.”

Case Name: The New York Times Company v. United States Defense Counterintelligence and Security Agency(S.D.N.Y.)1:25-cv-02333
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On January 20, 2025, President Trump issued Executive Order No. 14,158, establishing the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) and appointed Elon Musk to lead DOGE. The New York Times filed a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request in September 2024 regarding Elon Musk’s government security clearances to the United States Defense Counterintelligence and Security Agency (DCSA), an agency of the federal government responsible for conducting background investigations and adjudicating security clearances. The New York Times’ FOIA request was denied on October 2, 2024, and the New York Times filed suit against the DSCA on March 3, 2025, alleging that the denial of its FOIA request was unjustifiable under FOIA’s personal privacy exemptions and deprived the New York Times and the public of important information concerning Musk’s security clearances and sought the release of the requested records.
Case Updates: Oct. 8, 2025:Judge Cotegrantedsummary judgment for the plaintiffs, holding that per plaintiffs’ September 2024 FOIA request, the DCSA must produce a single, two-page document listing any security clearances granted to Elon Musk at that time. DCSA had denied plaintiffs’ FOIA request on the ground that Musk’s privacy interest as a private citizen outweighed disclosure. The court held that the DCSA failed to prove disclosure of a list of Musk’s clearances would be a “clearly unwarranted invasion of personal privacy” and Musk’s public comments had reduced his privacy interests. It also held that substantial public interest into the thoroughness, fairness, or accuracy of security clearance determinations outweighed any privacy interests Musk holds.Oct. 20, 2025:Judge Coteacceptedthe DSCA’s proposed redactions to the FOIA disclosure.Nov. 3, 2025:The New York Timesfileda motion for reconsideration of the Judge Cote’s October 20 order, requesting for DSCA to produce both pages of the record, subject only to those redactions justified under privacy exemptions, and not to withhold page one or any investigation-related information in its entirety.Dec. 18, 2025:Judge CotegrantedDCSA’s motion to stay further disclosures pending the court’s resolution of the reconsideration motions.March 2, 2026:Judge Cote issued anopinion and orderresolving cross-motions for partial reconsideration, permitting DCSA to redact two additional entries from the responsive record on national security grounds, and denied the New York Times's motion for partial reconsideration.

Case Name: American Oversight v. U.S. Deptartment of Justice(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03414
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On March 15, 2025, President Donald Trump issuedProclamation 10903, entitled “Invocation of the Alien Enemies Act Regarding the Invasion of the United States byTren de Aragua,” which invoked the Alien Enemies Act to order the removal of all Venezuelan citizens not lawfully in the United States who are members of the Venezuelan gangTren de Aragua. On the same day, Venezuelans held by the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) were loaded onto planes and, despite acourt orderfrom Judge James Boasberg of the District Court for the District of Columbia ordering the government not to relinquish custody, a group of these detainees were flown to El Salvador and transferred to a Salvadoran prison. On June 24, 2025, a whistleblower complaint was filed which asserted that Emil Bove, who was then Principal Associate Deputy Attorney General, had told attorneys in the DOJ’s Office of Immigration Litigation (OIL) on March 14, 2025, that planes would be taking off over the weekend “no matter what” and that they should consider ignoring any court orders enjoining removals.Plaintiff American Oversight, a non-profit organization that promotes government transparency, submitted a request on July 15, 2025, under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), to the DOJ seeking all photographs, video recordings and/or audio recordings of the removals on March 15 and 16. Plaintifffiled suiton Sept. 25, 2025, asserting that they had not been given a final determination of their request and that no records had been produced. They allege a violation of FOIA, 5 U.S.C. § 552 and ask the court to compel Defendants to process these FOIA requests on an expedited basis and conduct a reasonable search for responsive records.
Case Updates: Jan. 20, 2026:DefendantansweredAmerican Oversight’s complaint, which included the defenses that it had exercised due diligence in processing Plaintiff’s FOIA request and that its actions did not violate FOIA or other statutory provisions.

Case Name: American Oversight v. U.S. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03200
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Plaintiff American Oversight alleges that, on July 7, 2025, the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) and Department of Justice (DOJ) released a memo describing those agencies’ review of records concerning the investigation of Jeffrey Epstein and stating that the review found no client list, no credible evidence that Epstein had blackmailed prominent individuals, and no evidence to support investigation of third parties. Plaintiff alleges that memo has been criticized and that it contradicts prior statements by the administration. On July 29, 2025, Plaintiff sent Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests to the FBI and to the DOJ’s Office of Information Policy (OIP) and Office of Legal Counsel (OLC) for production of records concerning the training provided to personnel who conducted the review, the roles of Attorney General Bondi and FBI Director Kash Patel in shaping the review, and whether any personnel expressed concerns or dissent about the conduct of the review. Plaintiff alleges that the FBI and DOJ OIP responded that their responses would be delayed and that the DOJ OLC acknowledged receipt and assigned processing numbers, but that the agencies have provided no further information in response to the requests. Plaintifffiled suiton Sept. 15, 2025, seeking an order compelling the agencies to search for all potentially relevant information, produce all non-exempt information to American Oversight, and enjoin the agencies from continuing to withhold all non-exempt records pertinent to the FOIA requests.
Case Updates: Feb. 2, 2026:DefendantsansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defense that Plaintiff is not entitled to compel the production of any record or portion of any record protected from disclosure under FOIA.Feb. 17, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that Defendants’ searches for potentially responsive records remain ongoing.March 16, 2026:Plaintiff filed amotion for a preliminary injunction, requesting that the court order Defendants to produce documents responsive to its FOIA requests by March 31, 2026, or at least ten days ahead of Attorney General Pam Bondi’s upcoming deposition by the United States House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.April 3, 2026:Judge BoasbergdeniedPlaintiff’s motion for a preliminary injunction compelling DOJ to produce records responsive to its FOIA requests before the April 14, 2026 deposition of former Attorney General Pam Bondi,writingthat “Plaintiff’s requested documents are already set to arrive in a timely fashion for the public conversation surrounding these controversies. The Court therefore sees no need to make use of an extraordinary and rarely granted equitable remedy.”

Case Name: American Oversight v. Federal Bureau of Investigation(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03597
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Plaintiff American Oversight alleges that, on July 8, 2020, it filed a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, request with the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) for production of FBI records relating to whether President Donald Trump was interviewed by the FBI or gave a statement or statements to the FBI between 2006 and 2008 concerning the FBI’s investigation of Jeffrey Epstein. Plaintiff further alleges that the FBI refused to confirm or deny the existence of any such records and refused to produce any such records, and that the denial was subsequently upheld on administrative appeal. Plaintiff argues that the FBI’s refusal to search for or produce records violates FOIA and that the second Trump administration’s allegedly ambiguous and contradictory statements and actions concerning the Epstein case and files warrant disclosure of the requested FBI records. Plaintifffiled suiton Oct. 7, 2025, seeking an order compelling the FBI to search for and produce non-exempt records responsive to American Oversight’s FOIA request.
Case Updates: Feb. 19, 2026:DefendantsansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defense that Plaintiff is not entitled to compel the production of any record or portion of any record protected from disclosure under FOIA.March 13, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that Defendants had not identified records responsive to Plaintiff’s FOIA request and that Plaintiff was still evaluating the FBI’s response to determine whether additional briefing would be necessary.

Case Name: Citizens for Responsibility & Ethics in Washington v. U.S. Department of Homeland Security(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03670
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) has stated that it “no longer maintain[s]” text messages generated after April 9, 2025 and has also stated that it no longer has the capability to search text messages in response to Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests. Citizens for Responsibility & Ethics (CREW), a non-profit government watchdog, sent a FOIA request to DHS on Sept. 12, 2025, requesting records relating to a proposed centralized citizenship data bank, and has yet to receive a response and has submitted other requests in recent months for which no text message records were produced. CREWfiled suitagainst the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), the National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), and their respective heads and argues that in failing to preserve or search agency text messages, the Defendants violate the Federal Records Act (FRA), which requires agencies to “make and preserve records containing adequate and proper documentation of the organization” and FOIA, which requires agencies to produce records requested by the public.CREW makes Administrative Procedure Act (APA) claims, arguing that Defendants’ policy is arbitrary and capricious and their violation of the FRA is agency action contrary to law and agency action unlawfully withheld. Plaintiff argues an entitlement to a writ of mandamus in the alternative. Plaintiff also makes FOIA claims, arguing that Defendants have a “policy or practice” of violating FOIA, have wrongfully denied CREW’s request for expedited processing, and have wrongfully withheld records. CREW seeks declaratory and injunctive relief to compel preservation of electronic messaging data, require NARA and DHS to initiate enforcement through the Attorney General, and order expedited FOIA processing of CREW’s pending requests.
Case Updates: Jan. 25, 2026:Plaintiffvoluntarily dismissedCounts 1-5 of its complaint, which included its APA and mandamus claims, as well as the FOIA claim that asserted Defendants have a “policy or practice” of violating FOIA. Since these dismissals effectuated the dismissal of all claims against Defendants National Archives and Record Administration and Marco Rubio, Plaintiff requested they be terminated as parties in the case as well. The two remaining FOIA claims concern the wrongful denial of expedited processing and the wrongful withholding of records.Feb. 2, 2026:Defendant DHSansweredPlaintiff’s request and included the defenses that it has not improperly withheld records requested by Plaintiff under FOIA and exercised due diligence in processing Plaintiff’s FOIA request.

Case Name: LatinoJustice PRLDEF and American Immigration Council v. U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement(S.D.N.Y.)1:25-cv-08516

Case Summary: On Oct. 15, 2025, LatinoJustice PRLDEF and the American Immigration Council filedsuitagainst ICE, the U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS), the U.S. Department of Justice (DOJ), and the Executive Office for Immigration Review (EOIR) to compel disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) of internal records concerning a new enforcement policy implemented by ICE on Jan. 21, 2025 that allowed civil immigration enforcement actions in or near courthouses, and by EOIR on Jan. 28 that allowed civil immigration enforcement in or near EOIR spaces. In May 2025, ICE authorized and implemented widespread arrests of noncitizens appearing for hearings in immigration courts with expedited removal proceedings for noncitizens with dismissed cases. Plaintiffs seek expedited processing of their 11 FOIA requests for agency communications, memos, and policy guidance relating to immigration court arrests and dismissals, court orders for reasonable searches, review of withholding claims.
Case Updates: Feb. 13, 2026:Plaintiffs filed asupplemental complaintadding claims arising from three FOIA requests related to and filed at the same time as the requests at issue in the initial complaint which were not yet ripe at the time the initial complaint was filed.

Case Name: Interfaith Alliance Foundation v. U.S. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03746

Case Summary: On Oct. 22, 2025, Interfaith Alliance Foundation (IAF), a non-profit organization focused on the separation of church and state, filedsuitagainst the Department of Justice (DOJ), Department of State, and the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) to compel responses to FOIA requests concerning President Donald Trump’sExecutive Order 14202, “Eradicating Anti-Christian Bias,” which created a Task Force to Eradicate Anti-Christian Bias. IAF seeks records on Task Force meetings, communications with external advocacy groups, communications between the Task Force and DOJ, and other related Task Force reports and guidance.

Case Name: Democracy Forward Foundation v. Federal Housing Finance Agency(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03700

Pulte’s criminal referrals to the Department of Justice for public officials over possible mortgage fraud. Plaintiff subsequently submitted a number of other FOIA requests seeking additional information on the same topic. Plaintifffiled suitagainst FHFA arguing that its failure to promptly produce documents constitutes a FOIA violation. Plaintiff seeks injunctive relief compelling Defendant to search for and produce responsive records.
Case Updates: Jan. 6, 2026:DefendantansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defenses that it had not improperly withheld records requested by Plaintiff under FOIA and exercised due diligence in responding to Plaintiff’s FOIA requests.Feb. 6, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that Defendant continues to search for and process documents responsive to Plaintiff’s FOIA requests.

Case Name: Democracy Forward Foundation v. U.S. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03755
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Plaintiff Democracy Forward Foundation (a nonprofit organization promoting transparency and accountability in government) sent three Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests to the Department of Justice’s (DOJ) Office of Justice Programs (OJP) and Office of Information Policy (OIP) seeking documents about the April 2025 terminations of more than 250 grants that funded violence reduction, victims’ services, juvenile justice, mental health, and reentry programs nationwide. Democracy Forward filed acomplaintagainst the DOJ and FBI challenging their failure to disclose these records concerning the Trump Administration’s termination of more than $800 million in grants.  The complaint alleges that the DOJ and FBI unlawfully withheld agency records in violation of 5 U.S.C. § 552, both by failing to make timely determinations and by invoking improper privacy exemptions to withhold communications of Tarak Makecha, a DOGE staffer reportedly involved in drafting a spreadsheet of 365 OJP grants targeted for cancellation. Democracy Forward argues that the records are of high public interest, especially because of Makecha’s dual placement at the DOJ and FBI. The suit asks the court to order the DOJ and FBI to conduct the relevant record searches, release all non-exempt records, and end their unlawful withholding of information related to the OJP grant terminations.
Case Updates: Feb. 5, 2026:Judge Nicholsdenieda motion by the administration to strike the complaint.

Plaintiff hasfiled suitarguing that agencies have violated FOIA because of their failure to timely respond and failure to unconditionally grant Plaintiff’s fee waiver. Plaintiff seeks declaratory and injunctive relief.
Case Updates: March 16, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that Defendants OMB and OIP had completed initial searches of documents and were actively reviewing and processing records to be produced to Plaintiff. Case Name: American Oversight v. U.S. Department of Homeland Security(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03812

Plaintiff alleges violations of FOIA, and seek an order compelling the agency to search for all potentially relevant information, produce all non-exempt information to American Oversight, and enjoin the agency from continuing to withhold all non-exempt records pertinent to the FOIA requests.

Case Name: Campaign Legal Center v. Social Security Administration(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03830
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On March 25, 2025, President Donald Trump issuedExecutive Order (EO) 14248, titled “Preserving and Protecting the Integrity of American Elections,” which directed the Social Security Administration (SSA) and the U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) to work in connection with the U.S. Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) and other federal agencies to use state voter rolls to search for potential, alleged illegal voting by non-U.S. citizens and create a federal database of voter information. On Oct. 31, 2025, nonprofits Campaign Legal Center and American Oversight filed suit against the SSA, challenging the agency’s failure to comply with Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests. filed by the Campaign Legal Center and American Oversight, seeking information related to the cross-agency effort to create a federal database of voter information. Plaintiffs assert that the agency has failed to conduct adequate searches for responsive records and has wrongfully withheld non-exempt agency records in violation of FOIA. Plaintiffs seek a court order compelling the SSA to conduct thorough searches for all responsive records and produce all non-exempt information, as well as an injunction preventing further withholding of records relevant to the FOIA requests.
Case Updates: March 3, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that Plaintiffs’ fee waiver request was denied and that Defendant SSA is unable to conduct a search for responsive records until it calculates the fee to process Plaintiffs’ request.

Case Name: Campaign Legal Center v. United States Citizenship and Immigration Services(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03829
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On March 25, 2025, President Donald Trump issuedExecutive Order (EO) 14248, titled “Preserving and Protecting the Integrity of American Elections,” which directed the Social Security Administration (SSA) and the U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) to work in connection with the U.S. Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) and other federal agencies to use state voter rolls to search for potential, alleged illegal voting by non-U.S. citizens and create a federal database of voter information. On Oct. 31, 2025, nonprofits Campaign Legal Center and American Oversight filed a lawsuit against U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS), challenging USCIS’s failure to respond adequately to Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests seeking records related to the implementation of EO 14248. Plaintiffs allege that USCIS has not complied with FOIA requirements, by failing to conduct an adequate search and unlawfully withholding non-exempt records related to the expanded federal database used for verifying voter eligibility. Plaintiffs seek a court order compelling USCIS to conduct reasonable searches, produce all responsive non-exempt records, and prevent further wrongful withholding of documents.
Case Updates: Dec. 3, 2025:DefendantansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defenses that it exercised due diligence in processing Plaintiffs’ FOIA requests and did not improperly withhold records requested by Plaintiff under FOIA.March 5, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that Defendant continues to process documents to be produced to Plaintiffs in response to their FOIA requests.

Case Name: Campaign Legal Center v. United States Department of Homeland Security(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03828
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On March 25, 2025, President Donald Trump issuedExecutive Order (EO) 14248, “Preserving and Protecting the Integrity of American Elections,” which directs the U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and other federal agencies to collaborate with states by cross-referencing voter rolls with federal immigration databases to verify citizenship status as part of an expanded voter maintenance database. On Oct. 31, 2025, nonprofits Campaign Legal Center and American Oversightfiled suitchallenging DHS’s failure to respond adequately to Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests seeking records related to implementation of the EO. Plaintiffs allege DHS has failed to conduct adequate searches for responsive documents and is unlawfully withholding non-exempt records related to implementation of the EO. Plaintiffs request the court compel DHS to produce responsive documents and stop unlawful withholding of documents.

Case Name: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington v. United States Department of Homeland Security(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03703
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Plaintiff Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW), a not-for-profit organization that promotes transparency in government, submitted expedited requests in April and August of 2025 to the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and its subordinate agency, Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, seeking records concerning the Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements (SAVE) program, a system designed to assist federal and local agencies to verify the citizenship or immigration status of noncitizens and naturalized citizens prior to granting benefits and licenses to them. CREW sought data and other records to shed light on agreements between DHS and states which permit states to access and use SAVE data to verify voting eligibility and, potentially, to purge voting rolls; the scope of access SAVE provides states to Social Security data and other personally identifiable information; and information about privacy or data security safeguards in place to protect this information. Plaintiff CREW filed acomplainton Oct. 20, 2025, alleging that Defendants failed to provide expedited processing or to produce the requested records as required by FOIA, and asking the court to compel DHS to process these FOIA requests on an expedited basis and conduct a reasonable search for responsive records.
Case Updates: Dec. 19, 2025:DefendantsansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and contended that Plaintiff’s FOIA requests are improper because they failed to reasonably describe the records soughtand/or would be unduly burdensome to process and that the Complaint should be dismissed to the extent Plaintiff has failed to exhaust administrative remedies.Jan. 16, 2026:Defendants filed astatus reportwhich indicates that Defendant USCIS had sent Plaintiff records responsive to its April 9, 2025 FOIA request on Jan. 14, 2026, and that DHS had referred Plaintiff’s Sept. 3, 2025 FOIA request to USCIS, who closed the request as redundant to the records it produced on Jan. 14, 2026. DHS also contended that Plaintiff has failed to exhaust administrative remedies with respect to its request dated April 9, 2025.

Case Name: American Oversight v. U.S. Department of Homeland Security(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03699
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) allegedly adopted two new policies and practices regarding text message data: (1) that text message data generated after April 9, 2025, is no longer maintained, and (2) that DHS no longer has the capability to conduct a search of text messages. The National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) has not yet approved these policies and practices. Between May and September 2025, American Oversight, a not-for-profit organization that promotes government transparency, allegedly submitted several Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests to DHS seeking text message data related to official government business, including requests for Secretary Kristi Noem’s text message data. DHS allegedly failed to respond to some of these FOIA requests and notified American Oversight that it no longer had the capability to search for text message data in response to other FOIA requests.In response, American Oversight sued DHS, Secretary Kristi Noem, NARA, and Acting Archivist Marco Rubio. Itscomplaintasserts that DHS’s April 2025 policy was not approved by NARA; the new policies violate the Federal Records Act (FRA), 44 U.S.C. § 3101, which requires agencies to preserve records relating to official business; and that DHS failed to conduct adequate searches for responsive text message and related records in violation of FOIA. The complaint further alleges that DHS’s failure to preserve, search for, and/or recover these records constitutes arbitrary and capricious agency action under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), 5 U.S.C. § 706, and that both DHS and NARA failed to fulfill their nondiscretionary statutory duties by not reporting, addressing, or seeking recovery of unlawfully destroyed records. Plaintiff requests the court declare that text messages and text message data regarding official government business are federal records subject to the FRA and agency records subject to FOIA; the failure of Defendants Noem and DHS to preserve text message data violated the FRA; DHS’s policy of failing to maintain text messages and data violates the APA; and Defendants violated their duties under the FRA and APA. Plaintiff also seeks a court order to compel Defendants to implement recordkeeping policies compliant with federal law, recover lost records, conduct reasonable searches for text messages responsive to FOIA requests, and prevent further unlawful destruction or withholding of agency records.
Case Updates: Dec. 10, 2025:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that Defendants provided Plaintiff with declarations addressing allegations raised by Plaintiff in its Oct. 20, 2025motion for a preliminary injunctionand that Plaintiff wished to withdraw this motion as a result. The court ordered the motion withdrawn on Dec. 12, 2025.Jan. 9, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportin which they stipulated the dismissal of three counts in Plaintiff’s complaint: that Defendants’ failed to fulfill their nondiscretionary statutory duties by not reporting, addressing, or seeking recovery of unlawfully destroyed records (Counts II and III), and DHS’s policy of not searching text messages is an unlawful under FOIA.March 6, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that DHS made an initial production of documents to Plaintiff on Feb. 13, 2026, but that DHS has had to pause its review of additional documents due to the lapse in appropriations that began on Feb. 13, 2026. DHS noted it will resume its review once the lapse in appropriations ends.

Case Name: American Oversight v. U.S. Department of Homeland Security(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03788
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: American Oversight, a non-partisan, nonprofit organization that aims to promote government transparency and accountability,suedthe U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) after these Defendants allegedly failed to search for and produce records in response to Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests submitted by American Oversight between May and August 2025. The complaint alleges these FOIA requests were submitted to Defendants to obtain information about actions taken by the administration, including the use of Signal to communicate highly-sensitive information regarding military strikes in Yemen; the deployment of the National Guard in Los Angeles; the June 14, 2025 military parade held in Washington D.C. at President Donald Trump’s direction; third-country removals of deportees to South Sudan; and the upheaval of government operation by the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE). American Oversight seeks declaratory and injunctive relief from the court, including an order that Defendants conduct adequate searches, produce all non-exempt responsive records, and provide indexes for any withheld records.

Case Name: Just Futures Law v. United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement(S.D.N.Y.)1:25-cv-08995
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Oct. 30, 2025, two non-profit organizationssuedthe U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) agencies after these Defendants allegedly withheld records in response to Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests submitted by Plaintiffs on July 16, 2025 concerning the agencies’ contracts with Israeli technology companies Cellebrite and Paragon. The complaint alleges that Defendants entered into contracts with these two companies to purchase surveillance technology, but that little is publicly known about how these technologies—which allegedly include phone hacking and zero-click spyware–are used on persons in the U.S. and abroad. Plaintiffs claim the receipt of these contracts and other related documents are crucial to promote public understanding of the technologies and their potential impact on civil and human rights and seek a court order that forces Defendants to promptly process the FOIA requests, release the documents, and address alleged denials of expedited processing and fee waivers.

Case Name: Reproductive Freedom for All v. U.S. Food and Drug Administration(D. Md.)8:25-cv-03565
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On or around May 2025, the Commissioner of the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) allegedly announced the agency would investigate mifepristone, an abortion medication. In June 2023, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) allegedly rescinded guidance related to emergency abortion care under the Emergency Medicine Treatment and Active Labor Act (EMTALA). On Aug. 4, 2025, Reproductive Freedom for All (RFA), a not-for-profit organization that works to protect and advance reproductive freedom at the federal and state levels, allegedly submitted Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests to FDA and CMS requesting records related to these actions. On Oct. 30, 2025, RFAsuedthe FDA and CMS after both allegedly failed to respond to RFA’s FOIA requests. RFA’s complaint alleges that the agencies have failed to respond to the FOIA requests within the statutory time period and wrongfully withheld records in violation of FOIA. RFA seeks an order compelling the agencies to produce all non-exempt records responsive to its FOIA requests and enjoining the agencies from continuing to withhold any and all non-exempt responsive records.

Case Name: Freedom of the Press Foundation v. Immigration and Customs Enforcement(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03883
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On June 16, 2025, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) issued updated guidance regarding congressional visitation to Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) facilities, contending that ICE facilities were exempt from statutory requirements to permit unannounced oversight visits by members of Congress. On June 19, 2025, Plaintiff Freedom of Press Foundation (FPF) submitted a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, request for communications between Congress and ICE regarding such visitations. ICE allegedly denied FPF’s request for expedited processing of the records. In June and July 2025, several members of Congress were turned away from ICE facilities, and as of Nov. 6, 2025, ICE has allegedly failed to produce responsive records to FPF’s FOIA request. FPFsuedICE on Nov. 6, 2025, alleging that the agency failed to disclose records within the statutory limit and unlawfully withheld records in violation of FOIA. FPF seeks an order compelling ICE to immediately disclose the requested information and a declaration that the agency wrongfully withheld the requested records.

Case Name: American Civil Liberties Union v. United States Food and Drug Administration(D. Md.)8:25-cv-03736
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On May 14, 2025, Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. (RFK) announced that the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) would be conducting a “complete review” of its mifepristone regulations. This review, driven by significant political pressure from anti-abortion organizations, allegedly relies on a controversial, scientifically questionable paper, despite mifepristone's well-established safety record since its approval in 2000. On Aug. 1, 2025, Plaintiff American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) broughtsuitagainst Defendant FDA pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552. Despite a formal FOIA request and expedited processing plea, the complaint alleges that the FDA has not provided any determination or produced documents, exceeding statutory deadlines. Plaintiff argues that the agency’s conduct in response to the FOIA request violates FOIA, and requests that the court order Defendant to produce all requested records.
Case Updates: Mar. 2, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that the parties are working together to reach a resolution and that Defendant intends to produce records.

Case Name: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington v. U.S. Department Of Justice(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03983
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Aug. 11, 2025, President Donald J. Trump issuedExecutive Order (EO) 14333entitled“Declaring a Crime Emergency in the District of Columbia”, which purported to place Washington, D.C.’s Metropolitan Police Department (MPD) under the direct operational control of the Attorney General. On Aug. 25, Trump issuedEO 14339, entitled “Additional Measures To Address the Crime Emergency in the District of Columbia”, which authorized the large-scale deployment of federal law enforcement officers and National Guard units to engage in law enforcement activities within the nation’s capital. On Aug. 20 and Aug. 27, Plaintiff Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW), a not-for-profit organization that promotes transparency in government, submitted twelve expedited requests under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, to the federal agencies deployed in Washington, D.C. seeking records concerning the federal deployments. Plaintiff filed a complaint on Nov.17 alleging that Defendants failed to provide expedited processing or to produce the requested records as required by FOIA and asking the court to compel Defendants to process these FOIA requests on an expedited basis and conduct a reasonable search for responsive records.
Case Updates: Feb. 11, 2026:Judge McFaddendismissedCount I with respect to items 2 through 6 of Plaintiffs’ FOIA request in response to Plaintiffs’ notice of voluntary dismissal, and denied as moot the government’s motion to dismiss those counts.

Case Name: Khalil v. United States Department of Justice(S.D.N.Y.)1:25-cv-09674
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Nov. 20, 2025, Plaintiffs Mahmoud Khalil and the Center for Constitutional Rights (CCR), an non-profit, public interest legal and advocacy organization that engages in the fields of civil and international human rights, filed acomplaintagainst several U.S. government agencies, including the Department of Justice (DOJ), Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), and the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) (together, the Defendant Agencies). The complaint alleges violations of the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, and the Privacy Act of 1974 (PA), 5 U.S.C. § 552a, for the Defendant Agencies’ alleged improper withholding of requested information related to their unlawful targeting, arrest, and attempt to deport Mr. Khalil, a Palestinian activist and lawful permanent resident, in retaliation to his pro-Palestinian advocacy. Plaintiffs’ FOIA/PA requests also allegedly sought records related private, anti-Palestinian organizations who purportedly collaborated with federal officials with respect to the targeting of Mr. Khalil. Plaintiffs ask the court to order Defendants to expeditiously search for and produce all records responsive to their request.

Case Name: Democracy Forward Foundation v. Federal Communications Commission(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04061
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Nov. 20, 2025, Democracy Forward, a not-for-profit organization that promotes transparency in government, filed acomplaintalleging that the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) had failed to respond to requests they had submitted on Sept. 18 2025 under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, seeking records with the potential to shed light on the “actions and priorities of FCC leadership.” The complaint alleges that the FOIA request was made in light of the agency’s alleged recent use of its authority to restrain protected speech and expression, noting in particular that Brendan Carr, the chairman of the FCC, publicly threatened FCC action against ABC if the network did not sanction Jimmy Kimmel for statements on his late-night show following the murder of Charlie Kirk. The complaint also alleges that Carr re-posted a message from President Donald Trump calling for the firing of a television host due to perceived criticism of the administration. Plaintiff asks the court to compel Defendant to process these FOIA requests on a timely basis and conduct a reasonable search for responsive records.
Case Updates: Jan. 29, 2026:DefendantansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defenses that they acted in good faith in responding to Plaintiff’s FOIA request and that Plaintiff’s request fails to comply with the requirements of FOIA to the extent that it fails to reasonably describe the records sought or presents an unreasonably burdensome effort upon Defendant.Feb. 27, 2026:The parties filed a joint status report which indicates that Defendant had identified 6,700 potentially responsive records, which it will begin to process and product, and that the parties continue to confer over the scope of other subparts of the request.

Case Name: Democracy Forward Foundation v. U.S. Department of Homeland Security(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04089
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: In October 2025, Plaintiff Democracy Forward, a not-for-profit organization that promotes transparency in government, submitted requests to the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and its subordinate agency, Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, seeking records concerning ICE’s alleged raid on a Chicago apartment building on Sept. 30. The FOIA requests seek “...all raw video and/or audio footage in the possession of DHS and/or ICE of the South Shore Chicago apartment complex raid” as well as records pertaining to ICE training materials. Plaintiff filed acomplainton Nov. 21, 2025, alleging that Defendants failed to provide expedited processing or to produce the requested records as required by FOIA, and asking the court to compel Defendants to process these FOIA requests on an expedited basis and conduct a reasonable search for responsive records.
Case Updates: Jan. 21, 2026:DefendantsansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defenses that they have not improperly withheld records requested by Plaintiff under FOIA, their actions or inactions did not violate FOIA or other statutory provisions, and they exercised due diligence in processing Plaintiff’s FOIA requests.March 17, 2026:In a minute order, Judge Ali ordered that Defendant ICE process and produce non-exempt portions of identified potentially responsive records to Plaintiff’s ICE Training Materials FOIA request by April 30, 2026.

Case Name: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington v. Federal Election Commission(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04116
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Plaintiff Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW), a not-for-profit organization that promotes transparency in government, submitted several expedited requests to the Federal Election Commission (FEC) under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, in June and August 2025 seeking records concerning the FEC’s alleged failure to disclose information about its new procedures for informing the public about its closure of enforcement cases, as well as records concerning former FEC Commissioner Trey Trainor’s failure to recuse from a case. CREW filed acomplainton Nov. 11, 2025, alleging that Defendants failed to provide expedited processing or to produce the requested records as required by FOIA, and asking the court to compel the FEC to process these FOIA requests on an expedited basis and conduct a reasonable search for responsive records.
Case Updates: Dec. 26, 2025:DefendantansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defense that some of the information Plaintiff seeks may be exempt from release under FOIA.March 2, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that the FEC had completed its searches for and production of responsive records and that CREW is reviewing the production and the FEC’s withholdings.

Case Name: American Oversight v. U.S. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04208
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: American Oversight, a not-for-profit organization that seeks to promote government transparency, filed acomplainton Dec. 3, 2025 alleging that the Department of Justice (DOJ) had failed to comply with requests submitted by Plaintiff on Nov. 20, 2025 under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, seeking records with the potential to shed light on federal government activity related to the tenure of Ed Martin, the former interim U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia, at DOJ, including in his roles as Director of the Weaponization Working Group (WWG) and as Pardon Attorney. Plaintiff alleges that it filed the requests following public reports that Martin was unlawfully destroying official communications relating to the WWG. Citing an urgent public interest and questions about government integrity, American Oversight requested expedited processing for all requests. The complaint alleges that the DOJ failed to grant or even issue a timely determination on the requests for expedition, in violation of FOIA. Plaintiff asks the court to compel Defendants to process these FOIA requests on an expedited basis and conduct a reasonable search for responsive records.
Case Updates: Jan. 28, 2026:The DOJansweredAmerican Oversight’s complaint, asserting that it has not violated FOIA. While the DOJ admits it has not provided the records or fully responded to the requests for expedition, it systematically denies all of Plaintiff's legal conclusions and characterizations of events, including that Martin acted unlawfully by concealing or destroying records.Feb. 10, 2026:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaint, which notes that the National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) opened an investigation into the alleged destruction of records by Martin. The amended complaint also adds two new causes of action, alleging the DOJ violated FOIA by failing to conduct adequate searches for responsive records and wrongfully withheld non-exempt responsive records from American Oversight. American Oversight added the requests that the court order the DOJ to conduct adequate searches, produce all non-exempt records within 20 days, and be enjoined from continuing to withhold records.

Case Name: American Oversight v. U.S. Department of Defense(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04220
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: American Oversight, a not-for-profit organization that seeks to promote government transparency, filed acomplainton Dec. 4, 2025 alleging that the Department of Defense (DOD) and Department of Justice (DOJ) had failed to comply with requests submitted by Plaintiff on Sept. 29, 2025 under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, seeking records that could shed light on the legal justifications for the September 2 and September 15 military strikes on alleged Venezuelan drug trafficking boats, as well as communications regarding the government’s public-facing statements about those legal justifications to the media or other public officials. Plaintiff asks the court to compel Defendants to process these FOIA requests on an expedited basis and conduct a reasonable search for responsive records.
Case Updates: Jan. 8, 2026:DefendantsansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defenses that their actions or inactions did not violate FOIA or other statutory provisions and that Plaintiff is not entitled to information that is exempt from disclosure under FOIA.March 5, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that Defendants are in the process of conducting searches and reviewing documents that may be responsive to Plaintiff’s FOIA request.

Case Name: American Oversight v. U.S. Department of Commerce(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04302
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: During the course of 2025, nine law firms agreed to provide a total of nearly $1 billion in free legal services to the administration in exchange for the administration’s agreement not to investigate them or seek to ban their lawyers from accessing federal buildings. American Oversight, a not-for-profit organization that seeks to promote government transparency, filed acomplainton Dec. 11, 2025 alleging that the Department of Commerce and Department of Justice (DOJ) had failed to comply with requests submitted by Plaintiff on Oct. 10, 2025 under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, seeking records that could shed light on these agreements, as well as details about the work these law firms are doing for the administration. Plaintiff asks the court to compel Defendants to process these FOIA requests and conduct a reasonable search for responsive records.
Case Updates: Feb. 25, 2026:DefendantsansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defenses that Plaintiff is not entitled to compel the production of any records that are exempt from disclosure under FOIA and Plaintiff’s FOIA requests are improper to the extent that they are overbroad, cannot be processed without undue burden, or fail to reasonably describe the records sought under FOIA.

Case Name: National Treasury Employees Union v. Office of Personnel Management(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03948
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Aug. 20, 2025, the National Treasury Employees Union (NTEU) submitted a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, request to the Office of Personnel Management (OPM) seeking petitions that agencies have submitted to OPM as required by Executive Order (EO)14171. This EO reinstated EO13957from the first Trump administration, which directed heads of executive agencies to petition the Director of OPM to place certain positions within a new excepted service category. EO 13957 was withdrawn during the Biden Administration. On Nov. 14, 2025, NTEUsuedOPM under FOIA, claiming that the administration failed to respond to records requests identifying which positions agencies seek to reclassify as Schedule Policy/Career in order toreduce their removal protections. NTEU seeks a declaration that OPM’s failure to release the records is unlawful and an order to make the requested records available to NTEU within ten calendar days.
Case Updates: Dec. 15, 2025:DefendantansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defenses that Plaintiff is not entitled to compel the production of any records that are exempt from disclosure under FOIA and Defendants actions or inactions did not violate FOIA or other statutory provisions.

Case Name: Protect Democracy Project v. U.S. Department of the Treasury(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04101
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Protect Democracy Project (Protect Democracy), a nonpartisan nonprofit, filed a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552,suitagainst the Departments of Treasury, State, and Homeland Security (DHS), as well as FinCEN, seeking records about how the administration is implementing recent executive actions that purport to target “Antifa” but are allegedly being used to investigate and compile lists of nonprofit and philanthropic organizations viewed as political opponents. The complaint explains that after President DonaldTrump issued anexecutive order designating “Antifa” as a domestic terrorist organizationandNational Security Presidential Memorandum‑7directing multi‑agency investigations into supposed networks and funders of political violence, Protect Democracy submitted expedited FOIA requests on Oct. 22, 2025, asking for guidance, tasking, lists of targeted nonprofits, and communications with banks and third parties concerning suspected “Antifa”‑related activity. The suit alleges that Treasury, State, DHS, and FinCEN either failed to acknowledge the FOIA requests, provided only a boilerplate shutdown message, or otherwise ignored statutory deadlines for making determinations and producing records, thereby constructively denying access. Invoking FOIA, Protect Democracy claims the agencies have not conducted adequate searches, are unlawfully withholding non‑exempt records, and have improperly failed to grant expedited processing. Protect Democracy seeks orders requiring prompt and comprehensive searches, release of all non‑exempt documents, expedited handling of the requests, and an award of fees and costs.
Case Updates: Feb. 20, 2026:Plaintiffs filed anamended complaintalleging they have exhausted administrative remedies under FOIA and are now entitled to an injunction.

Case Name: American Civil Liberties Union v. Department of Justice(S.D.N.Y.)1:25-cv-10189
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Since Sept. 2, 2025, President Donald Trump has allegedly ordered at least 22 lethal strikes against civilians in boats that the administration asserts were carrying drugs, killing roughly 87 people, pursuant to a legal opinion authored by the Department of Justice’s (DOJ’s) Office of Legal Counsel (OLC) which purportedly authorizes these acts and immunizes officials and services members involved from future prosecution. Such actions have garnered widespread bipartisan and expert criticism that the strikes violate domestic and international law. On Oct. 15, 2025, the American Civil Liberties Union and the American Civil Liberties Union Foundation (collectively, ACLU), and the Center for Constitutional Rights (CCR) filed identical Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests to the DOJ, Department of State (DoS), and Department of Defense (DoD). These requests sought the immediate release of the OLC opinion, unclassified summaries, and the presidential directive authorizing force. When the agencies failed to respond to these FOIA requests by the deadline, ACLU and CCR filed thislawsuiton Dec. 9, 2025, alleging FOIA violations for failing to make reasonable efforts to search for and release the requested records. Plaintiffs request that the court order immediate and comprehensive searches of documents, prompt production of all non‑exempt documents, and an award of costs and attorneys’ fees to the advocacy groups.
Case Updates: Feb. 26, 2026:Plaintiffsstipulatedto the dismissal of the claims brought in this action against DoS. Plaintiffs’ claims against DOJ and DoD remain unchanged.Feb. 27, 2026:The court granted Plaintiffs’ stipulation of dismissal of the claims against DoS.

Case Name: American Oversight v. U.S. Department Of State(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-03669
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Plaintiff American Oversight, a not-for-profit organization which collects, analyses, and disseminates information about the activities and operations of the federal government, submitted ten Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests to the U.S. Department of State (DOS), Department of Defense (DOD), and several other federal agencies concerning records related to the agencies’ and officials’ use of the messaging app Signal. Plaintiff alleges that these agencies and officials of the administration widely use SIgnal to conduct government functions. Plaintifffiledthis lawsuit after receiving limited or no communication in response to the requests and receiving no documents to date in response to these requests. Plaintiff argues that the agencies’ failure and/or refusal to search for and produce non-exempt records in response to the FOIA requests violates FOIA. As relief, plaintiff primarily asks the court to order defendants to search for and produce all non-exempt records responsive to these requests.
Case Updates: Dec. 19, 2025:DefendantsansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defenses that they have not improperly withheld records requested by Plaintiff under FOIA and that Plaintiffs’ FOIA requests are not valid to the extent that they do not reasonably describe the records sought or would be unduly burdensome to process.March 13, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that Defendants’ searches for and review of potentially responsive documents remains ongoing.

Case Name: American Oversight v. Federal Bureau of Investigation(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00020
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Jan. 5, 2026, American Oversight, a nonprofit organization,suedthe Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) and the Department of Justice (DOJ) for allegedly violating the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, by failing to respond to records requests submitted between March and September 2025 regarding the certification of the 2020 presidential election, the Jan. 6, 2021 attack on the Capitol, the related Trump pardons, and the DOJ’s “Weaponization Working Group” – an internal review of prior Trump-related investigations claimed to be politically motivated. The complaint alleges abuse of government power for political retribution, failure by the FBI and DOJ to conduct adequate searches for records responsive to its request, and failure to release non-exempt documents, as required by FOIA. American Oversight requests that the court order (i) the FBI and DOJ to search for and release all non-exempt records responsive to its FOIA request and (ii) prohibit these agencies from withholding records.
Case Updates: March 9, 2026:DefendantsansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defenses that they have not improperly withheld records requested by Plaintiff over FOIA and they exercised due diligence in responding to Plaintiff’s FOIA request.

Case Name: Center For Reproductive Rights v. U.S. Department of State(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04338
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: The Center for Reproductive Rights (CRR), a not-for-profit organization “dedicated to using the power of law to advance reproductive rights as fundamental human rights around the world”suedthe U.S. Department of State (DOS) for allegedly violating the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, by failing to respond to the CCR’s Aug. 29, 2025 records request. Plaintiff alleges it filed a FOIA request with DOS seeking information about “...the potential destruction of contraceptive supplies purchased with taxpayer funds by the U.S. Agency for International Development (USAID) to provide...reproductive health care in five African countries.” Plaintiff requests that the court order DOS to search for and produce records responsive to Plaintiff’s FOIA request
Case Updates: Feb. 27, 2026:DefendantansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defenses that it exercised due diligence in processing Plaintiff’s FOIA request, conducted an adequate search for documents responsive to Plaintiff’s FOIA request, did not improperly withhold records requested by Plaintiff under FOIA, and its actions or inactions did not violate FOIA or other statutory provisions.March 20, 2026:Plaintiff filed anamended complaintadding two additional FOIA requests. The second FOIA claim concerns a Dec. 3, 2025 request concerning the State Department’s guidance and enforcement of the Siljander Amendment. The third FOIA claim concerns a Jan. 27, 2026 request seeking updated information about the contraceptive stockpile after conflicting reports about whether it had been destroyed and how much product existed.

Case Name: Democracy Forward Foundation v. U.S. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04340
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
