Case Summary: On Oct. 23, 2025, Democracy Forward Foundation, a not-for-profit organization that promotes transparency and accountability in government, sent Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests to the U.S. Department of Justice (DOJ) and the U.S. Department of the Treasury (Treasury) seeking information related to President Donald Trump’s two Federal Tort Claims Act (FTCA) complaints to the DOJ, which request payment of roughly $230 million arising from federal investigations into his past actions. Allegedly, according to public reporting, DOJ attorneys, including some who previously represented President Donald Trump or other subjects during the underlying investigations, are considering administratively settling these FTCA claims and paying the president significant sums. Plaintifffiled suitagainst the DOJ and the Treasury arguing that their failure to promptly produce documents constitutes a FOIA violation. Plaintiff seeks injunctive relief compelling Defendant to search for and produce responsive records.
Case Updates: Feb. 6, 2026:DefendantsansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defenses that it exercised due diligence in processing Plaintiff’s FOIA request, did not improperly withhold records requested by Plaintiff under FOIA, and its actions or inactions did not violate FOIA or other statutory provisions.March 9, 2026:The parties filed ajoint status reportwhich indicates that Defendants’ searches and productions of documents remain ongoing.

Case Name: Democracy Forward Foundation v. U.S. Department of Health and Human Services(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04424
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Nov. 18, 2025, Democracy Forward Foundation, a not-for-profit organization that promotes transparency and accountability in government, sent Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) seeking information about communications and processes that HHS used to make the decision to change the caption on the Assistant Secretary for Health Admiral (Adm.) Rachel Levine’s portrait. Plaintiff alleges that “HHS recently altered the caption of the official portrait of former Assistant Secretary for Health Admiral (“Adm.”) Rachel Levine, who served in the Biden-Harris administration, to reflect her previous name, also referred to as a ‘dead name.’” Plaintiff also alleges that “Adm. Levine was the first transgender person to hold a Senate-confirmed position in the executive branch, and that HHS’s action to alter her legal name is part of the administration’s broader actions to ‘curtail the rights of transgender and intersex people through many federal agencies.’” When HHS failed to respond to the FOIA requests, Plaintifffiled suit, seeking injunctive relief compelling Defendant to search for and produce responsive records.
Case Updates: Feb. 17, 2026:DefendantansweredPlaintiff’s complaint and included the defense that its actions did not violate FOIA or other statutory provisions.

Case Name: Citizens For Responsibility And Ethics In Washington v. Department Of Justice(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-04426
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On March 25, 2025, President Donald Trump signedExecutive Order (EO) 14248(“Preserving and Protecting the Integrity of American Elections”), which calls for vastly expanding the federal government’s role in election administration by requiring states to share voter data with the federal government and granting the Department of Justice (DOJ) authority to compel state obedience with the federal government’s demands for voter data. Since May 2025, at least 40 states have allegedly received letters from the DOJ’s Civil Rights Division demanding full copies of their statewide voter registration files, and the DOJ has initiated legal action against several states for failing to do so. Between September and November 2025, Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW), a non-profit, non-partisan organization committed to ensuring the integrity of government officials and agencies, alleges that it filed three Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests with the DOJ’s Criminal and Civil Rights Divisions to obtain records that would explain the DOJ’s legal basis for demanding voter information and how the DOJ planned to use any data it collected. On Dec. 19, 2025, CREWsuedthe DOJ, alleging in its complaint that the DOJ failed to make timely determinations on and wrongfully withheld records responsive to CREW’s FOIA requests. CREW seeks an order that grants the expedited processing of its FOIA requests and requires the DOJ to preserve all potentially responsive records.
Case Updates: Feb. 19, 2026:Judge Kollar-Kotelly issued anorderwhich granted in part and denied in part Plaintiff’spreliminary injunction motion. The DOJ was ordered to expedite its processing of CREW’s FOIA requests, however Judge Kollar-Kotelly declined to direct the DOJ to process its requests at a specific rate, instead opting to direct the parties to develop a proposal for the expedited processing and production of the most urgent records.

Case Name: Asbestos Disease Awareness Organization v. National Park Service(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00029
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On November 5, 2025, the Asbestos Disease Awareness Organization (ADAO) submitted Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests to nine federal agencies with possible involvement in the White House East Wing demolition. These FOIA requests sought copies of asbestos abatement permits issued for the Ease Wing demolition and other records related to the release and removal of asbestos during the demolition. On Jan. 7, 2026, after the agencies failed to respond to ADAO’s FOIA requests within the statutory time frame, ADAOsuedthe Trump administration for alleged FOIA violations. ADAO requests that the court order the Defendants to produce the requested records.

Case Name: America First Legal Foundation v. U.S. Government Accountability Office(D.D.C.)1:25-cv-00662
Case Status: Case Closed/Dismissed in Favor of Government
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On March 6, 2025, America First Legal Foundation filedsuitagainst the Government Accountability Office (“GAO”), seeking to compel compliance with several FOIA requests. Plaintiff argued that GAO should be treated as an executive-branch agency subject to FOIA’s disclosure requirements, notwithstanding its traditional characterization as a legislative-branch entity that provides oversight and advice to Congress.
Case Updates: Jan. 9, 2026:In amemorandum opinion, the courtgrantedthe GAO’s motion to dismiss America First’s FOIA suit, finding that GAO is an agency in the legislative branch that falls outside of FOIA’s definition of “agency” and thus has no obligation to disclose records under the statute.

Case Name: Public Citizen v. U.S. Department of Health & Human Services et al(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00222
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: FollowingExecutive Order (EO) 14297, which directs federal officials to pursue “most-favored nation’ (MFN) drug pricing, President Donald Trump announcedagreementswith pharmaceutical companies Pfizer and Eli Lily on Sept. 30 and Nov. 6, 2025, respectively. Although the Department of Health & Human Services (HHS) and the Department of Commerce (DOC) negotiated these agreements, they did not release the underlying texts or related records when requested by Public Citizen, a nonprofit organization, pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552. On Jan. 27, 2026, Public Citizen filed acomplaintalleging that HHS and DOC violated FOIA by unlawfully withholding the MFN agreements with Pfizer and Eli Lilly. The complaint asserts that HHS and DOC had no legal basis to withhold the requested records under FOIA and that the withholding of these records therefore deprived the public of timely access to information on major drug-pricing arrangements. Public Citizen requests that the court declare Defendant’s failure to respond to its record requests unlawful and compel the disclosure of the requested records.
Case Updates: Mar. 16, 2026:The parties submitted ajoint status reportwhich indicates that Defendants are in the process of searching for and reviewing potentially responsive records, however Plaintiff requested that the court compel Defendants to complete these searches by March 30, 2026.

Case Name: American Oversight v. Transportation Security Administration(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00193
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: In March 2025, Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and the Transportation Security Administration (TSA) entered into a partnership which included sharing the names and birth dates of airline passengers.On Dec. 19, 2025, Plaintiff American Oversight (AO), a not-for-profit organization that promotes government transparency, filed Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests with TSA and ICE seeking records regarding TSA’s sharing of data with ICE for immigration enforcement purposes at U.S. airports. AO’s requests were allegedly denied by TSA and ICE. On Jan. 22, 2026, AO filed acomplaintalleging violations of FOIA for failure to conduct adequate searches for responsive records, wrongful withholding of non-exempt responsive records, and failure to grant expedited processing of its request. AO requests that the court order TSA and ICE to search for and produce to AO all records responsive to the FOIA requests that are not exempt from production under FOIA.

Case Name: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics In Washington v. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00275
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On May 3, 2024, the United States Department of Justice (DOJ) indicted United States Representative Enrique Roberto “Henry” Cuellar on multiple counts related to bribery and violations of the Foreign Agents Registration Act (FARA) for actions taken to advance the interests of an Azerbaijani oil company and a Mexican bank. On Dec. 3, 2025, while criminal proceedings against Rep. Cuellar were ongoing, President Donald J. Trump announced on Truth Social that he had pardoned Rep. Cuellar. Plaintiff Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW), a not-for-profit organization that promotes transparency in government, submitted several requests under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, in December 2025 seeking, on an expedited basis, records relating to the investigation and indictment of Rep. Cuellar and the decision to pardon him. CREW filed acomplainton Jan. 30, 2026, alleging that Defendants denied the request for expedited processing and failed to provide the requested records as required by FOIA, and asking the court to compel DOJ to process these FOIA requests on an expedited basis and conduct a reasonable search for responsive records.

Case Name: Democracy Defenders Fund v. Federal Bureau of Investigation(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00496
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Feb. 17, 2026, Plaintiff Democracy Defenders Fund (DDF), a non-profit…organization dedicated to restoring American democracy by defending the rule of law, fighting corruption, and protecting elections,suedthe Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) for allegedly violating the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, by failing to respond to records requests related to the investigation of Thomas Homan, the “Border Czar” of the Trump administration. DDF alleges that, on Sept. 23, 2025, it issued a FOIA request to the FBI concerning the FBI’s alleged investigation of Homan after it was reported that Homan allegedly accepted $50,000 from undercover FBI Agents posing as prospective government contractors for the purpose of securing future contracts with the government if President Donald Trump won re-election in 2024. Plaintiff further alleges that the FBI ended its investigation into Homan after Trump took office in 2025. On Jan. 5, 2026, the FBI allegedly denied DDF’s FOIA request in its entirety, a decision which DDF appealed and requested expedited processing for its appeal. The FBI allegedly denied the expedited processing request on Feb. 9, 2026 and DDF alleges the FBI has failed to respond to the substance of DDF’s appeal as of the date it filed its complaint, in violation of FOIA. DDF requests that the court (i) enter an order declaring that the FBI wrongfully withheld the requested agency records and issue a permanent injunction directing the FBI to disclose to DDF all wrongfully withheld documents.

Case Name: Williams v. National Aeronautics and Space Administration(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00564
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Feb. 19, 2026, two scientists and an association of engineers and scientists working at the Goddard Space Flight Center (GSFC) filed acomplaintagainst the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) and the Archivist of the United States to prevent Defendants from disposing of or destroying federal records and materials housed at the Goddard Information and Collaboration Center (GICC) and the rescission of the decisions to permanently shutter the GICC and the NASA Space Science Data Coordinated Archive (the “Database”). Plaintiffs allege that the reversal in September 2025 of NASA’s longstanding records-preservation framework violated the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as arbitrary and capricious, in excess of statutory authority, unlawful failure to act, and action taken without observance of required procedure and further allege that the decision to close the GICC and Database represents arbitrary and capricious action in violation of the APA. They seek declaratory and injunctive relief including the vacatur and recission of the closure decisions and an order requiring Defendants to preserve the Database and other records.

Case Name: ProPublica v. United States Department of Education(S.D.N.Y.)1:26-cv-01576
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Since Jan. 20, 2025, the U.S. Department of Education (ED) has implemented changes within its Office for Civil Rights (OCR), including the launch of new investigations and the reversal of some previous resolution agreements. OCR previously published a list of institutions under investigation and updated this list weekly, however it has not been updated since Jan. 14, 2025. Because of these actions, the public has no way of knowing which schools are under investigation or what OCR is doing on behalf of students experiencing discrimination. Between February and July 2025, ProPublica, an independent, nonprofit newsroom, allegedly submitted four Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests to ED, seeking records regarding new investigations of entities or institutions, notifications of terminations of resolution agreements, and communications with specific private groups. On Feb. 25, 2026, ProPublicasuedED, alleging that ED violated FOIA by failing to respond to ProPublica’s FOIA requests within the statutory deadline. ProPublica requests that the court declare that the records sought are subject to disclosure under FOIA and order ED to undertake an adequate search for, and immediately release, all responsive, non-exempt records to ProPublica.

Case Name: Oversight v. U.S. Customs and Border Protection(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00750
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Jan. 24, 2026, two U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) agents shot at and killed civilian Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, Minnesota. Following growing public concerns regarding CBP’s use of force and its compliance with protections under the U.S. Constitution, American Oversight, a nonpartisan, non-profit organization engaged in disseminating information about the government to the public, allegedly submitted three Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests to CBP on Jan. 26, 2026. Specifically, American Oversight sought records related to CBP’s expanded operations and use of force–including training materials, the communications of former CBP Chief Gregory Bovino, and legal advice and analysis concerning the Fourth Amendment and stop, search, and seizure authority. CBP allegedly acknowledged receipt of these requests, but has otherwise failed to respond. On March 4, 2026, American OversightsuedCBP, alleging it violated FOIA by failing to conduct adequate searches for responsive records, wrongfully withholding non-exempt agency records, and failing to provide all non-exempt responsive records. Plaintiff seeks that court to compel CBP’s compliance with the requirements of FOIA by releasing the requested records and prohibiting CBP from continuing to withhold records responsive to American Oversight’s FOIA requests.
Case Updates: April 3, 2026:District Judge Batesdeniedthe administration’s April 1, 2026motionto stay due to partial governmental shut down. Judge Bates extended the administration’s reply deadline until May 4 and ordered the administration to provide a status report by April 13 advising on whether agency staff have returned from furlough or if the reply deadline should be extended further.

Case Name: Center for Constitutional Rights v. United States Department of Justice(S.D.N.Y.)1:26-cv-01821
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Since taking office on Jan. 20, 2025, President Donald Trump has issued and implemented a series of executive orders that restrict access to medical care, travel, identification documents, and public accommodations by trans, non-binary, intersex, and gender non-conforming people (Anti-LGBTQIA+ EOs). The Center for Constitutional Rights (CCR), a non-partisan non-profit organization primarily engaged the fields of civil and international human rights, alleges that the implementation of Anti-LGBTQIA+ EOs amounts to discrimination against, and the erasure of, trans and intersex people in areas including employment, housing, legal aid, and medical care. CRR alleges that, between February and May 2025, it sent Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests to Defendant agencies concerning their implementation of Trump’s anti-LGBTQIA+ executive orders. On March 5, 2026, CRRsuedthe Defendant agencies, alleging FOIA violations after most of these agencies improperly refused to conduct any search and that four refused to respond to CRR’s FOIA requests at all.  CRR requests that the court order the Defendants to immediately make a full, adequate, and expedited search for the requested records in all of CRR’s FOIA requests; expeditiously process CRR’s FOIA requests; and disclose the requested records to CRR upon the completion of their expedited processing.

Case Name: Democratic National Committee v. U.S. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00825
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: In October 2025, the Democratic National Committee (DNC) submitted eleven Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552, requests for records concerning the potential deployment of federal agents and troops to polling places, ballot drop boxes, and election offices. These FOIA requests were allegedly submitted following President Donald Trump and his administration’s repeated threats to free and fair elections. On March 10, 2026, the DNCsuedthe Department of Justice (DOJ), Department of Defense (DoD), and Department of Homeland Security (DHS), alleging that these agencies violated FOIA by failing to produce records or even a list of withheld documents to the DNC within the statutory deadline. The DNC seeks an order compelling the production of responsive records, a Vaughn index of any withheld materials, and attorneys’ fees.

Case Name: American Oversight v. U.S. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00835
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Since January 20, 2025, the Department of Justice (DOJ) has filed lawsuits against more than two dozen states to obtain unredacted voter files — including registered voters' driver's license numbers and the last four digits of their Social Security numbers. On March 10, 2026, Plaintiff American Oversight, a nonpartisan nonprofit government transparency organization, filed acomplaintalleging these lawsuits are part of a broader effort to compile a national voter database, with DOJ intending to cross-reference the collected data with a Department of Homeland Security (DHS) database to identify noncitizens registered to vote and to transfer voter data to U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) for use in criminal and immigration-related investigations. American Oversight submitted four FOIA requests to DOJ and ICE in December 2025 seeking records about the agencies' collection, use, and sharing of voter data. The complaint alleges that Defendants failed to comply with FOIA's statutory time-limit provisions, failed to issue final determinations, and have produced no responsive records, leaving Plaintiff unable to inform the public about the voter data program. Plaintiff alleges two violations of the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552: (1) failure to conduct adequate searches for responsive records; and (2) wrongful withholding of non-exempt responsive records. Plaintiff seeks an order requiring Defendants to conduct reasonable searches, produce all non-exempt records within 20 days accompanied by indexes justifying any withholdings, and an injunction barring Defendants from continuing to withhold non-exempt records.

Case Name: Environmental Defense Fund v. Wright(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00928
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: In March 2025, Energy Secretary Chris Wright assembled a “Climate Working Group” (CWG) of  five outside scientists tasked with producing a report for DOE evaluating the science underlying EPA’s 2009 “Endangerment Finding,” the regulatory determination that greenhouse gas emissions endanger public health and welfare. DOE published the CWG's report on July 29, 2025, and EPA cited it in its Aug. 1, 2025proposed ruleto rescind the Endangerment Finding. In a separate lawsuit (EDF v. Wright, 1:25-cv-12249), a court found that the CWG was subject to the Federal Advisory Committee Act (FACA), and an order in that case compelled DOE to produce the CWG's records. Those records revealed that at least three DOE political appointees used personal email accounts extensively to conduct government business related to the CWG and the Endangerment Finding rulemaking.The Federal Records Act (FRA)requiresfederal employees who use personal email accounts for official business to either copy their official account on the communication or forward a complete copy to their official account within 20 days. After neither DOE nor the National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) responded to EDF's Feb. 9, 2026 notice letters identifying the apparent violations, the Environmental Defense Fund filed acomplaintalleging that Secretary Wright and Acting Archivist James Byron have each failed to perform nondiscretionary duties under the FRA — specifically, the obligation to notify the Archivist and initiate action through the Attorney General to recover unlawfully removed records under 44 U.S.C. §§ 2905(a) and 3106(a). EDF seeks declaratory and injunctive relief compelling Defendants to fulfill those obligations, and mandamus under 28 U.S.C. § 1361.

Case Name: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington v. U.S. Department of Homeland Security(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-00998
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On March 23, 2026, Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW) filed a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA)complaintin the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia against the U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) seeking records from DHS and its components concerning the administration’s tracking and surveillance of protesters opposing expanded immigration enforcement. CREW’s February 13, 2026 FOIA requests sought memoranda, forms, policies, and database records related to DHS’s collection of images, license plates, and other personal data on protesters and observers during operations such as “Operation Metro Surge” in Minnesota and “Operation Catch of the Day” in Maine, amid reports that DHS agents photographed protesters, threatened to place them in a “domestic terrorist” database, and used facial recognition and other technologies to track critics.The complaint alleges that, despite assigning tracking numbers and acknowledging one request, DHS and its components failed to timely determine CREW’s entitlement to expedited processing or to issue determinations on the requests within FOIA’s statutory deadlines, and have not produced any responsive records. CREW brings two FOIA causes of action: wrongful denial of expedited processing under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(6)(E) and DHS’s own regulations, and wrongful withholding for failing to conduct adequate searches, make timely determinations, and release all non‑exempt records. CREW seeks declaratory relief that DHS is violating FOIA; injunctions compelling DHS and its components to grant expedited processing, preserve potentially responsive records, promptly process the requests, and disclose all non‑exempt records with a Vaughn index; fee waivers; expeditious proceedings; retention of jurisdiction; and an award of costs and attorneys’ fees.

Case Name: Jane Doe 1 v. United States(N.D. Cal.)5:26-cv-02624
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Between December 2025 and January 2026 and as required by law, the Department of Justice (DOJ)releasedapproximately 3.5 million pages of investigative files related to Jeffrey Epstein’s criminal prosecution, including unredacted records containing the personally identifiable information (PII) of approximately 100 Epstein survivors. Following complaints from survivors and a February 2026 hearing before Judge Richard M. Berman, the DOJ retracted approximately 9,500 documents, but the records allegedly remain accessible through third-party platforms, including Google.On March 27, 2026, a Jeffrey Epstein survivor filed a putative class actioncomplainton behalf of all Epstein survivors whose PII was disclosed in the December 2025–January 2026 Epstein files released by the DOJ, in alleged violation of the Privacy Act of 1974, by disclosing records without consent. The complaint also brings claims against Google, alleging that the company has continued to index, cache, and generate AI content containing survivors’ PII despite receiving actual notice of the unlawful disclosures and that the conduct violates California’s Unfair Competition Law (UCL). Plaintiff seeks class certification, preliminary and permanent injunctive relief requiring Google to de-index and remove all victim-identifying materials, damages, and declaratory relief.
Case Updates: April 20, 2026:Magistrate Judge DeMarchigrantedPlaintiff’s motion to proceed pseudonymously.

Case Name: National Health Law Program v. Department of Health and Human Services(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01200
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On April 9, 2026, the National Health Law Program filed acomplaintagainst the Department of Health and Human Services and the Centers for failure to respond to Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests. The requests, submitted on Feb. 17, 2026, seek records relating to HHS’s and CMS’s data-sharing practices with the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), including policies governing the transfer of Medicaid enrollee information to ICE, communications between CMS officials and DHS representatives, requests made to state Medicaid agencies for immigration-related information, and the number of individuals whose data has been shared. Plaintiff alleges that beginning in the summer of 2025, HHS and CMS departed from their prior policy of not sharing Medicaid data with ICE and began doing so for immigration enforcement purposes, as detailed in aNov. 25, 2025 CMS Notice, without fully disclosing the scope of information transferred.Having received no final determinations or records in response to five sets of paired FOIA requests, Plaintiff alleges that HHS and CMS have failed to conduct adequate searches for responsive records and are wrongfully withholding non-exempt agency records. Plaintiff seeks declaratory and injunctive relief compelling production of all non-exempt responsive records, along with Vaughn indexes for any withheld materials, and attorneys’ fees.

Case Name: Democracy Forward Foundation v. U.S. Department of Justice(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01206
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: According topublic reporting, the Civil Rights Division (CRT) at the Department of Justice (DOJ) has allegedly collected state voter registration data to share with the Department of Homeland Security. CRT officials have also allegedly communicated with external individuals and groups associated with election integrity concerns, including alleged election-deniers Kurt Olsen and Election Integrity Network leader Cleta Mitchell. Between November 2025 and February 2026, nonprofit Democracy Forward Foundation (DFF) filed four FOIA requests seeking records of DOJ communications with those individuals and organizations, inter-agency agreements regarding elections and voter registration lists, and related internal communications and calendars of CRT voting section officials. Beyond acknowledging the requests, the department has not allegedly responded in substance to any of these requests. DFF then sought expedited processing in April 2026, given the approaching 2026 federal midterm elections.On April 10, 2026, DFF filed acomplaintalleging that the DOJ violated the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA)by failing to conduct reasonable searches, produce non-exempt responsive records, or respond within statutory time limits. DFF seeks a court order directing DOJ to search for and produce all responsive non-exempt records, a Vaughn index of any withheld records, and an injunction against further withholding.
Case Updates: April 20, 2026:Plaintiff filed anamended complaintintroducing an additional claim alleging that the DOJ violated the FOIA by failing to grant expedited processing for the requests made in this matter, citing exceptional media interest and an urgent public need to be informed about federal government activity. The claim also provides an update on the Kurt Olsen communication request, acknowledging recent communications by the CRT with regards to this matter.

Case Name: Democracy Defenders Fund v. Department of Homeland Security(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01230
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: A nonpartisan democracy nonprofit and an immigration law firm filed acomplaintagainst the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), the State Department, the Department of Commerce, and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) alleging failure to comply with multiple FOIA requests related to the “Gold Card” Visa Program implemented pursuant toExecutive Order 14351.On Sept. 19, 2025, President Donald Trump issuedExecutive Order 14351, directing the Secretary of Commerce, in coordination with the Secretaries of State and Homeland Security, to create and implement a Gold Card Visa Program. Under the program, individuals who pay $1 million to the Department of Commerce — or on whose behalf a corporation pays $2 million — are deemed eligible for EB-1A and EB-2 employment-based visas and a National Interest Waiver under 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(2)(B). The program launched in December 2025. Democracy Defenders Fund (DDF), a nonpartisan nonprofit, and Colombo Hurd PL, an immigration law firm, filed acomplaintalleging that DHS, USCIS, the State Department, and the Department of Commerce failed to respond to multiple FOIA requests — filed between January and February 2026 — seeking records on the Gold Card Program’s creation, implementation, processing procedures, and financial data. Plaintiffs allege Defendants violated FOIA’s statutory response deadlines, 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(6)(A)(ii), and wrongfully denied expedited processing. Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief.

Case Name: Center for Biological Diversity v. U.S. Office of Management and Budget(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01224
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Beginning in March 2025, the Center for Biological Diversity, an environmental nonprofit with more than 93,000 members, submitted five FOIA requests to the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) seeking the calendars and schedules of senior officials involved in regulatory review and environmental rollback, including OMB Director Russell Vought, former Acting OIRA Administrator Jeffrey Clark, General Counsel Mark Paoletta, Natural Resources Director Stuart Levenbach, and approximately 45 career OIRA regulatory examiners. The Center alleges it sought these records to document meetings related to large-scale reductions in force and the revision or rescission of environmental and public health regulations. It also alleges that calendars and schedules are relatively straightforward requests under FOIA as they do not typically implicate complex redactions under deliberative process or attorney-client privileges. OMB acknowledged all five requests but has not released any responsive records. For three of the requests, OMB estimated a processing time of 36–48 months, citing backlog. The Center filed administrative appeals, which OMB granted while acknowledging its own statutory violation, but provided no meaningful remedy.Plaintiffs filed acomplaintalleging that OMB violated FOIA,5 U.S.C. § 552, by failing to issue determinations within the mandatory 20-business-day deadline under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(6)(A)(i), failing to conduct adequate searches for responsive records under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(3), and failing to promptly disclose all responsive records under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(3)(A). Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief, an injunction ordering immediate determinations and prompt production of all nonexempt records, a Vaughn index for any withheld records, and attorneys’ fees.

Case Name: American Civil Liberties Union Foundation v. Department of Health and Human Services(S.D.N.Y.)1:26-cv-03098
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: The ACLU and the youth advocacy group National Center for Youth LawsuedHHS, the Administration for Children and Families (ACF), and the Office of Refugee Resettlement (ORR) seeking records related to the administration’s treatment of unaccompanied youth in ORR custody who are pregnant or seeking reproductive health services. Plaintiffs allege that, despite a 1997 settlement agreement requiring the administration to provide reproductive services to unaccompanied youth, ORR has a history of obstructing access to reproductive care. This history, Plaintiffs claim, in addition to an Office of Legal Counselopinionthat transporting youth to obtain reproductive care is unlawful andnews reportsthat ORR is placing many youth in Texas, a state with restrictive abortion laws, animated Plaintiffs’ concern that ORR was not following its regulatory and contractual obligations.Plaintiffs filed a request under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) in February 2025 seeking records related to “unaccompanied youth’s pregnancy identification, requests for and access to abortion, requests for and access to pre-natal care and childbirth, requests for and access to contraception, and the use of restraints on pregnant unaccompanied youth while in ORR custody.” This request, Plaintiffs allege, has gone unanswered, violating both HHS regulations and the Freedom of Information Act. Plaintiffs seek an injunction ordering the administration to search for and disclose all requested records, and to preemptively block the administration from charging Plaintiffs fees for the request, which they argue is likely to contribute significantly to public understanding of the operations or activities of the government.

Case Name: Democracy Forward Foundation v. U.S. Election Assistance Commission(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01271
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: Democracy Forward Foundation (DFF) filedsuitagainst the U.S. Election Assistance Commission (EAC) seeking compliance with two FOIA requests related to DFF’s concerns that the administration and EAC leadership were undermining integrity in the upcoming midterm elections. On Feb. 6, 2026, DFF submitted two FOIA requests to the EAC, the independent bipartisan federal agency that assists state and local election officials in administering federal elections. The first request sought emails sent by the four EAC Commissioners to external addresses and text messages sent or received on official business from October 2025 onward. The second sought communications between the Commissioners and seven named external entities — including the Election Integrity Network, Cleta Mitchell, the Public Interest Legal Foundation, and True the Vote — from May 2025 onward. DFF alleges that the records bear on the influence of outside individuals and organizations on EAC operations followingExecutive Order 14248, which directs the EAC to require documentary proof of citizenship for the federal mail voter-registration form and conditions certain federal grants on state cooperation with election-integrity measures. Plaintiff argues that EAC acknowledged receipt of the requests but estimated it would respond in ten months, after the 2026 federal elections, violating its duties under FOIA to conduct a reasonable search for the requested records. Plaintiff seeks an order compelling the EAC to conduct adequate searches, produce all nonexempt responsive records and a Vaughn index of any withheld materials, injunctive relief against continued withholding, and attorneys' fees and costs.

Case Name: Oversight v. Prevention et al(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01351
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On March 20, 2025, President Trump signedExecutive Order 14243, directing the federal government to share data across agencies with the stated purpose of eliminating information silos. Following the order, the Trump administration reportedly significantly expanded its use of Palantir Technologies — a data analytics and technology firm co-founded by Peter Thiel  — awarding federal contracts worth hundreds of millions of dollars to the firm across multiple agencies.On April 21, 2026, American Oversight, a nonpartisan nonprofit government transparency organization, filed acomplaintafter the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, the Department of Homeland Security, Immigration and Customs Enforcement, the Internal Revenue Service, and the Social Security Administration allegedly failed to respond within FOIA's statutory deadline to requests seeking records of Palantir’s communications with agency officials, contracts, data-access agreements, and written analyses produced using agency data. Plaintiffs allege violations of FOIA, 5 U.S.C. § 552, for failure to conduct adequate searches for responsive records and for wrongful withholding of non-exempt records. Plaintiffs seek an order compelling adequate searches, production of all non-exempt responsive records within twenty days, and declaratory and injunctive relief against continued withholding.

Case Name: Freedom of the Press Foundation v. Trump(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01402
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On April 1, 2026, the Department of Justice’s Office of Legal Counsel (OLC) issued anopiniondeclaring the Presidential Records Act of 1978 (PRA) “unconstitutional” and “invalid in its entirety.” The OLC opinion stated that “the President need not further comply with its dictates.” On April 2, 2026, White House Counsel David Warrington issued amemorandumadopting the OLC Opinion and establishing new recordkeeping practices for Executive Office of the President (EOP) staff that Plaintiffs allege are inconsistent with the PRA. This allegedly includes provisions permitting EOP personnel to decline to preserve text messages that constitute Presidential records under the Act. UnderExecutive Order 14215, the OLC Opinion is binding on the National Archives and Records Administration (NARA).The Freedom of the Press Foundation and Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW) — two nonpartisan nonprofits that regularly request and rely on Presidential records to fulfill their missions — filed acomplaintalleging that Defendants’ policies of non-compliance threaten the irrevocable destruction of Presidential records and impair Plaintiffs’ access to such records through the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA). Plaintiffs bring constitutional and equitable claims against all Defendants, including a separation-of-powers claim that the President’s unilateral refusal to comply with a duly enacted statute violates Article I and the Take Care Clause, and ultra vires and equitable claims seeking injunctive relief. Plaintiffs seek mandamus under 28 U.S.C. §§ 1361 and 1651 against Trump, Vance, and the Acting Archivist to compel compliance with specific non-discretionary PRA duties. Plaintiffs bring APA claims under 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A)–(C) and a FOIA policy-or-practice claim alleging that NARA’s refusal to process FOIA requests for Presidential records impairs future access. Plaintiffs seek declaratory judgments that the PRA is constitutional and that Defendants’ non-compliance policies are unlawful; preliminary and permanent injunctive relief; and an order requiring preservation of all PRA-covered records during the pendency of the litigation.
Case Updates: May 20, 2026:Judge BatesgrantedPlaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction, ordering the Executive Office of the President, the White House Office, the Office of the Vice President, and Chief of Staff Susie Wiles to comply in full with the Presidential Records Act — including preserving all presidential and vice presidential records, ensuring that employees using non-official messaging platforms (including Signal and WhatsApp) copy or forward records to official accounts within 20 days, and reestablishing compliant records retention policies — effective May 26, 2026. The court issued an accompanyingopinionholding that Plaintiffs have standing in both cases due to a substantial risk of noncompliance with the PRA and that the PRA is likely constitutional under the Property Clause and, independently, under the Necessary and Proper Clause. The court rejected the government’s Separation of Powers argument, finding that the PRA was narrowly drafted to serve several legitimate legislative purposes, falls squarely within Congress’s authority to structure the internal operations of the national government, and  does not impose an “an intolerable administrative burden” on the Executive Branch. The court also found that the administration’s internalguidance memolikely violates the PRA with respect to text message preservation, records created by the President or Vice President personally, and records disposal procedures.

Case Name: Protect Democracy Project v. Internal Revenue Service(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01486
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Sept. 22, 2025, President Donald Trump issued anexecutive orderdesignating “Antifa” – an abbreviation for anti-fascist – as a domestic terrorist organization, and issuedNational Security Presidential Memorandum 7 (NSPM-7), “Countering Domestic Terrorism and Organized Political Violence.” On Dec. 19, 2025, Protect Democracy Project, a nonprofit organization focused on democratic governance, submitted a FOIA request to the IRS seeking records relating to the agency’s implementation of those directives — specifically, any internal guidance, memoranda, protocols, or policies governing that implementation, excluding records identifying specific taxpayers or organizations. The IRS conducted a search, found no responsive records, and denied the request. Protect Democracy appealed on March 12, 2026, and the IRS acknowledged the appeal and told Plaintiff it would require until April 28, 2026 to consider it.On April 29, 2026, Protect Democracy filed acomplaintalleging that Defendants violated FOIA, 5 U.S.C. § 552, by failing to conduct an adequate search for responsive records and by wrongfully withholding non-exempt records. Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief compelling a reasonable search and the production of all non-exempt responsive records.

Case Name: Public Employees for Environmental Responsibility v. U.S. Department of the Interior(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01611
Executive Action: Response to FOIA and Records Retention
Case Summary: On Feb. 25, 2026, Public Employees for Environmental Responsibility (PEER) submitted three Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests to the Department of Interior for records related to the funding and administration of Freedom250, the nonprofit entity involved in planning the nation’s 250th anniversary celebration. Among other documents, PEER requested communications between DOI employees and Freedom250 representatives, memoranda of understanding with the National Park Foundation (NPF), donor vetting records, changes to the National Park Service (NPS)’s donations policy since Jan. 20, 2025, and a grant agreement for the “A250 Events” program. Because DOI had neither made a final determination nor produced records responsive to this request, on May 11, 2026, PEER filed acomplaintalleging that DOI’s failure to do so within the statutory deadline constitutes a wrongful withholding of agency records. Having allegedly exhausted administrative remedies, Plaintiff seeks a declaration that DOI wrongfully withheld records, a permanent injunction directing DOI to produce all responsive records, and attorneys' fees and costs.

Case Name: Phang v. Blanche(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01417
Executive Action: Epstein Files Transparency Act
Case Summary: In November 2025, Congress passed and President Donald Trump signed the Epstein Files Transparency Act, Pub. L. 119-38, requiring the Department of Justice and FBI to publicly produce all documents relating to Jeffrey Epstein, Ghislaine Maxwell, and individuals named in connection with their activities within 30 days of enactment. On April 27, 2026, Katie Phang, a journalist, lawyer, and legal analyst who has extensively covered Epstein and his network, filed acomplaintalleging that Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche has failed to comply with the Act in multiple respects: releasing only “a mere 3,965 files” by the statutory deadline, failing to redact names and images of victims, redacting thousands of documents — including the identities of alleged co-conspirators — without publishing the required written justifications in the Federal Register; retracting previously published documents referencing President Trump; and failing to produce documents that other sources confirm exist. Phang alleges these violations have directly impaired her ability to report on the Epstein files.Plaintiff bring claims under the Administrative Procedure Act, 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A)–(C), for agency action contrary to law, in excess of statutory authority, and arbitrary and capricious; and under ultra vires doctrine and the Declaratory Judgment Act, 28 U.S.C. §§ 2201–2202. Plaintiff seeks a declaratory judgment that Defendant is in violation of the Act, injunctive relief requiring production of all covered materials with impermissible redactions removed and lawful redactions explained, appointment of a special master to oversee compliance, and a jury trial.

Case Name: Valuta Corporation, Inc. v. Financial Crimes Enforcement Network(W.D. Tex.)3:25-cv-00191
Executive Action: Border Enforcement
Case Summary: In March 2025, Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) issued aGeographic Targeting Order(GTO) with the stated purpose of combatting illicit activity at the US-Mexico border. The order articulated that Money Services Businesses (MSBs) operating within 30 designated zip codes near the border are required to file Currency Transaction Reports for cash transactions exceeding $200. On May 30, Plaintiffs Valuta Corporation, Inc., and Payan’s Fuel Center, Inc. filed suit against Defendants, claiming that this order violates the Fourth Amendment, the Administrative Procedure Act (APA).
Case Updates: May 30, 2025:Plaintiffs filed a motion for atemporary restraining order.June 9, 2025:Defendants filed anoppositionto Plaintiff’s motion for a temporary restraining order.June 10, 2025:Plaintiffsfiled a replyto Defendants’ opposition to their motion for a temporary restraining order.June 24, 2025:Judge Leon SchydlowergrantedPlaintiffs’ motion for a temporary restraining order, barring the administration’s enforcement of the Geographic Targeting Order and noting that the order likely violates the APA and that the government failed to address key problems, such as the ease of circumventing the order by crossing the street and the disproportionate burden on small businesses.July 2, 2025:Judge Schydlowerextendedthe temporary restraining order through July 22, 2025.July 22, 2025:Judge SchydlowergrantedPlaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction, temporarily enjoining Defendants from “enforcing, implementing, and otherwise giving effect to the Border GTO as to the named Plaintiffs.”Sept. 12, 2025:Plaintiffsinformedthe court that Defendants had reissued the Border GTO with several modifications, including extending it to March 6, 2026, adding two new counties, and increasing the reporting threshold from $200 to $1,000. Plaintiffs asked the court to grant them summary judgment “without any need for further briefing” on their original claims.Sept. 19, 2025:The government filed anoticeof appeal from Judge Schydlower’s July 22 preliminary injunction, initiating an appeal in the Fifth Circuit as No.25-50776.Sept. 22, 2025:In light of the reissued Border GTO, Judge Schydlowerorderedthe parties to brief by Sept. 29 “the pending complaint’s viability, the TRO, and the preliminary injunction, and whether the parties’ arguments supporting and opposing the TRO, the preliminary injunction, and the pending summary judgment motion are, in whole or in part, moot.” Bothpartiesfiledon time.Oct. 1, 2025:Plaintiffsfileda cross-appeal challenging the scope of Judge Schydlower’s July 22 preliminary injunction, which was docketed in the Fifth Circuit together with the government’s appeal as No.25-50776.Oct. 6, 2025:Judge Schydlower issued anopinionand order to his July 22 grant of a preliminary injunction. The court adopted Judge Biery’s reasoning fromTexas Association of Money Services Businesses v. Bondi, currently on appeal in the Fifth Circuit as No. 25-50481.Oct. 20, 2025:The Fifth CircuitgrantedPlaintiffs’ unopposedmotionto stay further appellate proceedings on the parties’ cross-appeals pending issuance of the mandate inTexas Association of Money Services Businesses v. Bondi, No. 25-50481

Case Name: Citizens Against Donald Trump, Inc. v. Donald J. Trump(E.D. Mo.)4:26-cv-00037
Executive Action: Attempt to Acquire Greenland
Case Summary: On Jan. 11, 2026, Citizens Against donald[sic] Trump for Democracy (“CAT”), a Missouri-based non-profit, filed acomplaintagainst President Trump alleging that President Trump’s avowed intention to acquire Greenland is a violation of international law, in particular the Convention on Rights and Duties of States, and of NATO rules, in particular Article 5 of the Washington Treaty, and asking the court to enjoin him from “continuing his attempt to acquire Greenland” and to declare that “the acquisition of Greenland is not a power delegated to the United States by the Constitution.”
Case Updates: Jan. 26, 2026:Judge Fleissigdismissedwithout prejudice Plaintiff’s motion for a preliminary injunction hearing to refiling, directing Plaintiff to file a compliant motion.

Case Name: Zhaocheng Tan v. Donald Trump(D.C. Cir.)26-1047
Executive Action: Sale of TikTok
Case Summary: In April 2024, Congress passed theProtecting Americans From Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act(PAFACA), Pub. L. No. 118-50, requiring ByteDance to divest TikTok's U.S. operations by January 19, 2025 — or face up to $850 billion in civil penalties — citing concerns that China could leverage ByteDance to capture Americans' personal data and disseminate government propaganda. ByteDance did not divest, and between January 20, 2025 and September 25, 2025, President Donald Trump issued five executive orders —Executive Order 14166(Jan. 20, 2025),Executive Order 14258(Apr. 4, 2025),Executive Order 14310(June 19, 2025),Executive Order 14350(Sept. 16, 2025), andExecutive Order 14352(Sept. 25, 2025) — purporting to extend the divestiture deadline and directing the Attorney General not to enforce the statute. On January 22, 2026, Trump announced approval of a sale of TikTok’s U.S. assets to TikTok USDS Joint Venture LLC as a purported “qualified divestiture.” Two U.S. investors in Alphabet Inc. and Meta Platforms, Inc. — parent companies of YouTube and Instagram, which compete directly with TikTok — filed apetition for reviewon March 5, 2026, alleging that the extension orders, non-enforcement directives, and the sale approval caused financial injury when shares in both companies fell following those announcements. The petition alleges that all five extensions and the sale approval constitute ultra vires action because the statute permitted only a single 90-day extension conditioned on certifications to Congress that were never made; that the Attorney General's failure to investigate statutory violations constitutes final agency action that is arbitrary and capricious and in excess of statutory authority under the Administrative Procedure Act, 5 U.S.C. §§ 706(2)(A) and 706(2)(C); and that Respondents' conduct warrants equitable relief underArmstrong v. Exceptional Child Center, Inc., 575 U.S. 320 (2015). Petitioners seek a declaratory judgment that the extensions, the failure to investigate, and the sale approval are contrary to law, and an order directing the Attorney General to conduct the investigations the statute expressly mandates.
Case Updates: April 6, 2026:Plaintiffsamendedtheir complaint to add a new Plaintiff: John Doe, a pseudonymous former ByteDance employee. The amended complaint makes new factual and harm allegations based on behalf of Doe.April 29, 2026:The D.C. CircuitallowedPetitioners toamendtheir complaint to include information from a former employee at TikTok in the United States. In the unsigned order, the panel also allowed the former employee to proceed pseudonymously.

Case Name: Anthropic PBC v. U.S. Department of War(N.D. Cal.)3:26-cv-01996
Executive Action: Invocation of Federal Acquisition Supply Chain Security Act
Case Summary: Coming soon.On March 9, 2026, the artificial intelligence company Anthropic filed acomplaintagainst eighteen federal departments and agencies alleging violations of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) as well as the Fifth and First Amendments and Article II of the U.S. Constitution in the promulgation of thePresidential Directiveand subsequentSecretarial Orderdesignating Anthropic a “Supply-Chain Risk to National Security” and decreeing that “effective immediately, no contractor, supplier, or partner that does business with the United States military may conduct any commercial activity with Anthropic.” Plaintiff seeks declaratory and injunctive relief.
Case Updates: March 26, 2026:In anopinionandorder, Judge Rita Lin granted Anthropic’s motion for a preliminary injunction, blocking the administration from implementing policies banning federal agencies, contractors, suppliers, and partners from using Anthropic’s technology. The court found Anthropic likely to succeed on its First Amendment retaliation, Fifth Amendment due process, and APA claims, holding that the government’s actions appeared designed to punish Anthropic for protected speech rather than to address a legitimate national security concern. The court granted Defendants a seven-day administrative stay to seek emergency relief from the court of appeals.April 23, 2026:Judge Lindeniedthe Trump administration’s motion for a stay of proceedings to allow the administration to compile relevant records.

Case Name: Anthropic PBC v. U.S. Department of War(D.C. Cir.)26-1049
Case Status: Temporary Block of Government Action Denied
Executive Action: Invocation of Federal Acquisition Supply Chain Security Act
Case Summary: On March 9, 2026, the artificial intelligence company Anthropicpetitionedthe D.C. Circuit for review of the Department of War’sdeterminationthat Anthropic presents a supply chain risk to national security under the Federal Acquisition Supply Chain Security Act. Petitioner alleges that the Department of War’s actions are in violation of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) and are a “pretextual form of retaliation” in violation of the First and Fifth Amendments to the U.S. Constitution.
Case Updates: March 11, 2026:Anthropicaskedthe D.C. Circuit to stay the Department’s supply chain risk designation while its review of the case proceeds.April 8, 2026:The D.C. CircuitdeniedAnthropic’s emergency motion for a stay pending review, finding that while the company will likely suffer some irreparable financial harm, Anthropic appears to have “financially benefited” from its refusal to partner with the administration and the balance of the equities favors the government’s “management of how, and through whom the Department of War secures vital AI technology during an active military conflict.” The court granted Anthropic’s alternative request for expedited consideration, setting oral argument for May 19, 2026.

Case Name: Richie Enterprises LLC v. Bondi(D.D.C.)1:26-cv-01085
Executive Action: DEA Enforcement Actions
Case Summary: On March 31, 2026, Richie Pharmacal, a wholesale drug distributor based in Glasgow, KentuckysuedAttorney General Pam Bondi, DEA Administrator Terrence Cole, and the DEA after the agency issued anImmediate Suspension Order(ISO) suspending Richie’s Certificate of Registration (COR) and seized its controlled substance inventory that had been DEA-registered for over 50 years. The ISO alleged that Richie failed to adequately control the provision of controlled substances, citing a 2025 phishing scheme in which an imposter posing as a DEA agent stole 60,000 oxycodone pills, a DEA undercover operation in which Richie distributed controlled substances without adequate due diligence and failed to report “suspicious orders,” and Richie’s distribution of controlled substances to a customer with an invalid state license. Richie alleged that the ISO was arbitrary and capricious under the Administrative Procedure Act because the DEA failed to adequately support its finding of “imminent danger to the public health or safety” as required by 21 U.S.C. § 824(d)(1) and failed to consider Richie's corrective actions; and that the DEA’s failure to promptly schedule a post-suspension hearing violated the Fifth Amendment Due Process Clause and the APA. Richie seeks declaratory relief, dissolution of the ISO, a preliminary and permanent injunction against its enforcement, and return of its COR and inventory.

Case Name: Alliance of Nurses for Healthy Environments v. Zeldin(N.D. Cal.)3:26-cv-03118
Executive Action: Clean Air Act Enforcement
Case Summary: On Feb. 7, 2024, EPA promulgated afinal rulestrengthening the primary national ambient air quality standard for fine particulate matter (PM2.5) under the Clean Air Act,42 U.S.C. § 7409, lowering the annual limit from 12.0 to 9.0 micrograms per cubic meter. That rulemaking triggered the EPA Administrator’s statutory duty, under42 U.S.C. § 7407(d)(1)(B)(i), to promulgate attainment and nonattainment designations for all areas of the country within two years (by Feb. 7, 2026) via publication in the Federal Register.A coalition of seventeen environmental and public health nonprofit organizations filed acomplaintalleging that EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin has not promulgated a single designation under the 2024 standard as of the filing date. Plaintiffs allege that more than 75 million Americans live in counties where PM2.5 levels violate the 2024 standard, including in major cities like Atlanta, Charlotte, Cincinnati, Los Angeles, Pittsburgh, and San Jose, and that the failure to issue designations delays state implementation plan requirements and deprives Plaintiffs’ members of Clean Air Act health protections. Plaintiffs allege a violation of the Clean Air Act’s citizen suit provision,42 U.S.C. § 7604(a)(2), for failure to perform a nondiscretionary duty. Plaintiffs seek a declaratory judgment that the EPA Administrator violated the Act, an injunction compelling him to promulgate and publish designations, and an order retaining the court’s jurisdiction to monitor and enforce compliance.

Case Name: State of California v. Zeldin(N.D. Cal.)3:26-cv-03500
Executive Action: Clean Air Act Enforcement
Case Summary: The State of California, joined by 13 other state and local government Plaintiffs and the California Air Resources Board,suedEnvironmental Protection Agency (EPA) Administrator Lee Zeldin for his alleged failure to implement a 2024 Clean Air Act Rule related to soot. Plaintiffs allege the EPA has not fulfilled its mandatory duty to designate all areas of the country as attainment or nonattainment under the 2024 National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) for fine particulate matter. Plaintiffs seek declaratory relief and an injunction ordering Zeldin to promulgate designations for all areas of the country within 150 days of a court order.
